title: [Agent UX] Non-markdown file UX: directory listing inclusion + open-in-new-tab for HTML/PDF/images
description:
# Two related gaps

## 1. Links to .html / .pdf / image files should open in new tab

Today when a wiki page contains a link to \`my-report.html\` or \`diagram.svg\`, clicking it replaces the current wiki tab. For rendered-HTML artifacts (mockups, embedded visualizations, standalone reports) the user almost always wants them in a new tab so the wiki context is preserved.

### Proposal

- In the renderer, detect link targets with non-markdown extensions (.html, .pdf, .svg, .png, .jpg, .zip, etc.) and automatically add \`target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer\"\`.
- Alternative / companion: surface a small \"↗ open in new tab\" affordance next to non-markdown file entries in directory listings.
- Out-of-scope for this ticket: fixing Content-Disposition:attachment for .html files served from wikihub origin — that's wikihub-8vk (owner-opt-in rendering).

## 2. Directory listing should include arbitrary non-markdown files as first-class entries

Current folder view (\`app/routes/wiki.py:_folder_listing\` around line 296, rendered by \`folder.html\`) needs to be re-examined — it's unclear whether non-markdown files appear at all, and even if they do, whether they get sensible icons/affordances.

Jacob's use case: published \`docs/mockups/\` with 3 HTML files and 1 markdown index. The HTML files don't show up in a convenient directory browse on the wiki.

### Proposal

- Folder view lists ALL files, not just .md, grouped or sorted by type
- Per-extension icon (📄 for docs, 🖼️ for images, 🌐 for HTML, 📦 for zip, etc.)
- Non-markdown files get: file size, mtime, quick-action (\"↗ open in new tab\", \"⬇ download raw\", depending on Content-Type policy — wikihub-8vk decides that)
- Wikilinks that resolve to non-markdown files render correctly (right now unclear if they do)

### Design question

How does this interact with the \"wiki feels like a markdown encyclopedia\" aesthetic? Options:
- A) Non-md files always visible, mixed chronologically with .md pages
- B) Non-md files tucked under a \"Files\" section at the bottom of each folder view
- C) Per-wiki setting: \"show non-markdown files: inline / separate / hidden\"

Leaning (B) as default with (C) as override — keeps the default view reading like a wiki, not a file browser, but makes assets discoverable.

## Done when

- [ ] Renderer auto-adds target=_blank to links whose targets have non-markdown extensions
- [ ] \`folder.html\` template shows non-md files as first-class entries with appropriate icons + actions
- [ ] \`/agents\` page documents both behaviors
- [ ] Tested with \`jacobcole/picortex/docs/mockups/\` — verify HTML mockup links open in new tab and show up in the directory listing

## Related

- wikihub-8vk (serve-html-with-correct-content-type; owner opt-in rendering)
- Touches: app/renderer.py, app/routes/wiki.py (_folder_listing), templates/folder.html

title: Per-person sharing: Share UI on page view (button + modal + panel)
close_reason: Share button, modal, and shared-with panel in reader.html

title: Onboarding: import a public GitHub repo into an LLMWiki
description: Add a homepage / onboarding flow option where a user can paste a public GitHub repository URL and have WikiHub ingest it into a readable LLMWiki. Scope: accept repo URL in onboarding, clone/fetch public repo contents, generate an initial nice-to-read wiki structure from the code/docs, and land the user in the generated wiki. This is a key acquisition path because many users already have knowledge encoded in public repos and should be able to see WikiHub value immediately without manual page creation.

title: Volume backup strategy for git repos (AWS Lightsail)
description: Persistent volume at /data/repos holds all bare git repos. If Railway recycles or loses the volume, all wiki content is gone (DB has metadata but git content is lost). Need automated backup — e.g. periodic tar to S3/R2, or git bundle push to object storage.

title: CLI: gh-style multi-account auth (login/switch/status/list)
description:
The CLI already supports named profiles via `--profile NAME` (see cli/wikihub_cli/__main__.py:22-90), but the UX is AWS-shaped: no active-profile concept, no way to list profiles without cat'ing ~/.wikihub/credentials.json, and `wikihub login` silently overwrites 'default' if you forget --profile.

Adopt the gh CLI model so users can sign in with multiple accounts without explicitly logging out:

- `wikihub auth login` — adds a new account (prompts for profile name if one exists, or auto-names)
- `wikihub auth switch <profile>` — sets the active profile (stored in credentials.json as e.g. `_active: "work"`)
- `wikihub auth status` — lists all profiles, marks active one, shows server + username for each
- `wikihub auth logout [profile]` — removes one (default: active)
- Existing `wikihub login/logout/whoami` keep working (aliases or delegate) for back-compat
- `get_client()` reads active profile when --profile not passed and no env var set

Keep existing credentials.json format (just add a `_active` key at top level). Env vars still override everything.

Out of scope: OS keychain storage, SSO, org switching.

title: Migrate wikihub from AWS Lightsail to GCP (larger instance)
description:
Replace the saturating Lightsail VM with a properly-sized GCP Compute Engine instance.

## Why now
- Lightsail 54.145.123.7 is 2 vCPU / 1 GB. Sustained load avg 2.3+ even after the 2026-04-25 bot-throttle fix.
- Memory: 911 MB total, 725 MB used + 162 MB swap = constant pressure.
- We just hardened the box with hotfixes that aren't yet in repo (wikihub-9ntv) — good time to roll cleanly onto a fresh box that can be the new source-of-truth deployment.

## Source state to migrate
| What | Size / count |
|---|---|
| Postgres DB `wikihub` | **77 MB** (51 users, 131 wikis, 6062 pages) |
| Bare git repos (`/opt/wikihub-app/repos`) | **416 MB**, 254 repos |
| Whole app dir (`/opt/wikihub-app`) | 609 MB |
| .env | 20 lines, 15 secrets |
| TLS cert | self-signed wildcard `*.wikihub.md`, valid until 2036-04-17 |
| Services | systemd `wikihub.service` (gunicorn :5100), `wikihub-mcp.service`, postgres 16, nginx |
| Static welcome page | `/var/www/wikihub-static/welcome.html` |
| Nginx custom configs (NOT in repo — see wikihub-9ntv) | `/etc/nginx/conf.d/wikihub-{bot,ai}-ratelimit.conf`, `/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/wikihub`, `/etc/nginx/backups/` |
| App-modified templates (NOT in repo) | `_footer.html` (Twitter + feedback links) |

Total transfer: ~700 MB. Migration window <30 min wall-clock if scripted.

## Target state
- **GCP project:** TBD — Jacob to choose:
  1. Create new `wikihub-prod` project (cleanest, separate billing)
  2. Reuse `ideaflow-g-api` / `ideaflow-mew-slack-gmail` / `thoughtstream-493415` (fewer new resources to manage)
  3. Reuse `cortex-group-ws-dev` (where `cortex-gw-dev` already runs) — but mixing prod + dev not ideal
- **Compute Engine VM:** Recommend `e2-medium` (2 vCPU / 4 GB / ~$25/mo). Alternative `e2-standard-2` (2 vCPU / 8 GB / ~$50/mo) for runway. Skip e2-small — only marginal upgrade vs current.
- **Region/zone:** `us-east1-b` (closer to current AWS Virginia, minimizes cutover RTT). Alternative `us-west1-b` to colocate with cortex-gw-dev.
- **Disk:** 30 GB Balanced PD-SSD (~$3/mo). Current usage 6 GB.
- **Static external IP** for DNS stability across reboots.
- **OS:** Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (matches current).
- **Firewall:** ingress 80, 443, 22 (SSH from Jacob's IPs only). Internal-only Postgres.

## Migration plan

### Phase 1 — provision (no traffic impact)
- [ ] Decision: project + instance size
- [ ] `gcloud compute instances create wikihub-prod --machine-type=e2-medium --zone=us-east1-b --image-family=ubuntu-2404-lts --image-project=ubuntu-os-cloud --boot-disk-size=30GB --boot-disk-type=pd-balanced`
- [ ] Reserve static IP, attach
- [ ] Open firewall rules (tcp:80, tcp:443; ssh limited)
- [ ] Provisioning script: install postgres-16, nginx, python3.12, build-essential, libpq-dev, certbot (optional)

### Phase 2 — replicate (no traffic impact)
- [ ] Clone wikihub repo to `/opt/wikihub-app`
- [ ] Create `.venv`, `pip install -r requirements.txt`
- [ ] `pg_dump -d wikihub -Fc` from old → `pg_restore` to new
- [ ] `tar czf - /opt/wikihub-app/repos | ssh new 'tar xzf - -C /'` (rsync also fine)
- [ ] scp `.env`, `/opt/wikihub-app/claude-config/`, TLS certs (`/etc/nginx/ssl/wikihub.md.{crt,key}`)
- [ ] Copy custom nginx configs (sites-enabled/wikihub, conf.d/wikihub-{bot,ai}-ratelimit.conf, /var/www/wikihub-static)
- [ ] Copy hotfixed templates (`_footer.html` with twitter + feedback links)
- [ ] Install systemd units for `wikihub.service` and `wikihub-mcp.service`
- [ ] Smoke test: `curl -H 'Host: wikihub.md' http://NEW_IP/` returns 200

### Phase 3 — cutover (30-second window)
- [ ] Re-run pg_dump + repo rsync to capture any drift since Phase 2 (deltas should be tiny)
- [ ] Cloudflare DNS: change wikihub.md A record from 54.145.123.7 → NEW_GCP_IP (TTL was probably already low; if not, lower it 5 min in advance)
- [ ] Verify external curl
- [ ] Watch new box's logs for any errors

### Phase 4 — decom + repo hygiene
- [ ] After 24h with no rollback needed: stop the Lightsail box (do NOT delete; keep as cold backup for 7 days)
- [ ] Solve wikihub-9ntv: commit ALL the on-box hotfixes back into the wikihub repo (nginx configs, welcome.html, footer, ratelimit conf) so the new box's state IS the repo
- [ ] Update `AGENTS.md` deploy section: new SSH command, new IP
- [ ] Update `~/code/PROJECTS.md` and `~/memory/wikihub.md` with new infra info
- [ ] Delete Lightsail instance after 7 days

## Risk register
- **DNS propagation lag** — Cloudflare's TTL is fast; usually <60s. Pre-lower TTL 30 min before cutover.
- **Postgres version mismatch** — both are 16, fine.
- **wikihub-mcp.service** — verify port (probably 5101 or similar) doesn't collide on new box.
- **Anthropic creds in /opt/wikihub-app/claude-config/** — must scp these or Curator endpoint breaks.
- **Cloudflare-only origin certs** — the wildcard cert is self-signed for use with Cloudflare Full SSL Strict. New box needs the same cert OR we re-issue from Cloudflare's origin CA.
- **Active git pushes during migration window** — unlikely (no remote, post-receive triggers only) but worth pausing.

## Cost
- Lightsail: $10/mo (current) → GCP e2-medium: \~$25/mo (\~+$15/mo for ~2x RAM and proper headroom). e2-standard-2 \~$50/mo if we want serious runway.

## Acceptance
- [ ] wikihub.md resolves to GCP IP
- [ ] `/api/v1/agent/chat` returns 401 (auth-required, not 5xx)
- [ ] Curator panel works for logged-in users
- [ ] Page load <500ms p50 under normal traffic
- [ ] Load avg <1.0 sustained
- [ ] Lightsail instance stopped (not deleted yet)

## Decisions needed from Jacob
1. **GCP project:** new `wikihub-prod`, or reuse existing? (default: new)
2. **Instance size:** e2-medium or e2-standard-2? (default: e2-medium)
3. **Region:** us-east1-b or us-west1-b? (default: us-east1-b — closer to current)
4. **Cutover timing:** during low-traffic window (e.g. 3am ET) or now?

## Related tickets
- wikihub-9ntv (drift) — convert prod hotfixes back to repo. Should be done as part of Phase 4.
- (the other Lightsail-undersized ticket I created) — supersede / close as resolved by this ticket once shipped.
notes:
Post-cutover punchlist resolved 2026-04-25 ~04:40 ET:

1. ✅ wikihub-mcp.service: installed Node 20, npm ci + tsc, deployed sites-enabled/wikihub-mcp config, nginx -t + reload. Verified externally at https://mcp.wikihub.md/mcp (initialize → 200 with WikiHub server card).
2. ✅ Docs updated: ~/memory/wikihub.md (memory repo, pushed); PROJECTS.md on M3 (not in any repo, in-place); AGENTS.md on drift-commit-2026-04-25 branch.
3. ✅ Hotfix drift commit: see wikihub-9ntv (closed). Branch drift-commit-2026-04-25 has all on-box changes, ready for PR.
4. ✅ GCP firewall: wikihub-ssh source restricted from 0.0.0.0/0 to <my-mac-mini-IP>/32 + 35.235.240.0/20 (GCP IAP). default-allow-ssh restricted to GCP IAP only. RDP rule still 0.0.0.0/0 (default GCP, not used by wikihub — left alone).

Remaining (this ticket):
- After 24h with no rollback: stop+terminate Lightsail wikihub-dev (54.145.123.7).
- After 7 days: delete the Lightsail instance entirely.
- Merge drift-commit-2026-04-25 PR (needs human review since cli-installer has unrelated parallel work).
- New deploy procedure documented in AGENTS.md — works with the GCP box now.

title: Binary file handler ignores Page row visibility — only checks file ACL
description:
## Symptom

A .txt (or .pdf, .png, etc.) file uploaded via the API with `visibility: public` is correctly stored in the repo and indexed, but anonymous browser requests 404 unless a matching ACL grant exists in `.wikihub/acl`.

## Root cause

`app/routes/wiki.py` (binary-serving branch ~line 826) checks ACL via:
```python
file_vis = resolve_visibility(page_path, acl_rules)
if not is_owner and not can_read(page_path, acl_rules, user_name, file_vis):
    abort(404)
```

This uses **file-path ACL only**. The markdown branch checks the **Page row's visibility** as well. Page row visibility (set explicitly by `visibility: public` in the API call) is ignored for non-md files.

## Reproduction (verified)

1. Create wiki with default ACL (`* private`)
2. Upload a .txt: `POST /api/v1/wikis/USER/SLUG/pages {path: 'foo.txt', visibility: 'public'}` — succeeds
3. Anonymous browser GET `/@USER/SLUG/foo.txt` — 404
4. Add `foo.txt public` to `.wikihub/acl` — now 200

## Fix

In the binary handler, also accept the request if a matching Page row exists with public/public-edit visibility. Mirror the check in the markdown path.

## Related

- Closes loop with wikihub-uevw (this work). The .txt rendering fix landed but exposed this preexisting ACL inconsistency.
- Worked around for vision-convo wiki by adding `raw/** public` and `wiki/** public` ACL lines.

## Files

- `app/routes/wiki.py` ~826 (binary serving branch)
- `app/git_sync.py` `update_mirror_page` may also need updating to mirror non-md files based on Page row visibility

title: Redesign clone URL UI to match GitHub's pattern
description:
## Problem

The current clone UI is a plain "Copy clone URL" button in a dropdown. It copies a bare URL like `https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.git`. This is unfamiliar to users who expect GitHub's clone dialog pattern, and it doesn't communicate what you're actually getting.

## Design

Replace with a GitHub-style clone popover:

1. **Tabbed protocol selector**: HTTPS (default) | SSH (future) | CLI
2. **Pre-filled command** in a monospace input box:
   - HTTPS tab: `git clone https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.git`
   - CLI tab: `curl -L https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.zip -o rsi-wiki.zip`
3. **Copy button** (clipboard icon) next to the input
4. **Info box** below: subtle note explaining what you're cloning

## The Two-Repo Problem

WikiHub has two repos per wiki:
- `<slug>.git` — authoritative (owner-only read/write)
- `<slug>-public.git` — derived mirror (everyone else, read-only)

The git backend already handles this transparently — the same URL routes to different repos based on auth:
- Owner with API key → authoritative repo (can push)
- Anyone else → public mirror (read-only, no private pages)

**This is fine.** The URL is the same either way. But the UI should communicate:
- For non-owners: "This clones the public content. Private pages are excluded."
- For owners: "This clones your full wiki including private pages. Use your API key as password."

## Info Box Copy

Non-owner view:
> "Clones public pages only. Private pages are excluded from this view."

Owner view:
> "Use your API key as the password. This includes all pages."

## No push-back problem

Unlike GitHub forks, there's no expectation of pushing back to someone else's wiki via git. The clone is read-only for non-owners (the git backend rejects pushes from non-owners). If someone wants to contribute, they fork via the API/UI, which creates a separate wiki they own. So there's no confusion about "I cloned it but can't push" — it's the expected behavior and matches GitHub's model for repos you don't have write access to.
close_reason: Implemented GitHub-style clone command UI. Info box for public/private context deferred.

title: style sidebar scrollbars: thin, hover-only, no visual clutter
description: the right panel inner scrollbar (and potentially left sidebar scrollbar) look ugly when they appear. fix: use thin scrollbar styling (scrollbar-width: thin, webkit-scrollbar 4px), only visible on hover. both sidebars should use position:sticky and only scroll independently when their content overflows the viewport. the main page scroll should be the primary scroll axis. test on admitsphere/Common-App which has a long left sidebar.

title: Curate: Twitter #resource / #curate directory — extract tweet-sourced tips
description:
Build the Twitter-sourced portion of the curation directory. Seed example: Dhruv's thread on NVIDIA offering ~80 AI models via hosted APIs for free (https://x.com/dhruvtwt_/status/2047006444701274380) — 'plugs straight into OpenClaude, OpenCode, Zed IDE, Hermes agent'.

Flow:
1. Tag tweets worth curating (hashtag, bookmark, or seed list)
2. Extraction pipeline (decided by Twitter-extraction spike ticket)
3. Normalize into list entries: tweet URL, author, claim, verified? (did we confirm the offer is real), link to the actual service
4. Publish to curation surface

Quality rule: every curated tweet claim must be verified (click through to the actual vendor page) before publishing. Twitter is noisy.
notes: Blocked by Twitter-extraction spike AND architecture-decision.

title: auto-generated table of contents from headings
description: auto-generate a table of contents from ## headings in rendered pages. use markdown-it-py TOC plugin or custom heading extraction. bonus: per-section [edit] links like Wikipedia/Farzapedia.

title: MCP server: expose wiki content for AI agent queries
description: instead of RAG with embeddings, expose wiki content via MCP server (model context protocol). any Claude Code or AI agent can connect to a wikihub MCP server and read/search wiki pages natively. the LLM does its own reasoning — no need for us to run embeddings. inspired by Farzapedia's /wiki-query (which just reads files) and gbrain's MCP layer.

title: download button: clarify wiki-level vs page-level download
description: when viewing a single page (not wiki root), the download button offers 'Download ZIP' and 'Copy clone URL' but these download the ENTIRE wiki, not just the current page. this is confusing. fix: (1) make the dropdown text explicit — e.g. 'Download wiki as ZIP' and 'Clone entire wiki'. (2) add a 'Download this page (.md)' option that downloads just the current page's markdown file. the page-level download is just a link to the .md content-negotiated URL.

title: Sidebar: show ACL-granted private pages to authorized non-owners
Editor UX audit: wikilink autocomplete and owner visibility controls
description:
Audit and fix the current editor/page-management UX gaps surfaced during the WikiHub collaboration review.

Scope:
- verify whether wikilink autocomplete exists in the web editor; if missing or broken, track the exact gap and ship a minimal usable flow
- let owners change page visibility from the reader/manage surface without forcing a full Edit round-trip
- unify visibility affordances so the owner can see and act on privacy state where they read the page

Context: as of 2026-04-22 the editor template is still a plain textarea and the reader shows the page visibility badge but does not expose an immediate per-page visibility change control for owners.

title: No rate limiting on login or signup endpoints
description: 10 rapid-fire login attempts with wrong passwords all returned 401 with no throttling. 5 rapid signup attempts all succeeded immediately. An attacker could brute-force passwords or spam-create accounts. Note: API write endpoints have rate limiting (wikihub-un7), but auth endpoints do not.
close_reason: added IP-based rate limiting on login: 20 attempts per 5 minutes per IP, matching existing signup rate limiter pattern

title: RSS feed for wiki changes
description: Atom/RSS feed at /@username/wiki.atom showing recent changes. Enables watch-style workflows without polling. Each commit becomes a feed entry with title=commit message, content=diff summary, link=commit URL. Also consider a global /feed.atom for all public changes (explore-level).

title: Sanity-check rate limits + logging on all AI Curator features
description:
Add defensive rate limits and full request logging to every Curator / AI endpoint before this gets abused. Backend endpoint /api/v1/agent/chat currently has no per-user rate limit, no daily/monthly cap, no spend cap. One logged-in user with a compromised account or a dumb script could run up an Anthropic bill or DoS the server.

## Endpoints in scope
- POST /api/v1/agent/chat (Curator conversational agent — SSE, calls Anthropic, can spawn git clones + writes)
- POST /api/v1/roadmap/create-issues (natural-language → beads issues, also calls Anthropic)
- POST /api/v1/agent/claude-auth/* (login/logout for per-user Claude creds)
- Any future Curator-adjacent endpoint

## Limits to add (all configurable via env vars with conservative defaults)

### Per-user
- CURATOR_RATE_PER_MIN (default 6) — max requests / minute / user
- CURATOR_RATE_PER_HOUR (default 60)
- CURATOR_RATE_PER_DAY (default 200)
- CURATOR_RATE_PER_MONTH (default 2000)

### Per-IP (prevents single-source abuse even across accounts)
- CURATOR_IP_RATE_PER_MIN (default 10)
- CURATOR_IP_RATE_PER_HOUR (default 100)
- CURATOR_IP_RATE_PER_DAY (default 300)

### Token / cost budgets (cheap insurance against runaway loops)
- CURATOR_MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS (default 10 — already enforced)
- CURATOR_MAX_TOKENS_PER_REQUEST (default 4096 — already enforced)
- CURATOR_MAX_TOKENS_PER_USER_PER_DAY (default 500_000)
- CURATOR_MAX_TOKENS_PER_USER_PER_MONTH (default 5_000_000)
- Server-wide: CURATOR_MAX_TOKENS_PER_DAY (default 5_000_000)

### Session / concurrency
- CURATOR_MAX_CONCURRENT_SESSIONS_PER_USER (default 2)
- CURATOR_SESSION_TTL_SECONDS (default 1800 — already 30min)

## Logging — log every AI request

For each request, write a row to a new 'curator_request_log' table:
- timestamp
- user_id (nullable for claude-auth endpoints)
- api_key_id (nullable)
- endpoint path
- ip (from X-Forwarded-For, fall back to remote_addr)
- user_agent
- session/conversation_id
- message_length (chars)
- tool_calls_count (after response)
- tokens_in, tokens_out (from Anthropic response usage)
- model used
- duration_ms
- status_code
- stop_reason
- error (if any)

Retain 90 days minimum. Write to DB synchronously on request completion (SSE 'done' event).

## Response when limits hit
- Return 429 with JSON: {error, message, retry_after, limit_type: 'per_min'|'per_hour'|'per_day'|'per_month'|'tokens', current, limit}
- Set Retry-After header
- Frontend panel should surface this clearly (currently shows generic error)

## Alerting
- Log WARN when any user hits 80% of their monthly token budget
- Log ERROR when any user is rate-limited 3+ times in one minute
- Daily summary cron: 'top 10 AI consumers yesterday'

## Files to touch
- NEW: app/curator_limits.py (decorators + counters, Postgres-backed sliding windows)
- NEW: app/models.py — CuratorRequestLog model
- MODIFY: app/routes/agent_chat.py — wrap endpoints with @curator_limits
- MODIFY: config.py — all the env vars above
- MODIFY: app/templates/reader.html — richer 429 handling in sendCuratorMessage
- NEW: scripts/curator_daily_report.py — prints top consumers, flagged accounts

## Acceptance
- [ ] Rate limits enforced per-user and per-IP across minute/hour/day/month windows
- [ ] Every request row in curator_request_log with IP + UA + tokens + duration
- [ ] 429 responses carry retry_after and limit_type
- [ ] Frontend surfaces 429 with a human message
- [ ] Daily summary script works end-to-end
- [ ] Tested with a tight limit (e.g. CURATOR_RATE_PER_MIN=2) to verify the cutoff fires

## Why P1 not P0
The feature only just went live. No confirmed abuse yet. But every day it's live without these limits is risk; ship within a week.

title: Editor and new-page experience is broken — needs full QA pass
description:
The entire editing-a-page-manually and creating-a-new-page web experience is borked. This is P0 — the editor is a core user flow.

Needs investigation:
- Can you create a new page from the web UI? Does the form work?
- Can you edit an existing page? Does the editor load? Does save work?
- Is the Milkdown editor bundled and loading correctly?
- Are there JS errors in the console?
- Does the raw textarea fallback work if Milkdown isn't loaded?
- Does visibility setting work when creating/editing?
- Does the breadcrumb/navigation work after save?

This blocks dogfooding — if you can't manually edit pages, the site is API-only.

title: Improve empty profile page text and populate example accounts
description: The text 'This person has not published a personal wiki landing page yet' is unhelpful. Replace with something minimal or hide entirely. Also populate jacobcole and other accounts with real content so the site doesn't look empty.
close_reason: Fixed empty profile text - only shows to owner now. Populated jacobcole profile.

title: Rename wiki (change slug)
description: No way to rename a wiki (change its slug). Need: API endpoint to change slug, rename git repo dirs (both auth and public mirror), update all DB references (wiki.slug, URLs). Should also set up a redirect from old slug to new slug so existing links don't break.

title: Folder share UI: share button/modal on folder.html (backend already works)
description:
Backend folder-sharing is fully implemented and tested:
- `POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/share` accepts `pattern: "research/*"` to share a whole folder (see `test_folder_level_sharing` in tests/test_e2e.py)
- ACL grant system uses fnmatch globs; `resolve_grants` walks .wikihub/acl rules (`app/acl.py:93`)
- wikihub-ebi (closed) shipped the generalized share/unshare/list-grants API with wiki/folder/page patterns

But the **UI never had folder-level share discovery**. Verified: `folder.html` has zero "share" references on every branch (local `main`, `origin/main`). The share button/modal from wikihub-0bm is on `reader.html` only (on origin/main — the Mac Mini-local `main` lost it in a divergence, see separate note).

## Fix

Add a "Share" button to the folder page-actions bar (owner-only initially — non-owner share is a separate policy question). Clicking opens a modal pre-populated with the folder path as the pattern:

```
Share folder: research/
  Pattern: [research/*]    (editable — user can tighten to research/subfolder/* or widen to research/**)
  Share with: [@username or email  ]
  Role:        ( ) Read   ( ) Edit
  [ Share ]  [ Cancel ]
```

Below the button, a "Shared with" list summarizes current folder-level grants (rows showing principal + role + revoke button). Same visual language as the reader.html share panel.

## Pattern vocabulary UX

The backend accepts any fnmatch pattern. Don't expose glob syntax in the form for v1 — default to `<folder>/*` (immediate children only) with a "Include all subfolders" checkbox that flips to `<folder>/**`. Power users can hand-edit.

## Implementation notes

- Mostly a template-only addition; backend is done
- Reuse the JS for the reader.html share modal if possible
- Write test: `test_folder_share_ui` that owner-GETs the folder page and sees a Share button; anonymous/non-owner doesn't
- Mind the reader.html/folder.html recurring fragility (wikihub-bnj, wikihub-58c) — diff carefully against last-known-good

## Dependencies
- Clarify first: is the current `origin/main` `reader.html` share modal JS reusable? (Yes per grep — 82 share refs there)
- Pairs with wikihub-1 (sidebar: show ACL-granted pages to authorized non-owners) — same mental model

## Out of scope
- Group grants (separate ticket, filed alongside this)
- Key-as-principal (see two-axis note in wikihub-gzlt)

title: Verify public-edit authoring and merge flow end to end
description:
Validate two user-critical collaboration flows end to end:

1. Publicly editable wikis/pages can actually be edited by non-owners and anonymous/public users where intended.
2. The merge flow works correctly from proposal/diff through merge application.

This is a verification task, not a product spec. It should capture:
- exact flows tested
- environments tested (local/prod if possible)
- failures found
- follow-up bug tickets if either flow is broken

Requested explicitly by the user while preparing the IdeaFlow team wiki import.
notes: Production public-edit verification passed on 2026-04-22 using @jacobcole/public-edit-verification/wiki/open: anonymous API edit succeeded and unauthenticated web UI showed the Edit button. Merge-flow verification is blocked by the apparent absence of an implemented merge/proposal surface in the current repo; follow-up ticket created.

title: [Agent UX] API introspection: GET /api/v1/me/capabilities
description:
# Observed

After auth with wh_*, /api/v1/accounts/me returns {user_id, username, display_name, created_at} — correct but incomplete for agents figuring out what they can do.

What agents need (discoverable from the API, not from /agents HTML):
- Which wikis I own / can write to (with slugs)
- Current rate-limit budgets: writes_per_minute remaining, next reset time
- Feature flags: can I use bulk endpoints? idempotency keys? git push?
- Quota ceilings: max_wikis_per_user, max_pages_per_wiki, max_page_size

# Proposal

\`GET /api/v1/me/capabilities\`

\`\`\`json
{
  \"user\": {\"id\": 4, \"username\": \"jacobcole\"},
  \"wikis\": {\"owned\": [\"picortex\", \"...\"]},
  \"rate_limits\": {
    \"writes\": {\"limit\": 10, \"window_seconds\": 60, \"remaining\": 3, \"reset_at\": \"2026-04-23T20:15:00Z\"}
  },
  \"quotas\": {\"max_wikis\": 50, \"max_page_size_bytes\": 5242880},
  \"features\": {
    \"bulk_pages\": false,
    \"idempotency_keys\": false,
    \"git_push\": true,
    \"content_negotiation\": true
  },
  \"api_version\": \"v1\",
  \"documented_at\": \"https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/agents\"
}
\`\`\`

Agents call this on startup to configure themselves correctly — paginate writes if bulk is off, use idempotency-keys if the feature flag is on, etc. Also great for debugging (\"why am I hitting 429s? oh, my budget is 2 remaining\").

# Related

- wikihub-y3u (rate-limit changes) would update this response
- Feature flags should also live in /llms.txt for LLM-readable discovery

# Done when

- [ ] GET /api/v1/me/capabilities documented + live
- [ ] Returns live rate-limit budget (not just the cap — the remaining)
- [ ] Linked from /agents as \"start here\"
close_reason: Closed

title: Roadmap quick-capture: create issues from natural language via Claude agent

title: Return 404 instead of 403 for unauthorized private pages to prevent enumeration
File support roadmap: from image embeds to Google Drive mode
description:
Progressive file support beyond markdown-only browsing. The spec already covers storing binaries in git repos and rendering Obsidian ![[image.png]] embeds, but there's no serving route (images 404) and the sidebar/UI is markdown-only.

**Progressive stages (each unlocks the next):**

Stage 0 — Serve binaries from git repo (bug fix, not feature)
- Route to serve raw files from authoritative/public repo via git cat-file
- Content-type detection, cache headers
- Prerequisite for ![[image.png]] embeds to actually render

Stage 1 — Image attachments (core LLMWiki need)
- Upload images alongside markdown pages
- Inline image preview in editor
- Image gallery or attachment panel per page
- LLM wikis need diagrams, charts, screenshots — this is table stakes

Stage 2 — File browser
- Show all files in sidebar/tree, not just .md
- File type icons
- Non-markdown files visible but clearly secondary to wiki pages

Stage 3 — Non-markdown preview
- Inline PDF viewer, CSV table render, image lightbox
- Reference material browsable alongside wiki pages

Stage 4 — Google Drive mode (future, maybe separate product)
- Drag-drop any file type, bulk upload
- Full-text search across non-markdown files
- Folder-first browsing (vs page-first)
- This diverges from LLMWiki core mission — needs careful product thinking

**Key question:** How far past Stage 1 should we go before it stops being an LLMWiki and starts being a document hosting platform? The Karpathy pattern is markdown knowledge bases maintained by LLMs — images support that, but a Google Drive clone doesn't.

**ListHub contrast:** ListHub is deliberately text-only (flat markdown items, no binary support). WikiHub already went further architecturally (git repos can hold anything), but the serving/browsing layer is markdown-only. The gap is between what git stores and what the UI shows.

**Spec reference:** Binary storage is already specced (wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md, Data Architecture section). This epic covers the serving, browsing, and UX layers on top.

title: Wikipedia-style URLs: replace %20 with underscores
description:
## Context

WikiHub URLs currently use %20 for spaces in page names, making them ugly and hard to share:
`/@jacobcole/wiki/Tools%20%26%20Implementations`

## Research: How other platforms handle spaces

| Platform | Space handling | Example |
|----------|--------------|---------|
| **Wikipedia** | Underscores (`_`) | `/wiki/Machine_learning` |
| **Obsidian Publish** | Plus signs (`+`) | `/garden/My+Great+Note` |
| **GitHub wikis** | Hyphens (`-`) | `/wiki/My-Page` |
| **Most web apps** | `%20` (default) | `/page/My%20Page` |

## Obsidian Publish details
- $8-10/mo per site
- Plus signs for spaces, case-sensitive, no auto-slugification
- Supports `permalink` frontmatter override for custom slugs
- Password protection is all-or-nothing (no per-page ACL)
- No API, no git backing, no social features (fork/star)

## Recommendation: Wikipedia-style underscores

**Why underscores win:**
- Most readable: `The_Pattern` vs `The%20Pattern` vs `The+Pattern`
- Reversible: underscore ↔ space is lossless (filenames rarely have literal underscores)
- Battle-tested: Wikipedia has done this for 25 years, billions of URLs
- Obsidian-compatible: file stays `The Pattern.md` on disk, only URL changes
- Accept all forms: `The_Pattern`, `The%20Pattern`, and `The Pattern` should all resolve

**Implementation:**
- URL generation: `replace(" ", "_")` in all `_page_url()` and template helpers
- URL resolution: `replace("_", " ")` when looking up filenames
- Redirect: `%20` URLs → canonical underscore URLs (301)
- Edge case: files with literal underscores in names — rare, but need a policy (escape? accept ambiguity?)

**Bonus: permalink frontmatter support**
- Steal Obsidian Publish's `permalink` frontmatter field
- Let users set custom URL slugs per-page
- Useful for stable URLs that survive renames

## Ask Harrison
- Does underscore feel right, or would he prefer a different convention?
- Should we also lowercase URLs (case-insensitive matching)?
- How to handle the `&` in names like "Tools & Implementations" — underscore only handles spaces
close_reason: Implemented Wikipedia-style URLs: spaces in page names are now displayed as underscores in URLs (e.g., My_Great_Page instead of My%20Great%20Page). Added url_utils.py for path conversion, updated all route handlers to normalize underscore→space on lookup, added 301 redirects from %20 URLs to canonical underscore URLs, and updated all templates to use the page_url filter.

IMPORTANT: this needs extensive testing on the dev server (localhost:5100) before pushing to production. test at multiple viewport widths, test on pages with no TOC (< 3 headings), test with and without backlinks, test mobile collapse behavior. do NOT push to prod without agent-browser verification at each breakpoint.

title: Curator Agent: inline AI sidebar for wiki curation and navigation
description:
## Vision

An inline AI sidebar on every wiki page — inspired by Neal Stephenson's Librarian (Snow Crash) and the Curator (Ready Player One). A conversational agent that knows who you are, what page you're on, and can take actions on wikis you have access to.

### Simplest use case
You're browsing someone's idea list. You say "promote this to the featured ideas list." The agent reads the current page, writes to the featured wiki (which is public-edit), done.

### Key references
- **Snow Crash Librarian** — already in WikiHub philosophy docs
- **Ready Player One Curator** — guides users through a vast archive
- **Noos project** — Jacob's existing coding-agent-backed knowledge graph (uses Claude Code under the hood)

---

## Architecture: Coding Agent with Filesystem Access (Community Standard)

### Research finding (2026-04-10)

The LLM Wiki ecosystem overwhelmingly uses **coding agents with direct filesystem access**, not API-mediated tool use:

| Pattern | Examples | Share |
|---------|----------|-------|
| **Coding agent (filesystem)** | SamurAIGPT (1.5k★), kfchou, Astro-Han, MehmetGoekce | ~80% |
| **MCP server (API-mediated)** | lucasastorian, sage-wiki | ~15% |
| **Desktop app** | nashsu | ~5% |

Karpathy's gist is literally a CLAUDE.md template — the agent opens a directory, reads the schema, and reads/writes markdown files directly. No API, no database, no custom tools.

### Why this matters for WikiHub

WikiHub's storage is already git repos of markdown files. The Curator doesn't need a custom tool-use layer — it can work on the files directly:

1. **Clone wiki repo** to a temp working directory
2. **Run coding agent** (Claude API with bash+file tools, or Claude Code subprocess) pointed at that directory
3. **Agent operates on markdown files natively** — the exact pattern the community uses
4. **Push changes back** via git push or WikiHub API
5. **Post-receive hook** syncs changes to DB (already exists)

This is simpler AND more capable than building custom API tools, because:
- Agent can do anything with the files (not limited to predefined tools)
- Matches how the entire community already works with LLM wikis
- CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md schema file is the standard discovery mechanism
- WikiHub's per-wiki AGENTS.md already exists and serves this purpose

### Execution model: Claude API with code execution tools

Use the Claude API (not a Claude Code subprocess) with these tools:
- `bash` — run shell commands (scoped to the temp working dir)
- `read_file` — read files in the working dir
- `write_file` — write files in the working dir
- `edit_file` — patch files

This gives coding-agent capabilities without process management complexity. The server:
1. Creates a temp dir per session
2. Clones the relevant wiki repo(s) into it
3. Runs Claude API calls with file/bash tools scoped to that dir
4. On agent actions that modify files, commits and pushes back to WikiHub
5. Cleans up temp dir when session ends

### Sandboxing (MVP vs Production)

| Approach | Isolation | Complexity | When |
|----------|-----------|------------|------|
| **Temp dir + chdir** | Process-level | Low | MVP — sufficient for trusted users |
| **Unix user per session** | User-level | Medium | v1.1 — if multi-tenant |
| **Docker per session** | Container-level | High | v2 — if running untrusted code |

For MVP, a temp directory with the agent's bash tool restricted to that path is adequate. The agent can only read/write files it was given access to.

### Multi-wiki operations

The "promote to featured" use case requires access to TWO wikis (source + destination). The temp dir can contain multiple cloned repos:

```
/tmp/curator-session-abc/
  jacobcole/ideas/          ← cloned from /@jacobcole/ideas
  wikihub/wikihub/          ← cloned from /@wikihub/wikihub (public-edit)
```

The agent's CLAUDE.md tells it the layout and what it can do.

---

## Agent Context (injected as system prompt / CLAUDE.md)

```
You are the Curator, WikiHub's AI librarian.

## Current session
- User: @{username}
- Page: /@{owner}/{wiki}/{page}
- Permissions: {read/write/admin}

## Working directory layout
- {owner}/{wiki}/          ← the wiki you're viewing
- wikihub/wikihub/         ← the main WikiHub wiki (public-edit)
- {other cloned wikis as needed}

## How to make changes
Edit files directly. Changes are auto-committed and pushed to WikiHub.
Follow the wiki's schema.md for structure rules.

## Available wikis for this user
{list of wikis the user can read/write}
```

---

## MVP Scope

### Backend
- `POST /api/v1/agent/chat` — SSE endpoint, requires auth
  - Input: `{message, conversation_id?, page_context?}`
  - Output: SSE stream of agent text + tool call results
  - Creates temp dir, clones relevant repos, runs Claude API with file tools
- Session management: temp dir per conversation, cleanup on timeout
- Git integration: auto-commit + push on file writes

### Frontend
- Slide-out panel on right side of page (does not replace left nav sidebar)
- Toggle button in page actions area (or floating button)
- Chat input at bottom, messages scroll up
- Tool calls shown inline (collapsed by default, expandable)
- "Curator is editing..." indicator when writing
- Desktop only for MVP; hidden on mobile

### System prompt
- Injected with user identity, current page, wiki structure
- References the wiki's own AGENTS.md/schema.md for rules
- Tells agent to work directly with files in the temp dir

---

## Chrome Extension (v2)

A personal Curator that works on any website:
- Sidebar Chrome extension (connects to Cortex project)
- On WikiHub pages: uses the built-in Curator API
- On other sites: personal agent mode (save to your wiki, annotate)
- If built-in sidebar AND extension both present:
  - Extension detects WikiHub, defers to built-in (or takes over — user pref)

---

## Immediate Next Steps
1. Make wikihub/wikihub wiki public-edit
2. Add /api/v1/agent/chat SSE endpoint
3. Implement temp-dir clone + file tools for Claude API
4. Build frontend slide-out panel
5. Test: "promote this idea to featured" end-to-end

## Files involved
- NEW: app/routes/agent_chat.py — SSE chat endpoint + session management
- NEW: app/agent_tools.py — bash/read/write/edit tool definitions for Claude API
- NEW: app/templates/_curator_panel.html — sidebar UI
- MODIFY: app/templates/reader.html — add Curator toggle + panel
- MODIFY: app/__init__.py — register blueprint
- MODIFY: requirements.txt — add anthropic SDK

## Dependencies
- Anthropic API key in server .env
- Claude API with tool use (streaming)
- Git installed on server (already is)

title: Admin toggle to enable/disable Curator chat
description:
Add an admin setting to enable/disable the Curator chat feature, controllable both programmatically and via the admin UI.

## Programmatic
- Env var: CURATOR_ENABLED=true/false (default: false)
- Also stored in DB (admin_settings table or similar) so it can be toggled without restart
- DB setting overrides env var if set

## Admin UI
- Add toggle on the settings/admin page
- Simple on/off switch with label "Curator Agent"
- When off: chat panel doesn't render, /api/v1/agent/chat returns 503
- When on: panel renders on reader pages for authenticated users

## Implementation
- Add CURATOR_ENABLED to app config (read from env + DB)
- Add admin API endpoint: POST /api/v1/admin/settings {curator_enabled: bool}
- Add toggle to admin panel UI (settings.html or admin page)
- Guard the chat endpoint: return 503 if disabled
- Guard the frontend: only render panel HTML if enabled
- Guard by auth: only show to logged-in users (not anonymous visitors)

DB-backed toggle + admin-UI switch deferred (env var is sufficient for MVP; admin panel infrastructure not yet built).

title: [Agent UX] Bulk page upload endpoint OR git-push path
description:
# Observed

Importing 31 picortex planning docs to jacobcole/picortex required 31 sequential POSTs paced at 7s each (≈4 min wall time) because of the 10/min rate limit + no bulk path. Git-push would have taken 2 seconds.

# Options

## Option A: \`POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/pages/bulk\`

```json
{
  "pages": [
    {"path": "docs/a.md", "content": "...", "visibility": "public"},
    {"path": "docs/b.md", "content": "...", "visibility": "public"}
  ],
  "on_conflict": "skip|overwrite|error"
}
→ 207 Multi-Status
{
  "results": [
    {"path": "docs/a.md", "status": 201, "id": 1234},
    {"path": "docs/b.md", "status": 409, "error": "conflict"}
  ]
}
```

Same ACL checks + git commit batching (one commit per bulk call, not per page). Rate-limit counts the call as one operation, not N.

## Option B: Enable git push to the wiki's bare repo

Wikihub already hosts a bare repo per wiki at \`/@user/wiki.git\` (per README: \"Every wiki is a real git repo — clone, push, blame, bisect\"). If push is authenticated via HTTP basic with API key, agents can:

\`\`\`
git clone https://wh_KEY:@wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/picortex.git
cd picortex
# bulk-copy markdown files
git add -A && git commit -m \"...\" && git push
\`\`\`

This is the most natural bulk path and already aligns with wikihub's \"it's a git repo\" ethos. **Check whether push works today** — README implies it but I didn't test.

## Recommendation

Both. Git push is the idiomatic path; \`/pages/bulk\` is the convenience path for agents that don't want to touch git. Git push probably already works — if it does, this ticket collapses to: (a) confirm it works, (b) document it clearly on /agents, (c) still build \`/pages/bulk\` as a JSON convenience.

# Done when

- [ ] /agents page documents git-push workflow with a working example
- [ ] (if git push doesn't already work) wire up smart HTTP + API-key auth
- [ ] OR (if opting for bulk JSON endpoint) implement /pages/bulk with one-commit-per-call semantics

title: Cross-wiki wikilinks: [[/@user/wiki/page]] syntax
description: Platform-layer cross-wiki linking. Any wikilink starting with /@ resolves across wikis. No git submodules. Resolver checks target page existence across wiki_ids. Unresolved cross-wiki links render red-dashed. Documented in spec 2026-04-10.

title: Auto-convert uploads to Karpathy LLM Wiki format
description:
## Context

When someone uploads a zip of markdown files (e.g., an Obsidian vault, a folder of dated notes, Jeremy Nixon's thinking index), can we auto-detect structure and post-process it into Karpathy's LLM Wiki format?

## Karpathy format target
- `raw/` — immutable source docs
- `wiki/` — LLM-generated/maintained pages with frontmatter (title, tags, status, confidence, contradictions, open_questions)
- `index.md` — content catalog with one-line summaries per page
- `log.md` — append-only activity log
- `schema.md` / `CLAUDE.md` — conventions and domain rules
- Cross-references: symmetric wikilinks, key claims, connections sections

## What we'd need to detect
- Date patterns in filenames or content (daily notes → log.md entries)
- Existing wikilinks `[[...]]` (Obsidian native)
- Folder structure heuristics (e.g., `daily/`, `templates/`, `attachments/`)
- Frontmatter already present vs needs generating
- Which files are "source material" vs "synthesis/wiki pages"

## Open questions
- **Option vs default?** Should this be opt-in ("Convert to LLM Wiki format") or automatic on upload?
- **Destructive or additive?** Do we reorganize their files into raw/wiki/, or just add the missing pieces (index.md, log.md, frontmatter) on top of existing structure?
- **LLM-powered or rule-based?** Simple heuristics might handle 80% (date parsing, wikilink extraction, folder detection). Full Karpathy format (key claims, contradictions, confidence scores) probably needs an LLM pass.
- **When does it run?** On zip upload? On git push? As an async "maintain" action?
- **How does this relate to WikiHub's built-in maintenance vision?** (see llm-wiki-ecosystem wiki — WikiHub Opportunity page)

## Possible approach
1. Rule-based first pass: detect dates, extract wikilinks, generate index.md, infer frontmatter from filenames
2. Optional LLM pass: classify pages as entity/concept/source, generate key claims, find contradictions
3. Surface as "Organize" button on wiki page, or auto-suggest after zip upload

title: Sidebar folders collapse when navigating via sidebar link — state not persisted on page load
description:
## Problem

When clicking a sidebar link to navigate to a new page, folders that were expanded on the previous page collapse on the new page. User loses their place in the tree each time they navigate.

## Root cause

`applyFolderState()` in `app/templates/reader.html` (around line 918) only respects folder open/closed state if the user explicitly clicked the toggle chevron (which writes to `localStorage['wikihub-sidebar-folders']`).

Folders that auto-expand because they contain the active page are opened server-side via the template's `{{ 'open' if item.active else '' }}` — this state is never written to localStorage. On the next page load (in a different folder), that folder's entry in localStorage is still missing, so it falls through to "non-active folders with no localStorage state stay collapsed".

Effect: every time you click through to a page in a different branch of the tree, the previously-active folder collapses.

## Suggested fix (NOT a major refactor — ~15 lines of JS)

In `applyFolderState()`, after deciding the state for each folder, write the final state back to localStorage. That way any folder that was open on page load (whether by explicit toggle or by auto-expand for active page) persists its openness through navigation.

Edge case to preserve: if the user explicitly collapsed a folder (`state[path] === false`), keep that intent. Only persist `true` for folders that end up open.

## Files

- `app/templates/reader.html` — `applyFolderState()` near line 918
- Same pattern exists in `app/templates/folder.html` and `app/templates/profile.html` — keep consistent
- Tag as `recurring` once fixed since sidebar JS is historically fragile (see wikihub-58c, wikihub-bnj, wikihub-9c8j)

## Related

- wikihub-a6l (closed): established auto-expand-to-active behavior
- wikihub-9c8j (open): sidebar reorders items on click (different bug, similar area)

## Verification

- Open a wiki, click a page deep in folder A. Confirm folder A expands.
- Click a page deep in folder B. Folder A should remain expanded. Currently it collapses.

title: Production git push to WikiHub repo fails because pre-receive hook cannot import Flask
description:
Attempting to push to the production repo for @jacobcole/ideaflow at https://wikihub.md/@jacobcole/ideaflow.git fails before ref update.

Observed stderr from remote:
- pre-receive hook runs /opt/wikihub-app/repos/.../hooks/pre-receive
- hook imports app.acl -> app.__init__ -> from flask import Flask
- remote raises ModuleNotFoundError: No module named "flask"
- push is rejected with "pre-receive hook declined"

This breaks the documented owner git import path on production even though the Smart HTTP endpoint is reachable.

Repro:
1. Create wiki via API on production
2. Clone repo successfully over Smart HTTP
3. Commit any change
4. Push to main
5. Observe remote hook failure above

Likely fix area: production hook environment / shebang / venv activation or reduce hook dependency surface so ACL validation does not require importing the Flask app package.

title: Per-user wiki subdomains: username.wikihub.md with reserved subdomain list
description:
Per-user wiki subdomains: username.wikihub.md instead of wikihub.globalbr.ai/@user/slug.

## Product questions to resolve
1. What counts as a user's 'personal wiki'? Specific slug, default wiki, or first-created?
2. Do non-personal wikis get sub-subdomains (recipes.jacobcole.wikihub.md) or stay path-based?
3. How does @wikihub community index behave? (wikihub.wikihub.md is awkward)

## Required
- **Reserved subdomain list**: www, api, collab, staging, app, admin, dev, explore, help, docs, blog, about, status, community, wiki, wikihub (and more — architect will propose full list)
- **Wildcard DNS** (*.wikihub.md) — Cloudflare
- **Wildcard SSL cert** — Let's Encrypt DNS-01 via Cloudflare API, or Cloudflare Universal SSL
- **Nginx wildcard server block** on AWS Lightsail origin
- **Flask subdomain routing** — SERVER_NAME-based or Host header middleware
- **Username collision migration** — check existing usernames against reserved list
- **Backward compat** — /@user/slug URLs keep working (301 redirect to canonical subdomain)

## Interacts with
- wikihub-w0s4 (collab editor) — subdomain-hosted pages need to connect to wss://api.wikihub.md/collab; CORS, CSP, cookie scope concerns.
- wikihub-dz5 (community index wiki @wikihub) — conflict resolution needed

## Architect design doc
Pending — feature-dev:code-architect agent in progress.

title: Open redirect on login via unvalidated 'next' parameter
description: The login form's 'next' query parameter is followed without validation after successful login. An attacker can craft https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/auth/login?next=https://evil.com and after the user logs in, they're redirected to the attacker's site. Also accepts javascript: URLs. Fix: validate that next is a relative path on the same origin.
close_reason: validated next parameter with urlparse — rejects absolute URLs, protocol-relative URLs, javascript: URIs

title: Clickable page links on history and diff pages
close_reason: File names on history list and commit diff pages are now clickable amber links to the actual pages

title: Signup: auto-derive username from email (keep required but prefilled)
description:
Today `/auth/signup` requires a username with no hint that the site could pick one for you. For invite-flow signups the email is already prefilled; the username should be too.

## Proposed behavior

- When the email field has a value (prefilled via invite link, or typed by the user), sanitize the local-part into a candidate username and prefill the username field.
- Collision resolution: if the candidate is taken, append a counter (`tejas`, `tejas2`, `tejas3`) — same logic as `google_callback` at `app/routes/auth.py:272-280`.
- As the user edits the email, the username prefill updates live (debounced, via a small JS listener) unless the user has already edited the username field manually.
- Username stays REQUIRED — zero-input silent generation produces ugly URL slugs (`/@user_abc123`) that users will want to rename, creating username-rename support pain.

## Why not optional

WikiHub usernames are URL slugs (`wikihub.md/@tejas`). Users care about them. They should see what they're getting. Auto-generated `user_abc123` would be a worse UX than a 3-second typed username.

## Extract shared helper

The email → username sanitization logic already exists inline in `google_callback` (`app/routes/auth.py:266-280`). Extract to a helper in `app/auth_utils.py`: `suggest_username_from_email(email: str) -> str`. Use from:
- `auth.google_callback` (existing)
- `auth.signup` GET renderer (new — pass as `suggested_username` template var)
- Hypothetical future /api/v1/signup-preview endpoint (if someone wants autocomplete UX)

## Discovered via

Invite-flow user testing 2026-04-22. Currently a teammate has to think up a username on the spot when an invite link lands them on signup; prefill removes that cognitive load.

title: Beads: add reviewed/unreviewed status for human review tracking
Pages with only frontmatter title and no markdown heading show no visible title
description: When a page has a title field in YAML frontmatter but no # heading in the markdown body, the rendered page shows no visible heading at all. The browser tab title is correct but the page content has no h1. Verified on @jemoka/knowledge-base/raw/concept/kbhbayes_theorem which has frontmatter title Bayes Theorem but no heading in the rendered output. The frontmatter title should be rendered as an h1 when no explicit heading exists in the content.
close_reason: Closed

title: Signup form not detected by password managers (1Password)
description:
## Problem

The signup / create account page is not recognized by 1Password (and likely other password managers) as an account creation form. 1Password does not offer to autofill or save credentials.

## Likely cause

Password managers detect signup forms via:
- `autocomplete="new-password"` on the password field
- `autocomplete="username"` or `autocomplete="email"` on the identity field
- `<form>` element wrapping the inputs
- Input `name` and `type` attributes (`type="email"`, `type="password"`, `name="email"`)

If any of these are missing or non-standard (e.g., using `<div>` instead of `<form>`, custom input components without proper attributes, or missing `autocomplete` hints), password managers won't detect it.

## Fix

Ensure the signup form has:
1. A `<form>` element wrapping all inputs
2. `<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">` for the email field
3. `<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="new-password">` for the password field
4. `<input type="text" name="username" autocomplete="username">` for the username/display name field
5. A `<button type="submit">` (not just an onclick handler)

Test with 1Password browser extension after fixing — it should offer to save new credentials on submit.

title: Portable-self structure: distinguish always-loaded context from on-demand accessible content (Claude.md vs Claude skills, applied to WikiHub)
description:
The bootstrap-by-URL+key pattern (wikihub-4rel) gives an agent everything reachable. But not all of "everything" should be loaded into context every turn — that's wasteful and crowds the window. We need an explicit split, mirroring the Claude.md vs Claude-skills distinction, applied to WikiHub-as-context.

Research is happening in parallel: see ~/memory/research/agent-context-structures-2026-04-23.md (Claude memory model, OpenClaw, RAG patterns). Update the design here once that lands.

## The split (proposed)

**Tier 1 — Always loaded (the "soul" / always-in-context layer)**
- A small curated dump that goes into the agent's system prompt every conversation
- Token budget: ~500–2000 tokens
- Contents: who Jacob is, current focus, top-level preferences, vocabulary/identity, the index of what's available in tier 2/3
- Source: a designated wiki page, e.g. `@jacob/portable-self/soul.md` (name TBD pending research synthesis)

**Tier 2 — Skills / declarative tool definitions**
- YAML-frontmattered markdown files describing capabilities the agent can invoke
- Loaded by metadata only (name + when-to-use); body loaded only when the skill is invoked
- Source: a designated wiki folder, e.g. `@jacob/portable-self/skills/`
- Mirrors Claude Code's `~/.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md` pattern

**Tier 3 — RAG / on-demand knowledge**
- Full pages the agent retrieves only when relevant to the current turn
- Source: any wiki the scoped key has access to (per wikihub-gzlt)
- Retrieval via existing /api/v1/search + /api/v1/wikis/.../pages

## Concrete WikiHub artifacts (proposed)

A new convention: the user has a wiki named `@me/portable-self` with structure:
```
soul.md              # Tier 1 — always loaded
skills/
  schedule.md        # Tier 2 — load body on invocation
  contacts.md
  ...
notes/               # Tier 3 — RAG retrievable
journal/             # Tier 3
research/            # Tier 3
```

The `bootstrap` endpoint (wikihub-4rel) is updated to recognize this convention:
- If a wiki named `portable-self` exists for the user, its `soul.md` is included verbatim in the bootstrap response under a top-level `always_loaded` key
- The `skills/` directory listing (file names + frontmatter only, NOT bodies) is included under `skills`
- Other content is reachable via the regular API but not pre-loaded

## Why a wiki convention (not a separate config file)

- Jacob already curates wikis. No new tool to learn.
- Per-page visibility means the same `portable-self` wiki can be public OR scoped — agents see only what their key permits.
- Editing the wiki updates the agent's context — single source of truth.
- Public alternative: any user can publish their own portable-self for others to import.

## Key design question (for research synthesis)

Should `soul.md` be:
(a) A free-form prose page Jacob writes himself (highest fidelity, no constraints)
(b) Frontmatter-driven (`identity:`, `preferences:`, `focus:`, `vocabulary:` keys — easier for agents to parse, harder for Jacob to write fluidly)
(c) Hybrid (frontmatter for structured fields, prose body for everything else)

Recommend (c), but defer to research findings.

## Wiki page convention name

Open Q: `portable-self`? `agent-context`? `me`? `bio`? Whichever wins, register it in agent-discovery surfaces (wikihub-4rel) so any agent connecting via bootstrap knows where to look.

## Acceptance

- This ticket WRITES THE PLAN; implementation tickets fan out from it once research lands
- Naming convention chosen and documented in `docs/agents/portable-self.md`
- Bootstrap endpoint enhanced to recognize the convention
- A reference `@jacob/portable-self` wiki populated as the canonical example
- /agents page documents the three-tier split for users curating their own portable-self

## Dependencies
- BLOCKED-INFORMS-BY: wikihub-XXXXX (research on agent context structures, in flight)
- RELATED: wikihub-4rel (bootstrap endpoint will surface this), wikihub-gzlt (scoped keys gate the access tiers)
- RELATED: wikihub-fuj (contribute-back from AI conversations) — async write path back into the same wiki

## Out of scope
- Writing the actual content of Jacob's `portable-self` wiki — that's a separate curation task once the convention exists
- Cross-user portable-self imports ("clone Karpathy's portable self") — interesting future, not v1
notes:
## Research synthesis (2026-04-23) — concrete decisions

Full report: `~/memory/research/agent-context-structures-2026-04-23.md` (216 lines, 11 sources, ID-RAG paper found).

**Adopt OpenClaw's filename convention verbatim.** OpenClaw is the most explicit standard (162 community templates drop-in compatible at `github.com/mergisi/awesome-openclaw-agents`), and its split is a superset of Claude Code's. ID-RAG (MIT paper, arxiv 2509.25299, Sept 2025) is the academic framing of exactly this split — endorses structured queryable identity store, retrieval per decision loop, identity separate from episodic memory.

### Final naming

A user's `@user/portable-self` wiki has this layout:

```
SOUL.md              # Tier 1 — agent identity (~300-500 tok)
                     #   name, values, communication style, behavioral guardrails.
                     #   Stable across users.
USER.md              # Tier 1 — who the human is (~500-1500 tok)
                     #   name, role, current focus, preferences, relationships, projects.
                     #   This is the CLAUDE.md-equivalent personal block.
AGENTS.md            # Tier 1 — operating procedures (~500-1500 tok)
                     #   workflows, conventions, "always do X" rules.
TOOLS.md             # Tier 1 — tool reach-for guide (~200-500 tok, optional)
MEMORY.md            # Tier 1 — auto-written learnings index (≤200 lines cap)

memory/
  YYYY-MM-DD.md      # Tier 3 — daily logs; today + yesterday auto-read,
                     #   older retrieved via memory_search
  topics/
    <topic>.md       # Tier 3 — auto-written topic detail files
skills/
  <skill-name>/
    SKILL.md         # Tier 2 — Anthropic's YAML-frontmatter skill spec
                     #   (lowercase-hyphen name, description ≤1024 chars,
                     #    body <5k tok). Metadata always loaded; body on trigger.
    resources/...    # Tier 3 — bundled scripts/refs, read on demand
wiki/                # Tier 3 — RAG-retrievable pages (the bulk of WikiHub content)
```

Aggregate Tier 1 budget: **~3-8K tokens.** Per-file cap: 20K characters (mirrors OpenClaw).

### Decisions resolved (no longer open questions)

- **Convention name:** `portable-self` ✓ (replaces earlier candidates `agent-context`, `me`, `bio`)
- **`soul.md` format:** hybrid — frontmatter for structured identity fields when useful, prose body for everything else. Matches both OpenClaw and Letta core-memory persona block.
- **Use `AGENTS.md` not `CLAUDE.md`** as the procedure filename — Anthropic itself recommends `AGENTS.md` for cross-tool compatibility; `CLAUDE.md` signals tool-lock-in.
- **Keep `USER.md` separate from `SOUL.md`.** Both Letta (persona vs human blocks) and OpenClaw split these. Agent identity stays stable when the user changes; user facts can be edited without rewriting the soul.

### Bootstrap endpoint contract (refines wikihub-4rel)

`GET /api/v1/me/bootstrap` returns:

```json
{
  "user": { ... },
  "scope": { ... },
  "always_loaded": {
    "soul":    "<full SOUL.md content>",
    "user":    "<full USER.md content>",
    "agents":  "<full AGENTS.md content>",
    "tools":   "<full TOOLS.md content or null>",
    "memory":  "<MEMORY.md head — first 200 lines>"
  },
  "skills": [
    { "name": "...", "description": "...", "path": "skills/<name>/SKILL.md" }
  ],
  "wikis":     [ ... per-wiki summaries ... ],
  "discovery": { ... API entrypoints ... }
}
```

The agent stuffs `always_loaded.*` into its system prompt verbatim. Skills go in by metadata only; bodies are fetched via standard page reads when triggered. Wikis are RAG.

### ID-RAG note (forward-looking, NOT v1)

ID-RAG suggests a structured identity *graph* queried per decision loop, not per session. For long-horizon persona coherence (multi-day, multi-context conversations), this matters. We can add an `identity/<entity>.md` directory later for this without restructuring v1. Defer until drift is observed.

### What NOT to do (from the report's "skip" list)

- Don't invent a new word for SOUL (e.g., `core.md`, `persona.md`). `SOUL.md` is the dominant convention.
- Don't merge `USER.md` into `SOUL.md`.
- Don't put `wiki/` in bootstrap. RAG only.
- Don't use `CLAUDE.md` as a filename.

### Contrarian note worth weighing

Claude Code deliberately collapses everything into `CLAUDE.md`. That works for task-scoped coding agents. For a persistent personal agent against a knowledge base, OpenClaw's explicit split is better — identity stable, user facts churn, procedures evolve independently. We're in OpenClaw's regime.

## Caveats

- OpenClaw primary docs are thin; the 8-file bootstrap list reconciles across 4+ secondary sources and 2 GitHub issues but lacks a single authoritative first-party page listing all eight.
- ID-RAG is one paper, single benchmark (election sim). Treat empirical claims as directional; the conceptual contribution (identity as queryable graph distinct from episodic memory) is what's worth borrowing.

title: Collaborator git push access with per-path ACL enforcement
description:
Today every wiki has a clone URL (/@user/slug.git) and two repos: authoritative (owner rw) + -public.git (non-owner ro mirror). ACL grants in .wikihub/acl give collaborators WEB edit access but git push is owner-only — receive_pack enforces user.username == owner.username.

What's needed:
- Non-owner collaborators with an :edit grant on some path (whole wiki, folder, or page) should be able to `git push` changes scoped to what their grants allow.
- Writes to paths they don't have :edit on must be rejected, ideally atomically (entire push fails rather than partial apply).

Design paths worth evaluating (get Codex second opinion before picking):

1. **Pre-receive hook ACL enforcement** — collaborator pushes to the authoritative repo; a pre-receive hook walks the pack, diffs changed paths, checks each against ACL grants, rejects the whole push if any path isn't allowed. Pros: one repo. Cons: fetch leaks private history; pack parsing is fiddly; risk of false-allow bugs.

2. **Per-collaborator view repos** — build <slug>-shared-<username>.git per collaborator containing only their visible + editable paths. They push there; server merges back into authoritative. Pros: clean visibility isolation. Cons: N repos per wiki, merge/conflict complexity, sync lag.

3. **Fork + PR flow** — collaborator forks (already supported), pushes to their fork, submits a PR. Simpler but not 'real git push access on the wiki'. Might be the right answer for contributor UX.

4. **Punt on git push for collabs; keep web-only edit for grants** — simplest; git push stays an owner-only power user feature. Document the tradeoff.

Mixed public/private complicates mirrors: a collaborator granted edit on notes/*.md needs a view where notes/ is present (including private notes shared with them) but private/secrets.md is absent. None of the current mirrors do that — public mirror is visibility-gated only, not user-gated.

Dependencies: this probably needs wikihub-ec48 (concurrent-edit warning) before real-time collab, but git-push collab is async so it could land independently.

Related: existing sharing UI (app/routes/api_wikis.py share endpoints) and ACL parser (app/acl.py). Auth already accepts API keys + passwords in git_backend._check_basic_auth.

title: Agent setup: add password save cues + make username apparent
Explore cards: hide empty stats instead of placeholder text

title: Serve binary files from git repo (images 404)
description:
![[image.png]] Obsidian embeds are supported in the renderer but there's no route to actually serve binary files from the git repo. Images referenced in markdown pages 404.

Needs a route that serves raw files from the authoritative or public mirror repo via git cat-file, with proper content-type detection and cache headers. Should respect the same auth dispatch as other wiki reads (owner gets authoritative repo, everyone else gets public mirror).

This is Stage 0 of the file support roadmap (wikihub-291) — prerequisite for any image/attachment functionality to work.
close_reason: Added binary file serving route in wiki_page() — non-markdown files (images, PDFs, etc.) are now served from git repos with correct content-type and cache headers. Fixed public mirror regeneration to preserve binary files intact instead of mangling through UTF-8 decode/encode. Added PDF inline embed rendering via <object> tag for ![[file.pdf]] syntax. ACL-aware: respects per-file visibility.

title: Landing page showcase wiki cards link to # (dead links)
description:
The three wiki cards on the landing page ('deep-learning-notes' by @karpathy, 'systems-design-wiki' by @sarah, 'effective-altruism-research' by @marcus) all link to href='#'. These are hardcoded demo data with fake users.

Clicking these cards does nothing, which may confuse visitors. Options:
1. Link to actual real wikis on the platform (jacobcole's, harrisonqian's)
2. Make them non-clickable/decorative
3. Link to the explore page as a fallback

title: Split folder into standalone wiki
