title: sign-up page doesn't trigger 1Password autofill
description: Individual fields work with 1Password (can fill them one at a time), but the smooth one-click autofill flow doesn't trigger — 1Password doesn't offer to fill the whole form at once. May need a specific combination of autocomplete attributes, form structure, or a hidden username field to get password managers to recognize it as a cohesive signup form.

title: Design GitHub sync and pushback for repo-backed wikis
description: Design a longer-term model for keeping a WikiHub wiki automatically up to date from a linked GitHub repository and, potentially, pushing structured edits back to GitHub. Scope: one-way sync from GitHub to WikiHub, bidirectional update semantics, conflict handling, branch/PR model vs direct push, audit history, and how repo-backed wikis differ from ordinary wikis. This should stay separate from the initial public-repo onboarding import ticket because it needs deeper product and source-of-truth design.

title: Global Curator entrypoint across wiki UI
description: Add a persistent in-product Curator / coding-agent entrypoint that is available across the main wiki UI, not just on reader pages. Scope: launcher visible in shared chrome or persistent sidebar/page header; can ask about the current page or the whole current wiki; can propose/create/edit content from the UI; clearly distinguishes signed-in editable mode from any anonymous/public assistant mode if both exist. Existing related work covers the reader-page Curator drawer (, ) but does not explicitly define a global cross-page launcher or whole-wiki interaction model.

title: Add Karpathy LLM Wiki format guide to wikihub/wikihub docs
description:
Add a page explaining the Karpathy LLM Wiki format to the official wikihub/wikihub wiki. This should help new users understand the format WikiHub is built to host.

Key content: directory structure (raw/wiki/schema), frontmatter fields, the three operations (ingest/query/lint), page content structure (summary, key claims, connections, contradictions), index.md, log.md, CLAUDE.md roles.

Think carefully about where this fits in the wiki's organization and the new user experience. Consider the existing pages: Getting Started, Agent Integration, API Reference, Examples, philosophy/*.
close_reason: Page created and index updated

title: Search should include ACL-shared private docs for the current user
description:
Google Drive readiness assessment found that search is not team-ready because shared private docs are not surfaced the way a collaborative workspace needs.

Desired behavior:
- when an authenticated user searches, results should include content shared to them via ACL/page/wiki grants, not only their own docs or public docs
- access control must still be enforced on snippets/results
- add regression coverage for searching shared private content

Why:
A small team using WikiHub as a shared workspace will give up quickly if teammates cannot search across the docs that were explicitly shared with them.

Evidence:
- /tmp/wikihub-gdrive-assessment.md
- app/routes/api_wikis.py search path
notes: Claimed from Google Drive readiness follow-through on 2026-04-22. Expanding search to ACL-shared private docs.

title: Add comment-only access tier for collaboration
description: Consider a distinct access level between read and write: comment access. Users with this role should be able to leave page-level or inline comments without being able to edit the page directly. Scope: permission model, UI affordances on the reader page, owner moderation/review workflow, interaction with future suggestion flows, and whether comment access is page-level, wiki-level, or both.

title: Design history: ideaflow-vision wiki — scaffolding decisions
description:
Documentation of design decisions for the new `ideaflow-vision` wiki at https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/ideaflow-vision.

## Origin
Jacob asked to synthesize ~57 Slack messages tagged #explainingideaflow (2022→Feb 2026) plus future notestream captures into a coherent LLM wiki about the IdeaFlow vision. Separate from vision-convo (which is one conversation's snapshot).

## Claude's original proposal
- Name: `ideaflow-vision`
- Structure: index.md, AGENTS.md, raw/ by year-window, wiki/framings/ (one page per metaphor), wiki/pitches/ (audience-specific), wiki/themes/, wiki/sources/
- Synthesize Jacob's own framings, cross-link to vision-convo
- Skip notestream for v1

## Codex second opinion (gpt-5.4 high, session 019daa5f)
- ✅ Keep separate wiki (agreed)
- ⚠️ "Too axis-heavy" — don't make framing, audience, theme, era all first-class
- Recommended hybrid: primary=functional layers (personal/team/humanity), secondary=framings, single chronology.md, single entry-points.md
- 80–90% synthesis, 10–20% verbatim quotes
- Add page types: north-star-thesis, what-it-is-not, system-map, chronology, canonical-terms, open-questions, use-cases
- Rename `pitches/` to `translations/` or `entry-points/` or `explainers/` — "pitches" reads salesy
- Risks: context collapse, internal language leakage, metaphor sprawl, product/worldview blur

## Jacob's decisions
1. **Keep `pitches/`** — overrule Codex's rename. Pitches is good.
2. **Use verbatim quotes heavily** — keep original lines, don't over-synthesize. Jacob's voice matters.
3. **Framing for evolution:** "The vision hasn't necessarily changed longitudinally, but it has been refined and has evolved and matured, and more forks have been discovered, with more details and realizations." → this is the backbone of chronology.md
4. Adopt Codex's missing page types (north-star-thesis, what-it-is-not, system-map, etc.)
5. Adopt Codex's functional-layer primary organization (personal → team → humanity)
6. Save this design history in the wiki itself (wiki/meta/design-history.md) and in this beads ticket

## Risk mitigations adopted
- Editorial note in AGENTS.md acknowledging synthesis of historical framings
- Date every ingested excerpt
- Separate canonical current framing from historical
- Link from framings/ pages back to raw/ with timestamps

## Related
- Vision-convo wiki: https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/vision-convo
- Slack source archive (local-only): ~/code/ideaflow-slack-wiki/
- Original session: 1137ed19-6d0a-4294-9240-7c559603a947 (line 462)
- Codex session: 019daa5f-4a5c-7771-a654-a07d44510344

title: Cmd+K trigger looks bad on homepage
description: The Cmd+K trigger element in the top-left nav looks visually broken or out of place on the homepage/landing page. Needs styling fix to match the rest of the nav.
close_reason: Added appearance: none / -webkit-appearance: none to .search-trigger in base.html — Safari was ignoring the custom background and rendering the native white button control

title: Batch-import Jacob's Google Docs exports into /@jacobcole/jacobcole
description:
## ⚠️ REVIEW THIS PLAN BEFORE EXECUTING

Jacob exported ~253 markdown files from his Google Docs universe to
`/Users/jacobcole/code/DataExports/` (also in `~/Documents/vibe-coding-guide/DataExports/`
and `~/Documents/Personal/Book-Draft/source-material/google-docs-full/`).

Only 22 pages made it to prod (`/@jacobcole/jacobcole`). **Import never ran at scale.**

## Export inventory
- `markdown-by-hierarchy/` — 88 files, canonical tree structure (chronicpain/, foodslist/, ideaflow/, infrastructure/, manifestos/, misc/, philosophy/, thingsyoudidntknowexisted/, worldgestalts/, worldquestguild/)
- `markdown-by-category/` — 89 files, same docs re-grouped (duplicate of hierarchy)
- `markdown-new-missing-docs/` — 48 files, second-pass export catching what first pass missed
- `markdown-new-subdomains/` — 26 files, each satellite site (sleep/aspirations/lifehacks/healingartsgrant/etc.) as its own doc

After dedup: ~120 unique documents to import. Current: 22. Gap: ~100.

## Proposed plan (REVIEW)

**Target:** `/@jacobcole/jacobcole` (personal wiki)

**Steps:**
1. Write a batch importer script (`scripts/import_jacobcole_gdocs.py`) that walks a source tree and uploads to `POST /api/v1/wikis/jacobcole/jacobcole/pages`, preserving relative paths.
2. **Dry-run first.** Print every page it *would* create with size + path. No writes.
3. Sources in order (with dedup on normalized slug):
    - `markdown-by-hierarchy/` (primary, canonical tree)
    - `markdown-new-missing-docs/` (layer on, under `missing/` prefix OR same tree if paths match)
    - `markdown-new-subdomains/` (under `subdomains/` prefix — each satellite site as its own page)
    - **SKIP** `markdown-by-category/` (duplicate of hierarchy)
4. **Default visibility: `private`** — safer for a maximal personal brain dump. Jacob flips pages `public` selectively afterward.
5. **Conflict policy:** skip existing pages by default (don't clobber the 22 curated ones). Optional `--force` flag to overwrite.
6. Post-import: run a PII/privacy scanner on imported pages that lists any page containing:
    - Email addresses (`[\w\.]+@[\w\.-]+`)
    - Phone numbers (`\d{3}[- .]?\d{3}[- .]?\d{4}`)
    - Google Docs URLs (`docs\.google\.com/document/d/\S+`)
    - URLs to other private-ish satellite sites (`\*\.jacobcole\.net`, `\*\.jacobblog\.net`)
   So Jacob can review before flipping anything public.

## Flags to review explicitly before making public
These docs likely contain other people's info:
- `hiringblurb.md`, `hiringlist.md`, `hotconnections.md`, `housinglist.md`, `ilparty.md`
- `shadirecs.md` (shadi recommendations)
- `people/*` (if any)
- `productfeedback/bugslist.md`, `productfeedback/productfeedback.md` (internal ideaflow)

## Out of scope for this ticket
- Recursively fetching the satellite subdomain docs fresh from the web (already in `markdown-new-subdomains/` from the export)
- Breaking into multiple wikis (e.g. `/@jacobcole/health-wellness`). Can be done later by moving pages.
- Visibility flips — Jacob reviews the PII report and flips manually.

## Backup before execution
Prod backup from 2026-04-20 already exists at `~/wikihub-backups/prod-2026-04-20-020552/` (db dump + app tar + nginx + SSL). Good safety net if import misbehaves.

title: Master 'things to curate' meta-list — living backlog of curation targets
description:
Create a single canonical list of everything we eventually want to curate. This is the backlog that drives the content-creation tickets.

Initial entries (from Jacob 2026-04-24):
- MIT / Harvard / Stanford entrepreneurship (3 separate tickets)
- Startup free-credits / programs (GCP, AWS, Azure, etc.)
- NVIDIA free hosted AI model APIs (80+ models)
- Agent IDEs / coding agents (OpenClaude, OpenCode, Zed, Hermes agent — all mentioned in seed tweet)
- Twitter-sourced tip threads generally

Add categories as we discover them. Each entry: title, category, status (not-started / in-progress / published / linked-to-awesome), ticket ref.

At this stage we have few enough wikis that showing all of them is better than an arbitrary cutoff. Can add pagination later when there are hundreds.

title: Bootstrap-by-URL+key: any agent gets full WikiHub context from a single connection string
description:
Today an agent that wants to use WikiHub has to: read /agents docs, learn auth, hit /api/v1/accounts, store a key, then re-implement common ops (read page, write page, list wikis). It works but it's not "drop-in" — there's friction every time someone wires WikiHub into a new agent framework.

Goal: any portable agent (a generic Claude/ChatGPT/custom agent runtime) should be able to add WikiHub support in ~5 lines AND mint a scoped key for less-trusted contexts (group chat, embedded contexts, shared-with-friends agent). This is the WikiHub-side counterpart to the user's portable-agent project.

## Scope (this ticket)

1. **Scoped-key minting endpoint** is provided by the parent feature: wikihub-XXX (scoped/restricted API keys — see sibling ticket). This ticket DEPENDS on that.

2. **`/api` discovery doc** (just shipped, wikihub-75e7) gains a `keys.scoped` link pointing at the scoped-key endpoint, so an agent that hits the discovery root learns about scoping in one round-trip.

3. **Reference Python client** — a tiny package (or single module under `cli/wikihub_client/`) with the bare minimum:
   ```python
   from wikihub_client import WikiHub
   wh = WikiHub(api_key="wh_...")
   wh.read("@me/notes/index.md")
   wh.write("@me/notes/new.md", "# Hello")
   wh.list_wikis()
   wh.mint_scoped_key(label="group-chat", max_visibility="public-edit", read_only=True, expires_in="7d")
   wh.feedback(kind="bug", subject="...", body="...")
   ```
   Distribute via PyPI as `wikihub-client` (separate from `wikihub-cli`, which is the user-facing CLI). Keep it under ~200 lines — agents pulling it in shouldn't pay for a heavy dep.

4. **Reference Node/TS client** — same surface, same name (`@wikihub/client`). Mirrors the Python package. The MCP server is already TypeScript so this can share types with `mcp-server/`.

5. **`docs/agents/portable-agent.md`** — a ~1-page recipe walking through:
   - "I have an agent. How do I add WikiHub in 5 lines?"
   - "How do I run my agent in DM mode (full key) vs group mode (scoped key)?"
   - The actual code snippets in Python + TS
   - When to mint a fresh scoped key per session vs. reuse one

6. **Update agent-discovery surfaces** (per AGENTS.md sync table: /AGENTS.md route, /llms.txt, /agents HTML, /.well-known/wikihub.json) to advertise the client packages and the portable-agent recipe.

## Why P2

Real value, but blocked by the scoped-keys feature. After that lands this becomes a 1-2 day docs+packaging effort.

## Open Q (for Jacob)
- **Should the reference client be vendored into the agent's repo, or pulled from PyPI/npm?** PyPI/npm is more standard but adds an install step. Vendoring is zero-friction but creates drift. Recommend: PyPI/npm with a copy-paste fallback in the docs.
- **What's the user's portable agent built on?** (Claude Agent SDK, custom, OpenAI Assistants?) — if there's a specific framework, we should ship a framework-specific binding alongside the generic client.

## Dependencies
- BLOCKED by the scoped-API-keys ticket
- Aligns with: wikihub-1ry (capabilities), wikihub-75e7 (/api discovery), wikihub-o6d (OpenAPI), the existing mcp-server
- Should NOT duplicate the MCP server's surface — MCP is for tool-call agents; this client is for agents that orchestrate WikiHub calls directly without an MCP layer

## Acceptance
- A new agent can `pip install wikihub-client` and write a working integration in <10 lines
- Calling `wh.mint_scoped_key(...)` returns a usable scoped `wh_*` key
- `docs/agents/portable-agent.md` exists and is linked from /agents and /llms.txt
- /api discovery doc advertises the scoped-key endpoint and the client packages
notes:
## Decisions (Jacob, 2026-04-23)

**Reframe.** This is NOT a Python/TS SDK ticket primarily. It's a **bootstrap pattern** ticket. The goal:

> Give any agent (OpenClaw, Claude Code, custom, anything) a single URL+key and it should immediately be able to fetch all the user's context.

Framework-independent. No Claude-specific bindings, no OpenAI-specific bindings. The agent runtime just needs HTTP.

## Canonical share-string format

A single copy-pasteable connection string the user hands to any agent:

```
https://wikihub.md/?key=wh_<scoped-key>
```

OR (preferred — basic-auth style, harder to leak via referrer header / browser history):

```
https://wh_<scoped-key>@wikihub.md/
```

Pick one canonical form (basic-auth-style preferred) and document it. Any agent reading the string can:
- Parse host (`wikihub.md`) and key
- Hit `GET /api/v1/me/capabilities` → knows scope, wikis available, rate limits
- Hit `GET /api/v1/me/bootstrap` (new endpoint, see below) → gets full snapshot in one round-trip

## NEW: GET /api/v1/me/bootstrap

Single-shot endpoint that returns everything an agent needs to "know who I'm working for" in one fetch:

```json
{
  "user": { "id": ..., "username": "jacob", "display_name": "..." },
  "scope": { ... echo of capabilities scope, or null for full keys ... },
  "wikis": [
    {
      "slug": "@jacob/notes",
      "title": "...",
      "description": "...",
      "visibility": "...",
      "page_count": 47,
      "recent_pages": [{"path": "...", "title": "...", "updated_at": "..."}, ... up to 10],
      "index_page": {"path": "index.md", "excerpt": "first 500 chars"} | null
    },
    ...
  ],
  "rate_limits": { ... },
  "discovery": {
    "api_root": "/api",
    "openapi": "/api/v1/openapi.json",
    "feedback": "/api/v1/feedback"
  }
}
```

So the agent's onboarding becomes literally: read the connection string → one HTTP call → it has everything.

## Settings UI: "Connect an agent" button

In /settings, alongside the scoped-keys section, add a **Connect an agent** button. It opens a modal:
- Form: label, scope chips (visibility ceiling, wiki allowlist), expires-in dropdown, read-only toggle
- Submit → mints a scoped key (via `POST /api/v1/keys/scoped` from gzlt)
- Modal then shows the connection string in a big monospace box with a Copy button
- Subtitle: "Paste this into your agent. The key is shown once — store it now."
- Also a small QR code for mobile-paste workflows

This is the human ergonomics part — most users won't curl `POST /api/v1/keys/scoped` directly.

## Out of scope (for this ticket)

- Reference Python/Node client libraries — separate, optional, lower priority. Most agent runtimes can do raw HTTP fine. If users ask, file as follow-ups.
- Framework-specific bindings (Claude Agent SDK, OpenAI Assistants, etc.) — explicitly NOT shipping these. The HTTP API + bootstrap endpoint is the universal interface.
- MCP integration — the MCP server already exists for agents that prefer that protocol. This ticket is for agents that just want HTTP.

## Acceptance

- New `GET /api/v1/me/bootstrap` endpoint returns the snapshot JSON above
- Settings UI has a "Connect an agent" button that mints a scoped key and produces a copy-pasteable connection string
- `docs/agents/connect.md` documents the connection string format and the one-call bootstrap flow with curl examples
- /api discovery doc and /llms.txt advertise both the bootstrap endpoint and the connection-string format
- An OpenClaw agent (or any other) given the connection string can fetch the bootstrap and start working with no other configuration

title: Architecture decision: where does curation live? (WikiHub vs Noos vs dedicated list-platform)
description:
Jacob has made multiple attempts at list-style products. Before investing in curation content, decide the long-term home.

Options to evaluate:
1. WikiHub — pages under a @curate or @resources account, one wiki per community/topic. Pros: public-edit, git-backed, already has search/RAG. Cons: wiki model may be too freeform for a structured list.
2. Noos — thread-first, Slack-native. Pros: capture is trivial (just a slack msg). Cons: not legible/browseable long-term.
3. New list-platform — third/fourth attempt at a dedicated list product. Pros: fit for purpose. Cons: past attempts didn't land; maintenance cost.
4. Hybrid — Noos for capture, WikiHub for the canonical rendered list.

Deliverable: a short design doc (can live at @jacobcole/curation-architecture on wikihub.md) that picks one and justifies it. Think critically — don't default to wikihub just because we're already in this repo.

Context to consider: how awesome-lists work (github README + community PRs), why they win, and whether we can just BE an awesome-list with better ingest tooling.
notes: Relates to parent epic. Block the MIT/Harvard/Stanford content tickets on this decision so we don't write content into the wrong place.

title: Rename visibility levels for clarity: public→public-view, unlisted→unlisted-view
description:
## Problem

The current visibility names are confusing. 'public' doesn't make it clear that it means read-only (no editing). Users could reasonably think 'public' means anyone can edit.

## Proposed Naming

| Current | New | Meaning |
|---------|-----|---------|
| public | public-view | Anyone can read, only owner can edit |
| public-edit | public-edit | Anyone can read AND edit |
| unlisted | unlisted-view | Only people with the link can read |
| private | private | Only owner/ACL can read |

## Scope

- Update the `VALID_VISIBILITIES` set in `app/acl.py`
- Update all references in routes, templates, models
- Update frontmatter parsing to accept both old and new names (backwards compat)
- Update the visibility badge labels in reader.html, folder.html
- Update API docs
- Migration: existing pages with 'public' should map to 'public-view', 'unlisted' to 'unlisted-view'
- Keep accepting old names in frontmatter for backwards compatibility (Postel's Law)

## Files involved
- `app/acl.py` — VALID_VISIBILITIES
- `app/routes/api_wikis.py` — visibility endpoints
- `app/templates/reader.html` — visibility badges
- `app/templates/folder.html` — visibility badges
- `app/models.py` — any visibility defaults
- `wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md` — update spec

## Notes
Candidate for Codex — thorough rename across codebase.

title: Dead anchors on landing page: /agents#credentials and /agents#cli
description:
Landing page links 'More →' and 'Full CLI reference →' go to /agents#credentials and /agents#cli respectively. Neither anchor exists in agents.html — grep for 'id="credentials"' and 'id="cli"' returns zero hits. Clicks land at the top of /agents with no scrolling.

title: Review: Editorial Picks on Explore — how should curation work?
description:
Currently Editorial Picks on /explore is hardcoded to show @wikihub/wiki as a single wiki card. This was auto-generated code, not a deliberate product decision.

Options:
1. **Current: hardcoded @wikihub/wiki card** — just shows the official wiki. Not really 'editorial picks.'
2. **Pages from @wikihub/wiki as picks** — the Featured Wikis page we created has curated links. Could render those as cards instead of showing the wiki container.
3. **Tag/flag system** — wikis get an 'editorial_pick' flag, admins toggle it. Explore queries for flagged wikis.
4. **Curated from the community index wiki** — @wikihub/wikihub Featured Wikis page is the source of truth. A special tag or frontmatter field marks wikis for the explore page.
5. **Remove editorial section entirely** — just show popular (by stars) and recent. Let the @wikihub wiki be the editorial venue, not the explore page.

The community index wiki (@wikihub/wikihub/Featured Wikis) already exists as editorial curation. The question is whether /explore should also have its own editorial section or just link to the community wiki.

title: Agent-discoverability: content negotiation on / (Accept: text/markdown)
description:
When a request to / comes in with Accept: text/markdown, return /AGENTS.md content instead of landing.html. Vercel-pattern — one URL, multiple formats. Cleanest UX for smart agents.

Research doc: docs/agent-discovery.md.

title: Hover preview cards leak when user moves to another link before fetch completes
description: In app/templates/base.html hover preview JS, openPreview() pushes a loading card onto stack then fetches. If user hovers a new link before fetch resolves, closeFromDepth() pops the loading card, but the fetch callback still appends a real card to document.body without re-adding it to stack. The orphan card has no owner and cannot be closed. Fix: in the fetch .then callback, after findIndex, return early if idx < 0 (stale response).

title: Agent-discoverability: head signals (link rel, HTTP Link header, title hint)
description:
Add <link rel="alternate" type="text/markdown" href="/AGENTS.md"> and <link rel="alternate" type="text/plain" href="/llms.txt"> to base.html AND landing.html (landing has its own <head>, no base inheritance). Also emit an HTTP Link: </AGENTS.md>; rel="alternate"; type="text/markdown" header on /. User approved a small agent-friendly hint in landing page <title>.

Research doc: docs/agent-discovery.md.

title: Sidebar: level-2 children not indented past parent (wikihub-58c regression)
description:
## Problem

Children of folders at the 2nd nesting level appear at the same x-position as their parent folder, not visibly indented underneath it.

User report (2026-04-20): "The children of a folder need to be indented farther underneath it. Right now they're at the same level ... at least true on a secondary level, second level down."

## Root cause

In app/templates/reader.html:
- `.sidebar-folder-toggle { width: 20px }`  (line 45)
- `.sidebar-children { padding-left: 20px }` (line 52)

These are EQUAL. A folder-row is `[toggle 20px][item]`, so the parent's folder-icon sits at x=20+8 = 28px inside the folder. A child file row inside .sidebar-children has no toggle, so its item starts at x=20px inside the parent folder — which means the file's icon lands at exactly the same 28px as the parent's icon. No visible nesting.

## Why the regression test didn't catch it

tests/test_e2e.py:636 (`test_sidebar_indentation`) checks only:
1. `.sidebar-children` CSS exists with `padding-left >= 16px` — passes (it's 20)
2. `<div class="sidebar-children">` appears in HTML — passes

It does not verify that `padding-left > toggle-width`, which is the real invariant.

## Fix

1. Bump `.sidebar-children` padding-left to 28px (or more) so child items are clearly indented past the parent's icon.
2. Strengthen the regression test to enforce `sidebar-children.padding-left > sidebar-folder-toggle.width + minimum visual gap`.

## Related

- Parent ticket: wikihub-58c (closed, tagged recurring)
- Attaching recurring label.

title: Sidebar: highlight too wide + child alignment off
description:
## RECURRING ISSUE — sidebar indentation

⚠️ CHECK THIS whenever touching sidebar CSS or templates.

### Current problem
Child items under folders don't appear visually nested enough. padding-left: 12px on .sidebar-children is insufficient — needs ~20px.

### Files affected
- app/templates/reader.html
- app/templates/folder.html  
- app/templates/profile.html

### History
- 2026-04-10: Fixed highlight overflow (overflow-x: hidden, flex constraints) but indentation still too shallow
- 2026-04-10: Identified as recurring — keeps regressing when sidebar code is touched

### Verification checklist
After ANY sidebar change:
1. Check a wiki with nested folders (e.g., /@jemoka/knowledge-base)
2. Verify child items are clearly indented under parent folder
3. Verify highlight background doesn't overflow sidebar width
4. Compare with Notion's sidebar indentation as reference

title: [Agent UX] Public issue-posting HTTP API + GitHub Issues/beads sync w/ approval gate
description:
# Context

Internally wikihub's Curator agent already has a `create_issue` tool (`app/routes/agent_chat.py:696`) that files roadmap issues with `{title, issue_type, priority, description}`. But there is NO external HTTP endpoint for agents/integrations/humans to post an issue directly against a wiki.

Jacob's ask: "if there's an API to literally have it go into the beads. Maybe we could have issues sync into the beads or something, though that probably should be subject to approval so you don't have injection."

# Three layers to design

## Layer 1: Public REST API for issue creation

Propose:
```
POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/issues
Authorization: Bearer wh_...
{
  "title": "...",
  "body": "...",
  "issue_type": "feature|bug|task|epic|chore",
  "priority": 0-4,
  "source": {"kind": "api|github|webhook|human", "ref": "..."}
}
→ 201 {"id": "...", "url": "/@owner/slug/issues/..."}
```

Require the authenticated user to have write access to the wiki (reuse `_get_wiki_or_404` + ACL check). Issues are stored in the existing roadmap-issues table (wherever Curator agent writes today).

## Layer 2: GitHub Issues integration (bidirectional, optional per wiki)

Per wiki settings:
- **Mirror mode (sync-out)**: wikihub issues → GitHub issues on a configured repo (webhook-driven). Safe — wikihub is the authority.
- **Ingest mode (sync-in)**: GitHub issues → wikihub staging queue (see Layer 3). NOT auto-approved because untrusted external input.

Store PAT / GitHub App credentials per wiki, encrypted. Surface in wiki Settings page.

## Layer 3: Beads sync with approval gate (the prompt-injection safety)

Jacob's concern: "subject to approval so you don't have injection."

Design:
- Incoming issues from the public API (or GitHub ingest) land in a `staging_issues` table with `approved=false`
- Owner sees a pending-issues dashboard (`/@owner/slug/issues/pending`)
- On approve, issue copies into wikihub's internal store AND optionally emits to beads via `bd create` (run on the wikihub server against a configured beads repo path)
- Approval has a `review_hint` free-text field so the owner can add "bd-prefix: picortex" or "close-as-dup: wikihub-xxx" before ingest
- Rejection tombstones the row with reason

Alternative: make approval auto for issues posted by the wiki owner's own API key, manual for everyone else.

# Open questions

- Do we want `issues` as a first-class wikihub concept (new table/routes/UI), or keep piggybacking on the Curator agent's internal roadmap store?
- Can we reuse beads' git-backed format directly — e.g. wikihub maintains a thin `.beads/issues.jsonl` file inside each wiki's git repo? That would give git-native sync without extra plumbing. Promising.
- GitHub App vs per-wiki PAT — the App is slicker but much more setup work.

# Done when

- [ ] `POST /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/issues` documented + live
- [ ] GitHub mirror works one-way (wikihub → GH) for one test wiki
- [ ] Staging queue + approval UI for incoming issues from non-owner sources
- [ ] Optional beads `.beads/issues.jsonl` write on approval

# Related

- `app/routes/agent_chat.py:696-745` (existing create_issue tool schema)
- Jacob's prompt-injection concern: untrusted external issue body could contain instructions aimed at agents reading the issue later → staging + explicit human approval is the answer
- Beads repo format: JSONL per-project in `.beads/issues.jsonl` + `.beads/metadata.json`

title: Subdomain assignment strategy — auto-claim vs opt-in, collision policy, opt-out
description:
Current (shipped in wikihub-3fjb): subdomains are OPT-IN per wiki. Owner claims at /@user/slug/settings. jacobcole has 15 wikis, only rsi-wiki claimed a subdomain.

## Options on the table

### 1. Keep opt-in (current)
- Wikis stay at <user>.wikihub.md/<slug> until owner claims a subdomain.
- Pros: no automatic collisions, no migration needed, user controls branding.
- Cons: most wikis never get the nicer URL; wiki subdomain feature is underused.

### 2. Auto-claim at creation, first-come-first-served
- On POST /api/v1/wikis, try to claim the slug as a subdomain. If collision, fall back to empty (still accessible under user subdomain path).
- Pros: most wikis auto-get the nice URL. Aligns with jacob's maximalist preference.
- Cons: early users hog common slugs (ideas, notes, recipes, etc.). Collision = silent fallback unless we surface it.

### 3. Auto-claim with <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md (sub-subdomains)
- Every wiki auto-gets <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md (no global collisions).
- Pros: no collisions ever, every wiki gets a nice branded URL.
- Cons: nginx + Cloudflare wildcard-of-wildcard complexity. Cloudflare doesn't support *.*.domain — requires SaaS addon or per-user DNS records. Long URLs.

### 4. Hybrid: auto-claim with namespace fallback
- Try <slug>.wikihub.md first. If taken, auto-assign <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md.
- Pros: common slugs still work for the first claimer, everyone else gets namespaced.
- Cons: mixed URL schemes across the product. Inconsistency.

### 5. Bring-your-own-domain (BYOD)
- Let users CNAME their own domain (e.g., notes.example.com → wikihub origin).
- Pros: max flexibility, no wikihub.md URL at all.
- Cons: per-domain SSL provisioning (Caddy on-demand, or Cloudflare for SaaS), DNS ops.

## User control primitives needed regardless
- [ ] Opt-out of subdomain (nullable already — setting to null works)
- [ ] Rename / change subdomain post-creation (API already supports — works)
- [ ] Reserved-word enforcement (already in subdomains.py RESERVED_SUBDOMAINS)
- [ ] Username-collision check (already checks via username_conflicts)

## Personal recommendation (for review)
Start with #2 (auto-claim at creation, first-come-first-served) for MVP. Surface collisions in the settings UI: "subdomain <X> is taken, claim something else or keep path URL". Collect feedback, layer in #3 or #5 only if needed.

## Decision owner
jacobcole — opinion TBD, initial lean is #2.

## Related
- Closed: wikihub-3fjb (initial opt-in implementation shipped 2026-04-20)
- Open: real-time collab (wikihub-w0s4) which targets staging.wikihub.md

title: Landing page: 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' CTA
description:
Consider adding secondary CTAs on the landing page hero alongside 'Publish a wiki in seconds'. Candidates:

1. **'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity'** — the grand vision, taps into Snow Crash / Librarian philosophy. wikihub isn't just a tool, it's a collective knowledge project.

2. **'Make private collaborative lists with agents'** — the practical agent use case. Not everything is public publishing — private wikis where your agent writes and organizes alongside you.

3. **'Agent-native Google Drive'** — positions wikihub as the file system / knowledge layer that agents can actually read and write to, unlike Google Drive. Markdown files in git = structured, versionable, API-accessible. Google Drive is for humans with browsers; wikihub is for humans AND agents. This framing resonates with the 'why not just use Notion/Google Docs' objection — those tools have terrible agent APIs.

These could be secondary CTAs, tagline variants, rotating hero text, or value prop cards below the fold. The key insight: wikihub has three distinct value props for three audiences:
- **Publishers** → 'Publish a wiki in seconds' (Karpathy wave, Obsidian users)
- **Visionaries** → 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' (knowledge builders, researchers)
- **Agent users** → 'Agent-native Google Drive' or 'Collaborate with agents on private knowledge' (developers, AI power users)

title: Cmd+K search: context-aware scoping to current wiki/author
Agent-discoverability: visible body signals (banner, paste-prompt, llms.txt)
description:
Top-of-body banner on landing.html: '🤖 Agents: read [/AGENTS.md](/AGENTS.md)'. Flip paste-prompt example from wikihub.md/agents → wikihub.md/AGENTS.md. Promote /AGENTS.md in /llms.txt Documentation section (currently only /agents HTML is listed). WebFetch-style extractors drop <head>; visible body is the reliable channel.

title: Support preparing collaborator invites without sending notification emails yet
description:
Follow-up from the team-wiki prep task (`wikihub-vdwe`).

Current behavior:
- bulk/wiki/page share immediately creates pending invites and sends notification emails

Need:
- a way to stage collaborator/invite preparation without actually notifying people yet
- examples: draft the invite roster, preview the payload, or create pending invite records with email sending suppressed until an explicit later action

Why:
This blocks the founder workflow of preparing a team wiki and collaborators ahead of time, then reviewing before the first send.

Open questions:
- should this be a dry-run mode only?
- or true staged pending invites with a later 'send now' action?
- how should the UI/API expose draft vs sent state?

title: hover dropdowns disappear when moving mouse to options
description: multiple dropdown menus across the site use hover to show/hide, but there's a gap or timing issue that causes the dropdown to disappear before the user can reach it. known instances: (1) download/clone dropdown on wiki pages — hover the download icon, try to move mouse down to the options, dropdown vanishes. (2) account menu in top-right nav — same issue. (3) hamburger menu on mobile. audit ALL hover-based dropdowns site-wide and fix them all. likely fix: add a small padding/bridge between trigger and dropdown, or switch to click-to-toggle with click-outside-to-close.

title: Visibility indicators: subtle lock/globe icons in sidebar and listings
description:
Make it obvious which pages are public vs private without being overwhelming. Options:
- Lock icon on private pages only (public is the default, no icon needed)
- Or globe icon on public pages only
- Show in sidebar items and content listings (folder view, explore cards)
- Should be very subtle — small, muted color, doesn't clutter the sidebar
- The current vis-badge on page view is fine; this is about the sidebar/listing context where you scan many items

title: System-adaptive light mode + theme toggle (Auto/Light/Dark)
description:
Add a light palette matching the Obsidian+amber brand, gated on prefers-color-scheme with a user-selectable override.

Requirements:
- Default: follow OS via @media (prefers-color-scheme: light)
- Override: data-theme="light|dark" on <html>, persisted in localStorage (wikihubTheme=auto|light|dark)
- No flash of wrong theme — inline <script> runs before render
- highlight.js swaps github-dark-dimmed ↔ github when theme changes
- meta theme-color adapts (browser UI chrome color)
- Theme toggle in user menu (or nav) cycling Auto → Light → Dark

Palette (light):
- bg-base: warm ivory/paper (#faf7f1)
- text-primary: warm charcoal (#2a2520)
- accent: darker amber/bronze (#a67525) for AA contrast
- borders: dark warm rgba
- hljs: github light

Scope:
- app/templates/base.html — tokens + script + toggle UI
- .interface-design/system.md — document light palette
- scan templates for hardcoded colors that need adjustment

title: infobox rendering from frontmatter metadata
description: render certain frontmatter keys as a Wikipedia-style infobox sidebar on pages. e.g. person pages get photo, 'known for', 'current role'. pure template work on top of existing frontmatter parsing. inspired by Farzapedia's person pages.

title: Webhook notifications for wiki changes
description: Allow users to register webhook URLs that receive POST notifications when pages in their wikis change. Payload: {event, wiki, page, author, sha, timestamp}. Enables Slack/Discord integrations, CI pipelines, downstream sync. API: POST /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/webhooks to register.

title: Google OAuth login not working despite correct provider console config
description: Google OAuth is configured in the Google Cloud Console with the correct redirect URIs, but login flow is failing. Needs collaborative debugging with Harrison to diagnose — could be callback URL mismatch, client secret issue, or Flask-side handling. Requires hands-on investigation together.

title: [Agent UX] Serve non-markdown files with correct Content-Type; owner opt-in to render HTML instead of attachment
description:
# Observed

Deployed wikihub at https://wikihub.globalbr.ai returns .html files with:
  content-type: application/octet-stream
  content-disposition: attachment; filename=\"<name>.html\"

Example: \`GET /@jacobcole/picortex/docs/mockups/00-platonic-ideal.html\` → download prompt in Safari instead of rendering.

Local \`app/routes/wiki.py\` line 803-827 looks correct — it calls \`mimetypes.guess_type(page_path)\` and returns whatever type that gives (text/html for .html). Only PDF gets Content-Disposition set (inline). So deployed code is either:
- Diverged from git (my local clone was last updated 2026-04-12; server may have newer code), OR
- Has middleware / nginx / Cloudflare rule adding Content-Disposition:attachment

# Why it matters

Agents and users publishing rendered docs, mockups, or interactive SVG naturally want to host .html files. Today the workaround is to push to GitHub Pages instead (I did that for picortex mockups → now at https://tmad4000.github.io/picortex/). Wikihub should be able to do this natively.

# But security concerns are real

Serving attacker-controlled HTML from wikihub.globalbr.ai origin = potential XSS against the SSO session cookie of other wikihub users. The \`attachment\` disposition is probably deliberate defense.

# Proposal: owner opt-in with isolated subdomain

Ship the render-HTML feature safely:

1. **Per-wiki setting** \`render_html: true\` (default false). Owner must explicitly enable for their wiki.
2. **Served from a cookie-less subdomain**: e.g. \`raw.wikihub.globalbr.ai/<owner>/<slug>/<path>\` (or \`<hash>.raw.wikihub.globalbr.ai\` for stronger isolation). The main wikihub SSO cookie is not sent to this origin, so malicious HTML can't steal sessions.
3. Main wiki page links to the raw-subdomain URL when render_html is on.
4. Still serves with proper \`Content-Type: text/html\` + a strict \`Content-Security-Policy\` that blocks scripts from loading user cookies / cross-origin requests to the main origin.

Alternative simpler approach: add an \`?render=1\` query param on the main origin that strips Content-Disposition but requires the wiki owner to explicitly opt in via settings. No subdomain needed but slightly weaker isolation.

# Immediate workaround for agents

Document on /agents: \"HTML files are stored but served as download. For rendered artifacts, host on an external origin (GitHub Pages, jacobcole.ai, etc.) and link from a markdown page.\"

# Done when

- [ ] Per-wiki \`render_html\` setting exists in DB + UI toggle
- [ ] Raw subdomain OR safe query param serves .html / .svg / .css / .js as their natural mime types when enabled
- [ ] /agents page documents the feature + the default (downloads-for-safety)
- [ ] E2E test covers a .html file rendering correctly when opted-in

# Related

- app/routes/wiki.py:803-827 (non-md serving logic — local code looks correct, verify deployed diverged)
- wikihub-o6d (Content-Type + openapi.json discoverability)
- wikihub-8dz (Cloudflare WAF blocks — adjacent origin/headers work)

title: Per-user activity feeds
description: Each user gets an activity feed at /@username/activity showing their recent edits across all wikis. This is the agent-first equivalent of a GitHub contribution graph. Shows: page creates, edits, forks, stars. API: GET /api/v1/users/:username/activity. Web: rendered as timeline on profile or dedicated page.
notes: Related work (2026-04-24): wikihub-ry5w (card-rendering layer for this timeline — consider whether it should be absorbed into 6b1's scope), wikihub-9bi (follow+personalized feed, a downstream consumer), and curation epic wikihub-zdy1. Treat as one coherent social/activity surface when designing the API — the same activity records feed /@user/activity, /@user card stream, and the follower newsfeed.

title: GET-based onboarding for vanilla LLM agents (web_fetch with only GET)
description:
Stretch goal noted 2026-04-22: enable a vanilla LLM (Claude.ai, ChatGPT, Gemini built-in tools) with **GET-only** web_fetch to onboard to wikihub by following instructions in AGENTS.md — no Custom GPT Action, no code exec, just a URL to hit.

## Why

Today, to onboard an agent requires `POST /api/v1/accounts`. Vanilla LLM web-fetch tools are GET-only. That's a sharp cliff in who can onboard without human copy-paste.

## Possible design

- `GET /onboard?username=<handle>` — creates an account, returns JSON with API key
- Rate-limit aggressively: 1/IP/hour; reject bots via basic heuristics
- Must still produce the same `client_config` JSON as POST so the agent can paste it into its credentials file
- Optionally require a confirmation URL click — but that defeats the whole 'vanilla LLM' use case

## Risks

- GET-with-side-effects violates HTTP semantics (caching, prefetch, URL previews can trigger creation)
- Easier spam surface — abuse bots love GET
- Search engines + screenshots could leak API keys if the response ever renders in HTML (never render it — JSON only)

## Alternatives

- Keep as-is, document copy-paste curl workflow in AGENTS.md prominently (today's answer)
- Ship a 'signup magic URL' — user clicks, browser-only, returns JSON. Still not vanilla-LLM-friendly.
- Ship an MCP/Agent Skill plugin that third-party LLMs can install (what Cortex does) — highest lift

## Decision

Deferred. Needs product judgment call before implementation. File captures the aspiration; do not implement until explicit green-light from Jacob.

See also: `wikihub-<one-command-spec>` for the invariant this would extend.

title: WebSocket support for xterm.js terminal and Curator chat
description:
## Problem

The admin terminal (xterm.js) uses HTTP polling (100ms GET + POST per keystroke), causing ~200ms lag per character. The Curator chat uses SSE which ties up a gunicorn sync worker.

## Solution

Add WebSocket support via flask-sock. This gives:
1. Real-time terminal I/O (admin auth page)
2. Real-time Curator chat streaming (instead of SSE)
3. Foundation for future real-time features

## Requirements
- Add flask-sock to requirements.txt
- Switch gunicorn to gevent workers: `gunicorn -k gevent`
- WebSocket endpoint for terminal: /ws/terminal
- WebSocket endpoint for Curator chat: /ws/curator
- Update xterm.js to use WebSocket attach addon
- Update Curator panel JS to use WebSocket instead of fetch+SSE

## Risk
Changing gunicorn worker type from sync to gevent could affect request handling. Test all existing endpoints after the switch.

title: Hover preview: cap stacking depth to match LessWrong (+1 as potential bump)
description:
Follow-up to wikihub-5613 stack-leak fixes. Current behavior: unlimited recursion on related-link hovers (no depth cap).

**Goal:** match LessWrong's hover-preview depth behavior (that's the inspiration this feature is cloning). In my previous recommendation I said cap=3 on the assumption LessWrong stops around there in practice — **verify the actual cap LessWrong uses before implementing**, and ship that number, not a guess.

**The +1 question:** once we know LessWrong's cap N, consider shipping N+1 instead so users can explore 'one more step' than the reference. Trade-off:

Arguments for matching LessWrong exactly (N):
- clean design-reference match
- proven by their scale
- beyond N, users are probably better served by clicking through to a full page

Arguments for N+1:
- users who want to explore a concept neighborhood one more step get it
- still manageable on most laptops with alternating below/above placement
- with current fixes (timer leaks fixed, mouseleave suppression narrowed, orphan sweep, click-outside always closes), the reliability cost of one more level is low

**Implementation regardless:** one-line change in the related-link mouseenter handler — early-return when (depth + 1) is greater than or equal to CAP. The link keeps its href and normal click still navigates. Add an e2e test that POSTs a page with deeply-nested wikilinks and verifies the Nth hover-preview-link's mouseenter does not spawn an (N+1)th card.

See also: wikihub-5613 (the stack-leak fixes) — that work fixed reliability but did not introduce any cap.

title: Curator Agent: inline sidebar agent for wiki curation (Snow Crash / Ready Player One inspired)
description:
## Vision

An inline curator agent sidebar in the spirit of the Librarian from Snow Crash and the Curator from Ready Player One. An AI assistant that lives in the WikiHub UI sidebar and can take actions on your behalf — curate, cross-reference, promote content between wikis.

## Example Use Case

You're browsing someone's idea list. You want to promote an idea to the Featured Wikis page. The curator agent can:
1. Read the current page context (who you are, what page you're on)
2. Take actions with your permissions (read/write wikis you have access to)
3. Append content to public-edit pages, create pages in your own wikis, etc.

## Architecture Considerations

### Agent Backend
- **Claude Code under the hood** (like Noos project) — coding agent with file/API tools
- Should run in **print mode** for streaming responses to the sidebar
- Needs the current user's API key for WikiHub API calls
- Must be aware of: logged-in user, current page URL, current wiki context

### Security / Isolation
- Agent runs with the permissions of the current user (their API key)
- Must NOT get access to wrong directories on the server
- **MVP: Unix user permissions** may be sufficient — each agent session runs as a restricted user with only API access (no filesystem access beyond temp dirs)
- **Future: Docker containers** for full isolation if needed
- Agent should only interact via the WikiHub API, not touch git repos directly

### Parallel Agents
- Architecture should support multiple users running curator agents simultaneously
- Each agent session is stateless — gets user context at start, runs, returns result
- Server-side: queue or spawn model per request

### UI
- Sidebar panel on the right side of wiki pages
- Chat-like interface: user types a request, agent streams a response
- Agent can show proposed changes before applying them
- Minimizable/collapsible

### Chrome Extension (v2)
- Personal agent sidebar that works on any website
- If Chrome extension sidebar is open, WikiHub's built-in sidebar hides (avoid duplication)
- Extension would use the same WikiHub API key
- Relates to the Cortex project for personal agent infrastructure
- Could detect if user is logged into WikiHub and auto-configure

## MVP Scope (v1)
1. Right sidebar panel on wiki pages (behind feature flag or logged-in only)
2. Chat input → sends to Claude API with system prompt including page context
3. Agent can call WikiHub API endpoints (search, read_page, create_page, update_page, append_section)
4. Streams response back to sidebar
5. No filesystem access — API-only

## Open Questions
- Unix permissions vs Docker for agent isolation?
- Should the agent have its own API key or use the user's?
- Rate limiting per user?
- How to handle the Chrome extension ↔ built-in sidebar coordination?

title: Admin page HTML served without auth at /api/v1/admin/claude-auth
description: The admin Claude auth page template is served to any visitor at /api/v1/admin/claude-auth (HTTP 200, 8.7KB HTML). The API endpoints behind it correctly check admin token, but serving the admin UI to unauthenticated users is minor info disclosure. The page render should also require the admin token.
close_reason: Closed

title: Track umbrella QA sweep for homepage and user-facing workflow consistency
description:
Recent user requests asked for a broad audit of the homepage, links, uploads, user flows, and route consistency across the app.

This ticket is the durable umbrella for that QA sweep, separate from individual bug tickets.

Scope:
- landing page links and claims
- auth flows and redirects
- upload flows
- shared/settings/people/explore/profile routes
- agent surfaces (`/AGENTS.md`, `/llms.txt`, MCP, well-known endpoints)
- subdomain behavior

Output:
- maintain a checklist/report of working, broken, and untestable flows
- spin concrete bugs into child tickets as they are found
- close only when the sweep has been rerun after fixes

title: Review: bio/profile frontmatter in personal wiki — Claude-proposed, needs human sign-off
description:
The personal wiki profile-aware frontmatter (bio:, avatar:, links: fields in index.md) was proposed by Claude and added to the spec at 2026-04-10 03:45 PT in commit 10633ac. It follows the GitHub profile README pattern — your personal wiki index.md doubles as your profile page with special frontmatter fields rendered in the profile header.

Open questions:
- Is frontmatter the right place for bio/avatar/links, or should these be DB fields editable via a settings UI?
- Should bio come from frontmatter at all, or should the rendered markdown body BE the profile (no special fields)?
- Is the GitHub profile README pattern the right model, or is something like a Notion-style profile page better?
- How does this interact with agents writing to the personal wiki — should an agent be able to update your bio?

Currently working fine as-is. This is a design review, not a bug.

title: Signup/login: fix 1Password autofill so credentials save with a usable identifier
description:
Observed 2026-04-22 during the wikihub-skp7 invite-flow test: when signing up via the invite link, 1Password offered to save the login but the saved entry had a blank username — it didn't know whether to treat the email input or the WikiHub username input as the login identifier.

## Root cause (likely)

`app/templates/auth/signup.html` has two inputs that could be the login ID:
- `<input type="text" name="username" autocomplete="username">` (the WikiHub username)
- `<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">` (the account email)

With `autocomplete="email"` alone (not "username email") and `autocomplete="username"` on the other field, 1Password/Chrome autofill heuristics don't know which to save. Login (/auth/login) only has a username field — so the email-based save from signup can't be replayed on login anyway.

## Proposed fix

1. On signup, mark the email input `autocomplete="username email"` so password managers save email as the login identifier (users remember emails better than WikiHub usernames).
2. On /auth/login, accept login by email OR username (needs route change) and expose both inputs, or keep one input with `autocomplete="username"` that accepts either.
3. Test in 1Password + Chrome + Safari password managers: signup → save → visit /auth/login → autofill should work.

Same class of issue probably affects the "email forward to login" redirect flow from `auth.signup` when someone already has an account.

See also: wikihub-[last-sign-in-hint ticket] and wikihub-[username-prefill ticket] — all three are invite-flow UX polish.

title: Add /api root endpoint: agent-friendly discovery index
description:
Today the API is mounted at /api/v1 (app/__init__.py:34). Hitting /api returns 404, which confuses agents that probe the obvious root before discovering versioned paths. They guess wrong endpoints, retry, sometimes give up.

## Fix

Add a GET /api endpoint that returns a small JSON discovery doc:

```json
{
  "name": "wikihub",
  "current_version": "v1",
  "versions": {
    "v1": {
      "base": "/api/v1",
      "openapi": "/api/v1/openapi.json",
      "capabilities": "/api/v1/me/capabilities",
      "docs": "/docs/api"
    }
  },
  "deprecated_versions": [],
  "feedback": "/api/v1/feedback",
  "request_id_header": "X-Request-ID"
}
```

Also serve the same doc at /api/ (trailing slash) and HEAD on both. Content-Type: application/json. Cacheable (5 min) since it changes rarely.

## Why P1

Trivial implementation, large agent-UX dividend. Pairs naturally with wikihub-o6d (OpenAPI) — this gives agents a known fixed entry point to discover everything else.

## Files
- new route in app/routes/api.py (or a dedicated app/routes/api_root.py)
- register at root (no /api/v1 prefix) — likely in app/__init__.py

## Acceptance
- curl https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/api → 200, JSON with the schema above
- curl https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/api/ → same response
- HEAD /api → 200 with same headers, no body
- Linked from README.md API section
close_reason: Closed

title: Email verification on signup
description:
Currently anyone can sign up with any email — no verification. For pending-invite security (sibling of wikihub-iga9 follow-up), we need to verify that a user truly owns the email claimed at signup before auto-applying pending grants.

1. On signup with email: generate verification token, send link; set users.email_verified=false.
2. /auth/verify-email?token=... sets email_verified=true.
3. Pending-invite materialization (wikihub-xxxx) only happens after verification.
4. Banner in UI prompting unverified users to verify.
5. Option to resend verification.

Not strictly required for Jacob's initial team flow, but closes the impersonation hole where attacker signs up claiming victim@corp.com and inherits the victim's pending grants.

title: SEO + LLM crawlability improvements
description: From Harrison brainstorm 2026-04-10. Wiki content should surface in AI search engines. Backlinked pages boost SEO. Partially done: public pages crawlable, llms.txt exists, content negotiation works. Still needed: sitemap.xml, OpenGraph/JSON-LD structured data, meta descriptions from excerpts, robots.txt verification.

title: Anonymous posting (claimable or non-claimable) across CLI, API, UI
description:
Support posting content anonymously, with two modes:

1. **Claimable anonymous** (DEFAULT) — post has no author attribution, but any authenticated user can claim authorship later (becomes their post going forward).
2. **Non-claimable anonymous** — post has no author attribution and can never be attached to an account. Permanently anonymous.

**Default when a user selects 'anonymous': claimable=true** (updated 2026-04-21 per user).

Must work consistently across all three surfaces:
- **API**: new fields on page create/update (`anonymous: true`, `claimable: true|false`); new `POST /api/v1/.../claim` endpoint for claim flow
- **CLI**: `--anonymous` and `--claimable/--no-claimable` flags on page-creation commands; `wikihub claim <page>` command
- **UI**: checkbox/toggle in the page editor; 'Claim this page' button visible on claimable anonymous posts when logged in

Open design questions (resolve before implementing):
- ~~Default: is anonymous posting claimable or non-claimable by default?~~ RESOLVED: claimable=true
- Abuse: anyone can claim (user's explicit design). First-come-first-served. No original-author-token protection. Spam/impersonation risk accepted.
- Storage: how is anonymous authorship represented in Postgres (null user_id? synthetic 'anonymous' user?) and in git (what goes in the commit author field — proposed: 'Anonymous <anonymous@wikihub.md>')
- ACLs: anonymous posts inherit wiki's default visibility. Private wikis can have anonymous posts.
- Audit log: existing audit_log already tracks IP/session — no new tracking needed.

title: Homepage: Get Started vs Sign In are different flows — audit duplication
Landing-page drop-zone upload fails with 400 CSRF (meta tag missing)
description:
POSTing to /new from the hero drop-zone returns 400 'CSRF token is missing'. Root cause: landing.html does not include the <meta name="csrf-token"> tag (it doesn't extend base.html). The inline fetch-wrapper at line 873 checks for that meta and early-returns if missing, so no X-CSRFToken header is attached.

Repro:
1. Sign in
2. Drop any .md file on the hero drop zone
3. Network tab: POST /new -> 400 'The CSRF token is missing'

Fix: add <meta name="csrf-token" content="{{ csrf_token() }}"> to landing.html head.

title: GET query-param login: ?api_key= and ?username=&password= on /auth/login
description:
Extend /auth/login to accept GET with credentials in the query string, so URLs can auto-sign-in (bookmarkable admin links, paste-in-iMessage auth on trusted devices). Already supported via POST form fields today.

Scope:
- GET /auth/login?api_key=wh_...&next=/x → session cookie + 302 to next
- GET /auth/login?username=u&password=p&next=/x → session cookie + 302 to next
- Reuse _check_login_rate_limit()
- Redact api_key= and password= query params from access logs (nginx + werkzeug)
- Flash a one-time warning: 'You signed in via URL — rotate this key if the link was shared'
- e2e test covering both GET flows
- Fail-closed: invalid creds → normal login page with flash + 401

Security: GET creds leak to access logs, browser history, bookmarks, Referer header. Document the tradeoff in CLAUDE.md / landing 'agents' docs. Users accept the risk by using the URL pattern.

title: Electron app + offline mode — potential Obsidian replacement
Cross-wiki search across trusted friends
description: From Harrison brainstorm 2026-04-10. Search across wikis that friends have shared with you. Example: who are investors I should talk to - searches friends shared wikis and surfaces connections with intro paths. Harrison: One of the greatest offerings you can do is open your wiki to someone. Depends on ACL sharing (exists), follow/friend model, cross-wiki search.

title: Improve default profile text for users without bios
Epic: Obsidian feature parity audit — autocomplete, page creation, conventions
Cross-wiki wikilinks: [[@owner/slug/path]] syntax with hover previews
description:
Support `[[wikilinks]]` across wikis, not just within one wiki. Today `[[Page Title]]` resolves within the current wiki; cross-wiki links require full URLs (`https://wikihub.md/@owner/slug/path`) which skip the rich hover-preview treatment.

## Why it matters

1. **Hover previews across wikis.** WikiHub's hover-preview cards only fire for internal wikilinks. Users crossing between wikis (e.g., ideaflow-vision → vision-convo) lose the preview UX and get raw anchor tags instead.
2. **Rename resilience.** Full URLs are brittle: rename a page and every outbound link from a sister wiki breaks silently. Cross-wiki wikilinks (resolved against canonical IDs or current titles) survive renames.
3. **Unified backlink graph.** The `wikilinks` table / page-graph today stops at wiki boundaries. Cross-wiki wikilinks would make a user's multi-wiki network legible as one graph — which is exactly the worldview WikiHub is hosting.
4. **Authoring ergonomics.** Writing `[[@jacobcole/vision-convo/projects/ideaflow]]` beats copy-pasting long URLs, especially in LLM-authored content.

## Triggering example

Just scaffolded `/@jacobcole/ideaflow-vision` with 15+ cross-links to `/@jacobcole/vision-convo`. All use full `https://wikihub.md/...` URLs. None trigger hover previews. If vision-convo ever renames `projects/ideaflow.md`, every outbound link from ideaflow-vision breaks.

## Proposed syntax (pick one — need decision)

1. **Path-qualified:** `[[@owner/slug/path/to/page]]` — explicit, unambiguous, closest to the URL shape. Display text defaults to page title; pipe for alias: `[[@jacobcole/vision-convo/projects/ideaflow|IdeaFlow]]`.
2. **Prefix-qualified:** `[[wiki:owner/slug:Page Title]]` — MediaWiki-style interwiki prefix. Less URL-like, more title-driven.
3. **Scheme-prefixed:** `[[wh://owner/slug/path]]` — most future-proof if we ever want other schemes (e.g., `gh://` for GitHub).

Recommend option 1 — closest to what users already see in URLs.

## Semantics to decide

- **Title vs path resolution.** Do we match the target wiki's page by path (stable, URL-like) or by title (friendlier, changes with renames)?
- **Permission handling.** If the target page is private, the link should render but hover preview should 404 gracefully (not leak metadata).
- **Rendering fallback.** If the target wiki doesn't exist or the page isn't found, render as a broken-wikilink (`color-wikilink-broken`) rather than a raw URL.
- **Backlink visibility.** Should `/@vision-convo/projects/ideaflow` see its inbound cross-wiki links from `/@ideaflow-vision/*` in its backlinks panel?

## Implementation sketch

Touch points:
- `app/renderer.py` — markdown-it-py wikilink plugin needs to recognize the cross-wiki pattern, build the correct URL, and mark the link for hover preview.
- `app/models.py` — `wikilinks` table currently scoped to one wiki; needs nullable `target_wiki_id` to represent cross-wiki edges.
- Hover preview fetcher in `app/templates/base.html` — currently hits `/api/v1/wikis/<current>/pages/...`; needs to honor a data attribute or URL pattern to switch wiki.
- Backlinks query — join across wikis when target page has cross-wiki backrefs.

## Out of scope (v1)

- Cross-account renames (what if target user renames their account).
- Private-to-private resolution (requires share-grant check at render time).
- Drag-and-drop authoring UI.

## Related

- Scaffolded the two-wiki setup that surfaced this: `ideaflow-vision` ↔ `vision-convo` on 2026-04-20. See `wikihub-4i3b` (closed).
- Vision-convo's `themes/llm-wiki-as-medium.md` already notes WikiHub as the IDE — cross-wiki linking is part of that IDE story.

## Priority justification

P2 — not blocking current work (full URLs work), but every day we don't have this, the cross-wiki graph gets staler and links get more fragile. Becomes P1 when a third wiki joins the network or a cross-wiki rename breaks something visibly.

title: Friendly git push error: 'owner-only, use web edit or fork'
description:
Today git_backend.receive_pack returns a blanket 401 for both (a) unauthed and (b) authed-but-not-owner. The second case should be a 403 with a helpful body so the user sees something like: 'Push rejected — only @OWNER can push to @OWNER/SLUG. You can edit via the web at https://wikihub.md/@OWNER/SLUG, or fork at https://wikihub.md/@OWNER/SLUG (then send a PR when that lands).'

This is the stopgap UX while we decide the real collab-write architecture in (long-term ticket). No ACL changes, no new push semantics — just differentiate 401 vs 403 and write a useful message.

Files: app/git_backend.py receive_pack + info_refs (for the service=git-receive-pack branch).

Test: attempt `git push` to a wiki you don't own — confirm a 403 with the helpful body, confirm owner push still works.

title: [Agent UX][BUG] GET /api/v1/wikis/<owner>/<slug>/history returns HTTP 500
description:
# Observed

Independent agent investigation 2026-04-23 (see session `3db5b175` notes). Route is defined at `app/routes/api_wikis.py:1123` and advertised in the agent docs, but hitting it in production returns:

\`\`\`
HTTP 500
{\"error\":\"internal_error\",\"message\":\"Something went wrong\"}
\`\`\`

Severity: **no version-aware edits possible** until this is fixed. Agents that want to do safe read-modify-write (fetch current content + ETag, apply diff, PUT if ETag still matches) have no way to check history.

# Repro

\`\`\`
curl -H \"Authorization: Bearer wh_...\" \\
  https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/api/v1/wikis/jacobcole/picortex/history
\`\`\`

# Done when

- [ ] Repro fixed (returns 200 with JSON)
- [ ] Integration test added so it doesn't regress
- [ ] Consider also adding `/api/v1/wikis/<o>/<s>/pages/<path>/history` per-page variant
- [ ] Support `?ref=<sha>` on page reads for time-travel GETs
close_reason: Closed

title: [Agent UX] Cloudflare WAF blocks non-curl user-agents (error 1010)
description:
# Observed

During the 2026-04-23 picortex session, the wikihub bulk-upload from Python urllib (default UA \`Python-urllib/3.12\`) was blocked with Cloudflare **error 1010** on every /api/v1/wikis/.../pages request — \`HTTP 403\` with a Cloudflare block page as the body (\"The owner of this website has banned your access based on your browser's signature\").

The exact same request payloads sent via **curl** (UA \`curl/8.x\`) succeeded.

# Why this matters

Python is the default HTTP client for many agent frameworks — Anthropic's SDK, LangChain, Instructor, etc. Any agent that isn't Claude Code itself (which happened to use curl) will hit this wall before it even sees a rate-limit, key-scope, or API-shape issue. The Cloudflare block returns a generic HTML page, not a JSON error, so agents get a confusing 403 with no machine-readable reason.

# Likely cause

Cloudflare WAF \"Super Bot Fight Mode\" or \"Bot Fight Mode\" blocks known programmatic user-agents by default. \`Python-urllib\` is in the default bad-bot signature set; \`curl\` is not.

# Options

1. **Allowlist in Cloudflare WAF** — exempt /api/v1/* from bot-fight rules (or entire wikihub.globalbr.ai). Risk: more scraping headroom, but the API is authenticated anyway so the bot-fight protection is redundant there.
2. **Require API key in query/header**: keep WAF strict but add a WAF bypass rule for requests carrying a valid \`Authorization: Bearer wh_*\` header. Needs a Cloudflare Worker or custom rule.
3. **Document the workaround** in /agents: \"If your HTTP client uses a generic Python/Node user-agent, set a custom User-Agent header explicitly (e.g. \`User-Agent: my-agent/1.0 (+wikihub-api)\`).\"

Recommend (1) scoped to /api/v1/*, plus (3) as a doc update until (1) is shipped.

# Done when

- [ ] /api/v1/* works with default Python urllib UA
- [ ] /agents page documents UA expectations + calls out the specific trap
- [ ] Integration test added: pytest that hits the production API with urllib and asserts no 1010

# Related

- Current /api/v1/* is already authenticated; Cloudflare bot protection there is double-coverage
- Affects picortex bulk-upload, future MCP servers, future integrations

title: bulk permission changes
description: no way to bulk change permissions on multiple pages at once. need ability to select multiple files and change visibility/ACL in one action.

title: diff.html crashes with 'NoneType is not iterable' when sidebar_items is None
description:
Found in journalctl during 2026-04-25 incident triage:

```
File 'app/templates/diff.html', line 107, in block 'body'
    {{ render_sidebar(sidebar_items) }}
File 'app/templates/diff.html', line 74, in template
    {% for item in items %}
TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable
```

Some diff requests have sidebar_items=None instead of []. Either:
1. Fix the route to always pass [] (search for diff route in app/routes/wiki.py and ensure sidebar_items is always set)
2. Defend in template: {% for item in items or [] %}

Both, ideally.

title: IdeaFlow Notion import cleanup: strip block-type markers and linkify child pages
description:
IdeaFlow Notion import markdown currently leaks raw Notion block types into the published content, e.g. literal lines like `[paragraph]` and `[child_page]` in the seed repo and live imported wiki.

Need to:
- identify the exporter/transform step emitting those markers
- render paragraph blocks as plain markdown text instead of prefixed block labels
- render child_page blocks as actual links using the child title and, when available, a resolvable target path or source Notion URL
- refresh the generated seed/import content after the transform is fixed

Examples observed on 2026-04-22 in the IdeaFlow import include `vision/explaining-ideaflow-2024.md`, `roadmap/feature-roadmap.md`, and `team/onboarding-quickstart-guide.md`.

title: DESIGN SPEC: one-command agent onboarding — verification is never a gate
description:
## The invariant

**`POST /api/v1/accounts` returns an API key that is immediately usable for every read/write operation on the caller's own data — without email verification, without browser interaction, without a second round-trip.**

This is the core value prop. It is the reason wikihub ships with 28 users on day 10. Any design change that violates it is wrong, regardless of how it's justified.

## What this forbids

Email verification MUST NOT gate any of these:
- Account creation (`POST /api/v1/accounts`)
- API key issuance / rotation
- Creating wikis (`POST /api/v1/wikis`)
- Creating, updating, deleting pages on your own wikis
- Creating API keys additional to the first
- Reading public content
- The CLI's `signup`, `login`, `new`, `write`, `read` subcommands
- Any of the agent-discovery surfaces (`/llms.txt`, `/llms-full.txt`, MCP tools)

## What verification MAY gate (enrichment, not core)

- Materializing pending invites addressed to an email (`wikihub-yjsv`)
- Auto-link of Google OAuth to an existing unverified email (`wikihub-ks5t.4`)
- Login-by-email convenience (as an alternative to login-by-username)
- Sender-reputation badges in the collaborator UI
- Public display of email as a contact ("contact this user")

## Discovered-in-session rationale

Jacob 2026-04-22: "One-command agent onboarding workflow. If you have to verify an email before you start using the app, that's lame."

## Stretch goal — 'vanilla LLM' onboarding

Today agents with any HTTP client can onboard:
```
curl -sX POST https://wikihub.md/api/v1/accounts \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"username":"my-agent"}'
```

Vanilla Claude.ai and ChatGPT's built-in `web_fetch` are GET-only — they can't issue that POST without additional tooling (Custom GPTs with Actions, Claude Agent Skills with code exec, etc.). **Nice-to-have follow-up**: file a ticket to explore a GET-based onboarding endpoint (e.g. `/onboard?username=X`) that a GET-only LLM could hit via an instruction in a copy-pasted URL. Weigh against spam/abuse risk.

## Cross-reference for future agents

Any ticket that touches authentication, verification, account creation, or API key issuance MUST cite this spec and affirm that it does not violate the invariant. Cross-link it from:
- `wikihub-yjsv` (invite token)
- `wikihub-ks5t.*` (auth audit family)
- `wikihub-750v`, `wikihub-3i9i`, `wikihub-um90` (the UX-polish trio)
- `wikihub-v8wa` (multi-account switcher)
- Any new auth/verification work

## Acceptance criteria for this spec ticket

1. This description is referenced by name from AGENTS.md as a guardrail
2. `/AGENTS.md` agent-facing route adds a 'Design invariants' section that states the no-verification-gate rule verbatim
3. Cross-references added as comments on every open auth-related ticket
4. `tests/test_e2e.py` has a `test_agent_onboarding_no_verification_gate` that creates an account with no email, then exercises every core write path on that key — regression guard.

title: Switch default canonical URL from wikihub.globalbr.ai to wikihub.md
description: Server BASE_URL env var still produces magic links / client_config pointing to wikihub.globalbr.ai, causing cross-domain session issues when users land on wikihub.md. Fix: set BASE_URL=https://wikihub.md in /opt/wikihub-app/.env, restart wikihub service, update app/credentials_hint.py:14 docstring to match.

description:
Build a curated resource list for Harvard entrepreneurship. Candidates to include:

- Harvard Innovation Labs (i-lab, Launch Lab X for alumni)
- HBS Rock Center / Rock Summer Fellows
- HBS New Venture Competition
- Harvard Ventures (undergrad)

Format per architecture-decision ticket. Minimum 20 entries w/ 1-sentence descriptions.
