title: Git clone/push fails: pre-receive hook missing Flask module
description: Git operations (clone, push) against wikihub repos fail with a server-side pre-receive hook error. The hook imports Flask but the module isn't available in the hook's Python environment. API works fine — only git operations are broken. Workaround: use REST API instead of git.

title: Mobile: sidebar toggle + basic mobile UX audit
description:
## Problem

The left sidebar is not accessible on mobile. There's no hamburger menu or toggle button to open it. This means mobile users can't navigate between pages within a wiki — they're stuck on whatever page they landed on.

## Scope

1. **Sidebar toggle on mobile** — add a hamburger/menu button that opens the sidebar as an overlay on small screens
2. **Basic mobile UX audit** — verify all core flows work on mobile:
   - Reading pages (text wrapping, tables, code blocks)
   - Navigation (breadcrumbs, sidebar, wikilinks)
   - Profile page layout
   - Search
   - Login/signup forms
   - Creating/editing pages (if logged in)

## Files involved
- `app/templates/reader.html` — has sidebar-overlay infrastructure but may lack a toggle button
- `app/templates/folder.html` — same
- `app/templates/base.html` — global nav bar, may need hamburger
- CSS in each template's style block — media queries for mobile
close_reason: Hamburger menu added to reader, folder, and profile pages. Opens sidebar as fixed overlay on mobile.

title: Add non-owner collaborator management roles (delegated admin / manage-sharing tier)
description:
Recent user request from Claude history asked whether non-owner roles exist and whether collaborators can manage invites/access without being the owner.

Current state:
- sharing exists for owners
- bulk + individual collaborator add exists
- but there is no clear delegated-admin / manage-sharing role for non-owners

Goal:
Design and implement an explicit collaborator role beyond read/edit, allowing trusted non-owners to manage sharing/invites (or another narrowed subset of admin powers) without full ownership transfer.

Need to resolve:
- exact capabilities of the role
- interaction with ACL and page/wiki grants
- UI surface for inviting/revoking as a non-owner
- audit trail and safety constraints

title: Consistent nav across all pages (landing + inner)
close_reason: Implemented in this session

title: backlinks: surface 'what links here' from wikilinks table
description: surface 'what links here' backlinks in the page sidebar. data already exists in the wikilinks table — just need to query and render. like Wikipedia's 'what links here' feature.

title: Private wiki/page: add request-access CTA on private-or-missing surface
description: When a user lands on a private wiki or page they cannot access, the current UX is a generic private-or-missing dead end. Add an elegant request-access affordance when the target exists but the viewer lacks access, without leaking sensitive existence details beyond what we intentionally expose.

title: Sidebar UX: custom ordering and folder visibility icons
description:
Consider a stronger left-sidebar information architecture for wiki navigation.

Requested exploration:
- support custom/manual ordering in the left-hand sidebar instead of only derived sort orders
- show privacy/visibility icons on folders in the left-hand sidebar, not just pages, so private/public state is legible during navigation

Questions to resolve:
- per-user ordering vs wiki-global ordering
- drag-and-drop ordering vs explicit sort metadata
- how folder icons should behave when children have mixed visibility
- whether folder icons should reflect explicit folder ACL/defaults, derived child visibility, or both

This is a product/UX ticket for future design and implementation, not an immediate code change.

title: Folder workflow: creation UX broken, no edit button on folder index, new page should default to current folder
[UX] Embed Noos Universal Feedback Widget on wikihub pages
description:
# Context

Noos already designed and implemented a universal feedback widget:
- Architecture doc: `~/code/noos/docs/UNIVERSAL_FEEDBACK_WIDGET.md`
- Entity-feedback doc: `~/code/noos/docs/ENTITY_FEEDBACK_ARCHITECTURE.md`
- Widget source: `~/code/noos/chrome-extension/noos-feedback.js`
- Server route: `~/code/noos/dist/routes/feedback.js`

Key design points from the architecture doc:
- Embeddable via `<script src="...noos.js">`
- Sets `window.__noosWidgetInstalled = true` so Chrome-extension fallback knows to skip
- API: `GET /api/feedback?url=<canonical-url>` → `{ issues: [], annotations: [], counts: {} }`
- URL normalization: strip UTM, trailing slash, fragments

# Task

Add the widget to wikihub's `app/templates/base.html` so every rendered page gets:
1. A floating feedback button (bottom-right)
2. Click → opens the noos widget keyed on the current page's canonical URL (`https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@<owner>/<slug>/<path>`)
3. Shows existing feedback + lets any authenticated noos user leave feedback

# Open questions

- Host the widget script at `wikihub.globalbr.ai/noos.js` (proxy) or hotlink `globalbr.ai/noos.js`? Hotlink is simpler; proxy avoids cross-origin concerns.
- Opt-in per wiki, or on by default? Suggest: off by default, owner can enable via Settings toggle.
- Should the widget also know about *wikihub* issues (from the other ticket about public issue-posting API)? That would unify feedback/issue surface. Probably yes, long-term — but v1 can just show noos feedback.
- Auth interop: noos widget expects noos SSO cookie. Users logged into wikihub (email + password) won't automatically have a noos session. Need cross-SSO or fall back to "log in with noos" button inside the widget.

# Done when

- [ ] Owner toggle in Settings: "Show feedback widget on pages"
- [ ] `base.html` conditionally includes the noos.js script
- [ ] Widget opens and shows feedback count on reader pages
- [ ] Cross-origin cookie story works (or noos login button renders inside widget)

# Related

- `~/code/noos/docs/UNIVERSAL_FEEDBACK_WIDGET.md` (full design)
- `~/code/noos/chrome-extension/noos-feedback.js` (implementation)
- Jacob's principle: reuse existing noos infrastructure rather than build parallel feedback system

title: Graph coloring: only color by folder when subfolders actually exist
description: When coloring graph nodes by folder, if the wiki has no subfolders (just files at the root level), every single node gets a different color because each file is treated as its own 'folder'. Should only apply folder-based coloring when there are actual subdirectories. If everything is flat at the root, use a single color for all nodes.

title: Design community index wiki (@wikihub as curated meta-index)
description:
The @wikihub/wikihub wiki should serve as a curated community index — an 'awesome list of awesome lists.' User stories:

1. A user creates a great 'Best Body Masters List' wiki. They want it featured.
2. The community wiki has a page like 'Best Fitness Wikis' that links to it with a blurb.
3. A visitor lands on @wikihub and can browse curated categories pointing to the best user wikis.

This is different from /explore (algorithmic, by stars/activity). The community wiki is editorial — someone decides what's featured.

Key questions to resolve:
- Is @wikihub/wikihub the right place for this, or should there be a separate @wikihub/featured wiki?
- How do categories work? Pages in the wiki like 'Best AI Tools Wikis', 'Best Reading Lists'?
- Should featured wikis get a badge/indicator on their own pages?
- Who can edit the community index? Just admins, or suggested-edit PRs from users?
- How does this interplay with the Explore page? Does Explore pull from the community wiki's editorial picks?

For v1: probably just manually curated pages in @wikihub/wikihub that link to user wikis with [[cross-wiki wikilinks]] or plain URLs. The index IS the wiki — no special machinery needed. Editorial curation is just writing markdown.

Reference: ListHub had similar brainstorming about community lists as indexes of other lists.

title: Survey: what's already covered by awesome-lists? (avoid duplication)
description:
Before curating our own lists, audit github.com/sindresorhus/awesome and its children for:

- MIT / Harvard / Stanford entrepreneurship resources
- General startup tooling (cloud credits, free tiers, YC resources)
- AI model free tiers / free inference (is there already an awesome-free-llm-apis?)
- Twitter resource threads

For each, note: coverage quality, last-updated date, maintenance health, and the gap we'd fill. If awesome-X already covers something well, we should link to it (not recreate it). If it's stale or missing, that's our opportunity.

Deliverable: a short table in ~/memory/research/awesome-list-audit-2026-04-<date>.md.
notes: Parent: curation epic. This gates content-creation — don't write a list if a live awesome-list already covers it better.

title: Read-only collaboration: suggest edits or request write access
description: Design a path for pages where the viewer only has public-view or read access. Options to evaluate: 1) allow non-destructive suggested edits on read-only pages, 2) offer an explicit request-write-access flow, or 3) both with clear permission semantics. Scope should cover UI entrypoints on the reader page, notification/review path for owners, and how it relates to the broader suggestion/comment system.

title: Per-person sharing: generalized share/unshare/list-grants API
close_reason: Implemented generalized share/unshare/list-grants API endpoints with wiki/folder/page-level patterns

title: Revert to arbitrary history state
description: Add ability to revert a page or wiki to any previous commit. Agent-first: API endpoint POST /wikis/:owner/:slug/revert with {sha, path?}. Web UI: 'Revert to this version' button on the diff view page. Should create a new commit (not force-push), so the revert itself is tracked in history.

title: Inline expansion of own wikis in left sidebar (no navigation needed)
description:
## Goal
In the left-hand sidebar on a user's profile page (e.g. /@jacobcole), allow expanding the tree of a wiki inline to see its pages, without having to first navigate into the wiki.

## Current behavior
Sidebar shows top-level wikis grouped by folder (health/, ideas/, local/, etc.) and a flat list of featured wikis. To see pages inside a wiki you have to click into it.

## Proposed behavior
Click a disclosure chevron next to a wiki name to expand it in place, showing the same page tree that would appear in the wiki's own sidebar (respecting ACLs / visibility). Collapsing returns to the compact view. State persists in localStorage so the tree stays as you left it across visits.

## Open questions / design considerations
- Scale: some wikis have hundreds of pages. Lazy-load page tree on first expand; cap initial depth; show "view all" affordance.
- Confusion risk: mixing wiki-level nav and page-level nav in one tree could muddle mental model. Maybe render expanded children indented with a subtle background so they read as "drilled in" rather than siblings of wikis.
- ACL: only show pages the current viewer can see (same rules as the wiki's own sidebar).
- Mobile: sidebar is already cramped; may need to stay collapsed-by-default on narrow viewports.
- Performance: fetch via the same endpoint used by the wiki's own sidebar; cache per-session.

## V2 scope
This is likely a V2 polish, not a V1 requirement — useful for owners navigating their own knowledge faster, and for visitors skimming a profile. Worth prototyping behind a feature flag to see if it's clarifying or overwhelming.

## Motivation
User (Jacob) has 10+ wikis and finds navigating into each one to see contents slow. Inline expansion would turn the profile into a live directory of his whole public knowledge base.

title: Warn when multiple editors have a page open at once
description:
Precursor to real-time collaboration. Before two people can edit the same page at the same time, we need a live warning banner when another editor is concurrently viewing/editing the same page.

Scope:
- Detect concurrent editors on /edit routes (heartbeat, presence pings, or similar)
- Show a live warning in the editor UI: 'X is also editing this page'
- Don't block save yet — just surface the conflict risk so users can coordinate out-of-band
- Decide on a presence mechanism: polling, SSE, WebSocket, or Redis pub/sub

Out of scope (for this ticket):
- Actual real-time collaborative editing (CRDTs, Yjs, OT)
- Conflict resolution on save (already handled via content_hash/If-Match)
- Cursors or live character sync

This is a 'before we add X, we should do Y' ticket — blocker/precursor for any real-time collab work.

title:
Render soft line breaks as
(breaks: true)
description: Single newlines inside a paragraph currently collapse to a space (strict CommonMark default in app/renderer.py:create_renderer). Users writing in Obsidian/chat style expect one-line-per-thought to render with line breaks, like GitHub comments and Obsidian do. Reported by user against https://jacobcole.wikihub.md/jacobcole/health/Sleep where a supplement list rendered as one wall of text. Fix: pass {'breaks': True} to MarkdownIt() in create_renderer().
acceptance_criteria:
A page with single-newline-separated lines renders
tags between them; e2e test added in tests/test_e2e.py; deployed to wikihub.md; Sleep page now displays line breaks.

title: Sidebar: distinguish folders from cross-wiki links in navigation
description:
## Problem

When navigating the sidebar, there's no visual distinction between:
1. **Folders** — subfolders within the current wiki (e.g. `ideas/` folder inside `jacobcole/jacobcole`)
2. **Wiki links** — links that take you to a different wiki entirely (e.g. from personal wiki to `jacobcole/ideas`)

Both use the same folder/page icons and indentation. Users can't tell if clicking will keep them in the current wiki or jump to a different one.

Currently the sidebar only shows items from the current wiki's file tree. Cross-wiki links only appear in page content as regular markdown links. The profile page has a separate "Wikis" section, but this pattern isn't used elsewhere.

## Proposed Solutions (pick one or combine)

### Option A: Wiki icon in sidebar (minimal)
Add a third icon type — a small "wiki" icon (e.g. book/globe/grid) — distinct from the folder and page icons. When the sidebar lists sub-wikis (e.g. on a profile or index page), use this icon instead of folder.

### Option B: "Your wikis" sidebar section
Like the profile page already does — show a separate labeled section "WIKIS" below the file tree in the sidebar. This already works on profile.html (lines 176-185). Extend to reader.html and folder.html when viewing a user's personal wiki.

### Option C: Breadcrumb-aware context switching
When you click from one wiki into another, the breadcrumb should make it visually obvious you've changed context — e.g. the sidebar header changes from "JACOB'S IDEAS" to the new wiki's name, with a "← back to profile" link. (This already happens, but the transition is jarring with no warning.)

### Option D: Wiki cards in page content
When the index page links to other wikis, render those as visually distinct cards (title + description + page count) rather than plain text links. This makes it obvious in the content area that these are whole wikis, not pages.

## Recommendation
Start with **Option B** — it's the simplest and profile.html already has the pattern. Then add **Option A** (wiki icon) for polish. Option D is nice for landing pages but is a bigger change.

## Files involved
- `app/routes/wiki.py` — `_build_sidebar_tree()` builds the sidebar
- `app/templates/reader.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 181-209)
- `app/templates/folder.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 47-69)
- `app/templates/profile.html` — already has the "Wikis" section pattern (lines 176-185)
close_reason: Implemented Option B + A: sidebar now shows a 'Jacob Cole's Wikis' section with book icons below the current wiki's file tree. Applied to reader.html, folder.html, and profile.html. Deployed to production.

title: Add a dedicated meeting-notes template/scaffold
description:
Google Drive readiness assessment found that WikiHub has general structured/freeform scaffolds but no explicit meeting-notes template.

Desired behavior:
- add a meeting-notes scaffold that is easy for a small team to start using immediately
- should fit existing WikiHub patterns (markdown, wikilinks, optional action items / decisions / notes structure)
- available from the existing wiki/page creation flows without introducing heavy new machinery

Why:
Meeting notes are a core shared-workspace use case and one of the faster ways to narrow the Google Drive gap.

Evidence:
- /tmp/wikihub-gdrive-assessment.md
- app/templates/new_wiki.html
- app/git_sync.py scaffold/template plumbing

title: TOC double-encodes HTML entities (ampersand shows as &)
description:
The table of contents in the right sidebar double-encodes HTML entities. Headings like 'Homerow & Vimium' render correctly in the main content but show as 'Homerow & Vimium' in the TOC.

Root cause: extract_toc() in renderer.py (line 38) strips HTML tags but doesn't unescape HTML entities. The resulting text (containing &) is then escaped again by Jinja's auto-escaping, producing &amp; in the HTML.

Fix: add html.unescape(text) after the re.sub() call in extract_toc().

Reproduction: visit https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@harrisonqian/tools-stack and look at CONTENTS sidebar — 'Homerow & Vimium' and 'Notes & Knowledge'
close_reason: Duplicate of wikihub-osd (same TOC double-encoding bug)

title: Clean up junk/test accounts polluting production People page
description: Multiple test accounts are visible on production People page: stress-test-1, stresstest123, stresstest_probe_99, ratelimit-test-1 through ratelimit-test-5, 'test spaces', 'test<script>...', test_upper, '../../../etc/passwd'. Need DB cleanup and possibly an admin delete-account endpoint.

title: Public-edit workflow: fix end-to-end UX gaps
description:
Umbrella ticket tracking the audit findings for the public-edit visibility workflow. Anonymous and non-owner users who technically have write permission via public-edit cannot discover or invoke editing from the UI in multiple places — the backend ACL (app/acl.py:125 can_write returns True for public-edit) is correct, but templates don't honor the user_can_edit flag that wiki.py:916 already computes.

## Scope

- Fix reader.html to use user_can_edit (supersedes wikihub-bvl)
- Make wiki_index compute and pass user_can_edit to reader.html
- Surface editing affordances in folder.html for non-owners
- Unify sidebar New page link under can_write, not is_owner
- Surface History in the page-actions bar (currently URL-only)
- Author attribution: anonymous commit fingerprint for revert/moderation
- "Request edit access" / "Sign in to edit" CTA for view-only pages

Related existing tickets:
- wikihub-bvl (covered by child A below; description root cause is wrong — not a stale deploy, template never reads user_can_edit)
- wikihub-4t2 (display-layer rename partly done in reader.html:274, storage rename still needed)
- wikihub-ub2.5 (panic button) — pairs with fingerprint work
- wikihub-ub2.6 (revert N anonymous edits) — depends on fingerprint ticket below

## Files (verified via audit)
- app/routes/wiki.py:777 wiki_page, :916 user_can_edit, :948 edit_page, :997 anon author attribution
- app/templates/reader.html:265-322 page-actions bar (never references user_can_edit)
- app/templates/folder.html:108-111, 181-186 owner-only gates
- app/acl.py:106 can_read, :125 can_write

title: Fix Edit button visibility: reader.html must honor user_can_edit for non-owners and anonymous users
description:
The variable user_can_edit is computed correctly in app/routes/wiki.py:916 (is_owner or can_write(...)) and passed to reader.html — but the template never reads it. The page-actions block at reader.html:295-322 only has branches for is_owner (line 295) and authenticated non-owner (line 306, Star/Fork only). Anonymous visitors and non-owner ACL grantees get no Edit button even when entitled.

## Fix
Replace or extend the conditional at reader.html:295 with a branch that renders the Edit button whenever user_can_edit is true, independent of is_owner and is_authenticated.

## Supersedes
wikihub-bvl — that ticket's stated cause (stale deployment) is incorrect; the bug is also present in the local repo. Close bvl when this lands.

## Reproduction
https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@harrisonqian/tools-stack (public-edit page). Amber "Public Edit" badge shows, no Edit button.

## Files
- app/templates/reader.html:265-322
- app/routes/wiki.py:916 (logic is correct, just unused)
close_reason: Closed

title: wiki_index route must compute and pass user_can_edit to reader.html
description:
When a wiki has an index.md, the wiki_index route renders reader.html but does not compute user_can_edit (app/routes/wiki.py:594, rendering at :632). Even after the reader.html fix lands, users on a public-edit wiki's index page get no Edit button because the template receives no truthy user_can_edit.

## Fix
Mirror the user_can_edit computation from wiki_page (wiki.py:916) in wiki_index and include it in the template context.

## Files
- app/routes/wiki.py:594 wiki_index
close_reason: Closed

title: Folder view: show New page / New folder for non-owners on public-edit wikis
description:
folder.html gates New page / New folder links (lines 108-111 and 181-186) on current_user.id == wiki.owner_id. On a public-edit wiki, a non-owner or anonymous visitor cannot discover how to create content from the folder view, even though the backend new_page route (app/routes/wiki.py:1024) accepts anonymous POSTs.

## Fix
Replace the owner-only guard with a can_write check. Pass user_can_edit from the folder route into the template and reuse it.

## Files
- app/templates/folder.html:108-111, 181-186
- app/routes/wiki.py folder route (look for folder_view / similar)

title: Sidebar 'New page' link should follow can_write, not is_owner
description:
Sidebar in reader.html:227-239 gates the New page link on current_user.id == wiki.owner_id. Should show whenever user_can_edit (or a wiki-level can_write) is true. Matches the folder view fix above.

## Files
- app/templates/reader.html:227-239

title: Surface page History in the page-actions bar
description:
The history routes (/@owner/wiki/history at wiki.py:709, /@owner/wiki/page/history at :719) exist but are not linked from any page surface. Users must guess the URL.

## Fix
Add a History button to the page-actions bar in reader.html next to Edit, linking to /@owner/wiki/page/history. Visible to any viewer for public pages; owner-only can be the conservative first cut.

## Files
- app/templates/reader.html:265-322 page-actions bar

title: Anonymous editor fingerprint: record IP/session in git commit trailer
description:
Anonymous edits currently all commit as 'anonymous <anon@wikihub>' (app/routes/wiki.py:997-998, app/git_sync.py:149-158) with no IP, session, or fingerprint. The git log cannot distinguish one anonymous editor from another. This blocks any meaningful anonymous moderation.

## Fix
Add a git commit trailer (e.g. 'X-Wikihub-Session: <hash>' where hash = sha256(ip + daily_salt)) on anonymous commits. Keep the visible author as 'anonymous' for privacy; the trailer gives admins a revert-by-session primitive without exposing raw IPs in history.

## Unblocks
- wikihub-ub2.6 (revert last N anonymous edits) — currently nonfunctional as a useful tool because all anonymous editors look identical in the git log
- Pairs with wikihub-ub2.5 (panic button) and wikihub-ub2.1 (IP rate limit)

## Files
- app/routes/wiki.py:997-998
- app/git_sync.py:149-158

title: Request edit access / Sign in to edit: contextual CTA on view-only and restricted pages
description:
Today a visitor to a public (view-only) page sees only the badge and download menu — no invitation to request access or sign in. On a public-edit page where anonymous writes are disabled (per wikihub-ub2.5 panic button), the visitor sees nothing prompting them to sign in either.

## Minimal standard implementation

Add a 'Request edit access' button to the page-actions bar when:
- user is not owner AND user_can_edit is false AND visibility in ('public', 'private')

On click, the minimal first cut is one of:
1. mailto: link with prefilled subject and body addressed to the wiki owner's public email (zero new infra)
2. POST to a tiny /api/v1/request-access endpoint that creates a notification row for the owner (nicer UX, one model + one route)

Also add a 'Sign in to edit' affordance when:
- user is anonymous AND the page is public-edit AND anonymous writes are disabled for this wiki

## Why standard / not overthought

- No new ACL machinery; no pending-approval queues; no invitation tokens in v1
- Owner sees the request in existing notification channel
- Can evolve into a real approvals queue later

## Relation
- Rides on the unified toolbar (wikihub-zy0h)
- Pairs naturally with anonymous-writes-disabled panic button (wikihub-ub2.5)

## Files
- app/templates/reader.html page-actions bar
- possibly new: app/routes/access.py with POST /api/v1/@<owner>/<wiki>/request-access

title: Email notifications on share (Cloudflare Email Service)
description:
Send an email when a wiki is shared with someone (existing user OR pending invite). Required for Jacob's team-onboarding flow.

**Decision (2026-04-21): Provider = Resend.** In progress by parallel agent. Do not touch email-sending infra in other tickets until this lands.

**Infra first:**
- Decide email provider: SES (already on AWS Lightsail), Postmark, Resend, or SMTP relay → **RESEND**
- Add env vars: EMAIL_PROVIDER, EMAIL_FROM, provider-specific keys (stored in /opt/wikihub-app/.env)
- Basic email-sending helper (sync first; queue later)
- Templated HTML+plaintext versions

**Email types:**
1. 'You were invited to a wiki' — for existing users (contains deep link)
2. 'You've been invited to create an account on wikihub.md' — for pending invites (contains signup link with email prefilled)
3. Optional: 'Your pending invite was applied' — when a new user signs up and inherits grants

**Respect:**
- Add an unsubscribe/prefs page (required in most jurisdictions)
- Rate-limit per recipient to prevent spam
- Don't email the sharer themselves

title: One-click navigation back to own profile from any page
description: Currently getting back to your own profile requires opening the account dropdown menu then clicking 'View profile' — two clicks. Should be one click from anywhere. Options: (1) clicking the avatar/initial in the nav goes directly to profile, (2) add a persistent 'Home' link in the nav, (3) the [[wikihub]] logo could go to your profile when logged in instead of the landing page. Need to decide which pattern fits best.
close_reason: Avatar in nav bar is now a direct link to profile (one click), dropdown chevron separated

title: Restructure Jeremy Nixon wiki into Karpathy three-layer format
description:
Jeremy's 452 pages are currently flat in the wiki root. The Karpathy pattern organizes into:

- schema.md — what this wiki is about, how it's structured
- index.md — table of contents (already exists)
- log.md — timeline of additions
- wiki/ — compiled, curated knowledge pages by topic
- raw/ — unprocessed source entries (the current 450 dated posts)

An agent could:
1. Move all dated entries into raw/
2. Create wiki/ pages that compile the best insights by topic (machine intelligence, learning, startups, thinking frameworks, etc.)
3. Generate schema.md describing the wiki structure
4. Create log.md with a timeline

This is a good showcase of the 'agent maintains a wiki' use case — the raw material exists, an LLM compiles it into structured knowledge.

title: Define onboarding artifact map for world vision, life paradigm, and IdeaFlow
description:
Create the next-step content plan for turning the Vision Convo wiki into clear onboarding materials.

Context:
The raw conversation and wiki already contain the major ideas, but the material is still distributed across themes, concepts, projects, practices, and resources. The immediate need is not more extraction; it is a clear onboarding architecture.

Goal:
Define the minimum set of public-facing artifacts and their relationship to each other so a new reader can understand:
1. the broad world vision
2. the personal life paradigm underneath it
3. where IdeaFlow fits as the concrete implementation wedge

Recommended output of this task:
- A short artifact map covering at least these documents:
  - Onboarding to the Vision for the World
  - Onboarding to My Life Paradigm
  - What IdeaFlow Is Really For
  - Skeptic's FAQ
  - Reading / Watching / Doing Path
- For each artifact: audience, purpose, tone, and 5-10 bullet outline
- A recommended reading order / onboarding ladder for new people
- A note on what belongs in each layer:
  - worldview / civilization thesis
  - personal practice / life paradigm
  - product / company wedge
- Guidance on what should be explicit but separated:
  - core thesis vs speculative material
  - inspiration vs practical next steps
  - system map vs personal story

Why this matters:
Without this artifact map, the worldview, practices, and product wedge can blur together. That makes onboarding harder and makes IdeaFlow easier to misread as either just a startup with vague philosophy or a total worldview disguised as a startup.

Suggested framing:
- Life paradigm = what kind of human to become
- World vision = what kind of civilization to build
- IdeaFlow = the concrete first lever / implementation wedge

This is a planning/content-architecture task first. Implementation can happen as follow-on writing tickets once the artifact map is approved.

title: Per-person sharing: wiki settings page with grant management
Graph system: page graph, full graph, filtered graph with progressive coloring
description:
## Context

Wikis are graphs. The wikilink structure already contains rich relationship data — we just need to visualize it. This spec defines a three-view graph system that works on any wiki (structure-agnostic) but looks better with metadata (progressive enhancement).

## Three views, one graph engine

Underlying data: nodes = pages, edges = wikilinks. Derived from the wikilinks index wikihub already builds for backlinks. No separate graph database needed.

### 1. Page graph (every page sidebar/footer)
- Current page = center node, highlighted
- Show all pages directly linked TO or FROM this page (1-hop)
- Optionally expand to 2-hop (friends of friends)
- Click node → navigate to that page
- Like Obsidian's local graph — scoped, useful, not overwhelming
- Must be fast enough to render inline on every page load

### 2. Full graph (wiki homepage + dedicated route)
- Every page is a node, every wikilink is an edge
- Force-directed layout — clusters emerge naturally from link density
- The "wow" view showing the shape of the whole knowledge base
- Zoom, pan, search, click-to-navigate
- Shown on wiki homepage (index.md) as an interactive embed and at a dedicated `/@user/wiki/graph` route

### 3. Filtered graph (overlays on full graph)
- Filter by: directory path, frontmatter `type:`, tag, or search query
- "Show only pages connected to X" — page graph launched from full graph
- "Hide all conversation pages" — reduce noise to see topic structure
- Toggle filters on/off, combine them

## Coloring strategy (progressive enhancement)

The graph looks good with zero schema knowledge and great with it:

| Signal | How to use | Agnostic? |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Directory path | `wiki/courses/` = blue, `wiki/topics/` = green, etc. | Yes — any wiki with folders |
| Frontmatter `type:` | Override directory color if present, more precise | Semi — only typed wikis |
| Frontmatter `tags:` | Secondary color ring or icon badge | Semi |
| Link count | Node size scales with inbound + outbound links | Yes — derived from wikilinks |
| Recency | Opacity or glow based on `updated:` or git date | Semi |

**Fallback:** no directories, no frontmatter, no nothing → every node same color, sized by link count. Still works. Degrades gracefully.

**Key rule:** never require metadata for the graph to function. Only use it to enhance when present.

## Where graphs appear

- **Page sidebar/footer**: page graph (1-hop), always visible, lightweight
- **Wiki homepage (index.md)**: full graph embedded inline — interactive `<canvas>` or collapsible section. WikiHub auto-injects on index.md pages, or expose a `{{graph}}` embed directive
- **Dedicated `/graph` route**: full-screen interactive graph with filter controls, per wiki

The homepage embed doesn't violate structure-agnosticism — it's a rendering feature of the homepage route, not imposed on content.

## Tech notes

- **Data source:** wikilinks index (already exists for backlinks). Endpoints: `GET /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/graph` returning `{nodes: [{id, title, type?, dir?}], edges: [{source, target}]}`
- **Frontend:** d3-force or similar force-directed layout. Lightweight, browser-native.
- **Performance:** wikis under 500 pages — render all nodes. Above that — progressive loading or cluster-first-then-expand. Page graph (1-hop) must be instant.
- **Interaction:** hover = show title + link count, click = navigate, drag = reposition, scroll = zoom

title: Session cookie missing Secure and SameSite flags
description: Session cookie is set with only HttpOnly; Path=/. Missing: Secure flag (required for HTTPS to prevent transmission over HTTP), SameSite=Lax or Strict (CSRF prevention). Fix: set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True and SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' in Flask config.
close_reason: added SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE (env-configurable, defaults on), SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE=Lax, SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True

title: Noos design question (reference): heavier base concept vs. list-of-lists
description:
## Cross-project reference

This ticket exists on the WikiHub side for visibility; the actual design question lives on Noos.

## The question

Does Noos need a heavier unit above `list` — a "base" or "space" with distinct identity, visibility boundary, export surface, or collaborator set? Today Noos is uniform: nodes + edges, with `list` as the container type and hierarchy built from list-of-lists.

## Why WikiHub cares

WikiHub's "wiki" is exactly that heavier unit — a git repo with its own ACL, mirror, and identity. The contrast between the two systems is informative either way: if Noos adds a heavier unit, agent guidance needs to mirror WikiHub's "page-first" heuristic. If Noos stays flat, the guidance stays "list-freely."

## Link

Noos side of the discussion: will be pinned here once created.

Status: reference only. Track the decision on the Noos side; echo outcomes back to this ticket.

title: Test login accounts (alice/bob) visible and functional on production
description:
The production login page at https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/auth/login shows 'test accounts' section with 'Login as alice' and 'Login as bob' buttons. The TESTING_LOGIN config is enabled on the production server, allowing anyone to log in as these test users without credentials.

This is a security/UX concern:
1. Visitors see test account buttons, which looks unprofessional
2. Anyone can log in as alice or bob and potentially create wikis, star things, etc.
3. Test accounts might have special permissions

Fix: disable TESTING_LOGIN in the production .env file
close_reason: test-login now requires both TESTING_LOGIN=1 AND debug=True — production (debug=False) always returns 404

title: Contribute-back from AI conversations (Stack Overflow for agents)
description: From Harrison brainstorm 2026-04-10. When someone gets AI help, contribute the QA back to a public wiki anonymized. Harrison: everyones getting coding help privately, Stack Overflow is a ghost town. This is the CCI pattern - could integrate with existing claude-collective-intelligence repo. Key: anonymized, opt-in, structured as wiki pages.

title: Decide collaborator git-write architecture (pick between options 1-5)
description:
Decide collab write architecture: fork+PR (3), per-collaborator view repos (2), staging-refs Gerrit-style (5), or punt (4). Codex second-opinion picked option 3 with option 5 as a polished variant; see comments on wikihub-3se7.

Key constraints any design must satisfy:
- `.wikihub/acl` is content — a collaborator who can write it can escalate.
- Frontmatter `visibility:` is content — same escalation risk.
- Rename/move and deletion semantics need explicit rules (a collab shouldn't delete or rename a path they can't read).
- Git's trust model is repo-level. Path-level read filtering inside a single repo is not something Git natively supports and is where leaks happen.

Out of scope for this ticket: the stopgap UX (tracked separately). This ticket is "decide and design" — pick the architecture, write it down, then split into implementation tickets. No coding until a direction is chosen.

title: wiki upload from drag and drop is broken
description: drag and drop wiki upload doesn't work. needs investigation — check the upload route, form handling, and JS drag/drop event listeners.

comments.author: hq
comments.text: user reports still broken — needs more investigation

title: Add a lightweight recent-activity view from existing git/event data
description:
Google Drive readiness assessment found there is no usable activity feed for a small team, even though git history and event data already exist.

Desired behavior:
- expose a simple recent-activity view showing who changed what and when
- reuse existing history / audit primitives where possible
- can be per wiki first; broader per-user/team views can follow

Why:
A team workspace needs a basic answer to 'what changed recently?' even before any richer social/feed system lands.

Evidence:
- /tmp/wikihub-gdrive-assessment.md
- app/routes/wiki.py history routes
- app/templates/folder.html history UI

title: Google OAuth should preserve next destination and invite context
description:
Workflow audit finding from 2026-04-22.

Current auth templates link straight to `/auth/google`, and `google_callback()` redirects to `/`. That drops `next`, invite email, and invite token context, so Google sign-in/sign-up cannot continue where the user was going.

Likely fix:
- stash validated `next`, `email`, and invite token in session / OAuth state before redirect
- restore them in the callback
- apply pending invites on successful Google auth
- add e2e coverage for invite + OAuth redirect preservation if feasible

Evidence:
- app/templates/auth/login.html
- app/templates/auth/signup.html
- app/routes/auth.py google_login/google_callback
- workflow audit report /tmp/wikihub-workflow-audit.md

title: Zero-shot onboarding worked example in /AGENTS.md
description:
Users want to paste a prompt into Claude Code (or any coding agent) that makes the agent do: signup with email → create a private wiki → invite teammates by email. Today /AGENTS.md covers individual endpoints but has no end-to-end worked example, so the agent does a lot of discovery and back-and-forth.

Fix: add an 'Onboarding in one call' (or similar) section near the top of /AGENTS.md with a copy-paste shell script that chains the three steps using only documented endpoints:
- POST /api/v1/accounts with {username, email} → captures api_key
- POST /api/v1/wikis with {slug, title}  (private is the default — the scaffolded .wikihub/acl starts with * private)
- POST /api/v1/wikis/<user>/<slug>/share/bulk with grants=[{email, role: 'edit'}, ...]
  - bulk-share already handles unknown emails by stashing pending invites

Also update the rendered /agents HTML template (per the 'agent instruction surfaces' sync rule in dev AGENTS.md) so humans see the same recipe.

Deliverable: pasting 'read https://wikihub.md/AGENTS.md, then create an account with <my_email>, a private wiki called <name>, and share it with <teammate_emails>' to Claude Code / Cursor / Codex should complete in one round-trip per step, no guessing.

title: Sort accounts and wikis by recent activity on Explore page
No CORS headers on API responses
description: API responses include no Access-Control-Allow-Origin or other CORS headers. Cross-origin requests from browser-based agents or integrations will fail. Need to decide CORS policy and implement it.
close_reason: Closed

title: Scoped/restricted API keys: tiered child keys for agents in different trust contexts
description:
Original scope: tiered child API keys for agents in different trust contexts.

REFERENCED BY: picortex-ybz — picortex wants to mint per-chat child keys so each group chat the bot joins gets scoped wikihub access (namespace = chats/<chat_id>, read: public, write: chats/<chat_id>, no delete). picortex-ybz is blocked on this ticket landing.

Core API needed:
- POST /api/keys/derive — input: parent key + scope object {namespace, read[], write[], delete[], expires_in_days}. Output: child key string.
- DELETE /api/keys/<child_key_id> — revoke
- Every call made with a child key audit-logs the child_key_id + parent_key_id

This is still P1 because it unlocks the whole delegated-agent-access pattern, not just picortex.
notes:
## Refinement (Jacob, 2026-04-24): scope as first-class named object

Supersedes the embedded-scope-on-key design from the original description.

**Problem with embedded scopes:** if scope attributes live on the key row itself, rotating a key means re-specifying the entire scope. Multiple keys sharing the same policy (main DM key + group-chat key + backup key all scoped identically) means duplicating the spec. Auditing "what does this scope grant" means joining across keys.

**Refined design:** scopes are named objects; keys reference them.

### Data model

```
api_key_scopes            api_keys
─────────────             ─────────
id                        id
owner_user_id             user_id
name (unique per owner)   key_hash
max_visibility            scope_id FK → api_key_scopes.id (nullable; null = full)
wikis_allowlist JSON      parent_id FK → api_keys.id (nullable)
wikis_denylist JSON       label
read_only bool            expires_at
created_at                created_at
updated_at                last_used_at
```

Rotating a key: delete old, mint new with `scope_id: <same>`. Scope definition untouched. Other keys under the same scope unaffected.

### API

```
POST   /api/v1/key-scopes                 # define a new scope
  { name, max_visibility, wikis_allowlist, wikis_denylist, read_only }
GET    /api/v1/key-scopes                 # list your scopes
GET    /api/v1/key-scopes/<name>          # scope detail + list of keys using it
PATCH  /api/v1/key-scopes/<name>          # edit — applies to ALL keys using it
DELETE /api/v1/key-scopes/<name>          # fails if any keys still reference it, unless force=true (which revokes them all)

POST   /api/v1/keys/scoped                # mint a key against a scope
  { scope_name, label, expires_at }
  → returns wh_* once
```

Editing a scope changes what every key with that `scope_id` can do. That's a feature — one place to tighten or loosen a whole cohort of keys. Audit log entries reference `scope_id` and `scope_name_at_time` so after-the-fact investigation still works.

### Why different from groups (wikihub-ss96)

Both are "named referenceable objects" but they live on different axes and shouldn't be conflated:
- **Group** (ss96) = a set of **users** that a resource can be shared with. Belongs to the resource-ACL axis.
- **Scope** (this ticket) = a set of **policy attributes** that a key can reference. Belongs to the key-scope axis.

A scope named "read-only-public" is not a group of users. A group named "friends" is not a key policy. Keep vocabulary distinct.

### Naming

"Scope" (OAuth convention) > "role" (IAM — but too tied to RBAC semantics) > "policy" (AWS — too generic). Going with `key-scope` in URLs and "scope" in prose.

### Settings UI

Two sections under /settings:
1. **Key scopes** — CRUD list of named scopes. Each shows attributes summary + count of keys using it.
2. **Keys** — list of keys grouped by scope. Per row: label, scope name (link), expires, last-used, revoke.

Minting flow: pick existing scope OR create new scope inline, then give the new key a label + expiry.

### Migration from the original embedded design (not yet shipped, so cheap)

Since we haven't shipped embedded scopes, there's nothing to migrate. v1 ships referenced-from-day-1.

### Scope edit cascade semantics (open Q — propose)

When `PATCH /api/v1/key-scopes/<name>` tightens the scope (e.g., removes a wiki from `wikis_allowlist`), all existing keys using that scope immediately lose access. That's intentional.

When it loosens (adds a wiki), existing keys gain access. Also intentional — that's the point of named scopes.

Propose: no warning UI on tightening v1; just document the behavior. Add a "impact preview" ("this will affect N active keys, last used X minutes ago") if it becomes a footgun.

### MVP cut (what the first PR should ship)

- `api_key_scopes` table + migration
- `scope_id` column on `api_keys`
- `POST /api/v1/key-scopes` + `POST /api/v1/keys/scoped` wired end-to-end
- Enforcement in `get_current_user_from_request` + `can_read`/`can_write` (scope clamps down, never expands)
- Minimal settings UI: list scopes, list keys, revoke. Mint-via-form is acceptable but CLI-via-curl is fine for v1.
- Deferred to follow-ups: restricted-session banner, audit-log UI, impact preview, bulk operations
close_reason: MVP shipped across 4 PRs on local main (not yet pushed): 2d5d8ee (PR 1: schema + migration), 47fb7ce/similar (PR 2: scope CRUD API), 3c3d4eb (PR 3: mint + enforce + capabilities + cascade), 034f599 (PR 4: settings UI). 66/66 tests pass. Deferred items filed as wikihub-gzlt.1 through gzlt.6: banner, audit log view, edit impact preview, bulk ops, inline scope create, rename. Ready for Jacob to review the 4 commits, push to origin, apply 2 migrations to prod Lightsail (2026-04-24_api_key_scopes.sql + 2026-04-24_api_keys_expires_at.sql), and mint his first scoped key via /settings.

title: Restricted-session banner: visible indicator when a session is authenticated via a scoped key
description:
wikihub-gzlt spec requires a persistent amber banner across every page when the current session is authenticated via a scoped key. Reason: a human user should never confuse a scoped (restricted) session for their main session.

Triggered when: `request.current_scope is not None` AND the auth path is session-based (e.g. `/auth/login?api_key=<scoped-key>`, or any future flow that mints a session from a scoped key).

Banner contents:
- Chip / badge: 🔒 Restricted access
- Scope label + summary: "group-chat-friends · read-only · max public-view · 1 wiki"
- Color: amber/orange band (design system accent)
- Position: top of page chrome, above nav, persistent across navigation
- Dismissible? NO — it should stay visible for the whole session to avoid confusion

Also gate dangerous-looking UI affordances (delete buttons, visibility-change dropdowns) with the scope info — e.g., gray them out with a tooltip "this action is disabled in restricted sessions".

Blocked on: nothing in particular; the hook is straightforward via the context processor + a template partial.

title: Audit log view in /settings for scope + key lifecycle events
description:
Every scope CRUD, key mint, and key revoke should be visible in /settings as an audit trail. Today these events go to the DB but no UI surfaces them.

Scope: list + filter + paginate audit_log rows relevant to the current user — scope.created, scope.updated, scope.deleted, key.minted, key.revoked, key.cascade_revoked, key.expired (when an expired key is first rejected).

Each row: timestamp, event type, actor (user), affected resource (scope name / key label), relevant metadata (e.g., children_revoked count for cascade events).

Pairs with wikihub-gzlt. Useful for detecting "wait, who minted this key?" moments.

title: Edit-scope impact preview: show affected-key count before commit
description:
The PATCH /api/v1/key-scopes/<name> endpoint already applies edits immediately to all keys using the scope. Today the response includes a "note" field warning about this. The UI should go further:

Before committing a scope edit, show a confirmation dialog:
"This will affect N active keys. Tightening max_visibility from public-edit to public-view means those keys will lose access to public-edit pages they could previously see. Last use across these keys: 3m ago, 2h ago, 14d ago."

Options: Confirm / Cancel.

Also relevant: loosening (adding wikis to allowlist) is usually safe, tightening is the dangerous direction — the dialog should emphasize tightening.

Prerequisite: scope editing in the settings UI is NOT in PR 4 of wikihub-gzlt (only create/delete). This ticket implies adding edit-scope-in-UI too, or being triggered from a CLI-only edit.

title: Bulk operations on scoped keys: revoke-all-under-scope, rotate-all-under-scope
description:
Two bulk actions useful in key-rotation and incident response:

1. Revoke all keys under a scope: click "Revoke all" on a scope row → confirm count → POST to a batch endpoint → all keys with that scope_id deleted (cascading grandchildren, but there shouldn't be any by the no-grandchildren rule).

2. Rotate all keys under a scope: mint a new key per existing key (preserving labels) and revoke the old ones, atomically. Return the new raw keys once (in a downloadable table).

Probably also want a "Revoke all keys with parent X" cascade button (today only DELETE /api/v1/keys/<id> on parent does this; batch-form UI would help).

Out of scope for gzlt MVP (PR 4 ships one-by-one revoke only).

title: Inline scope creation from mint-scoped-key form
description:
Today the mint-scoped-key form in /settings requires selecting an existing scope. If the user hasn't created one yet, they must bounce to the Key scopes section, create it, then return.

Shorten: the mint form's scope select has a "+ Create new scope" option that expands an inline mini-form, creates the scope via the API, then immediately populates the dropdown with the new scope selected. Submit mints the key.

Small usability win. Not functional-critical.

title: Rename scope in place (API + UI)
description:
PATCH /api/v1/key-scopes/<name> intentionally does NOT rename (PR 2 decision) because audit-log events reference scope_name_at_time strings. Occasionally users want to rename.

Options:
1. Allow rename, and add scope_id references to audit events (not just name strings) so history stays linkable across renames.
2. Implement as a "clone + revoke old" — new scope name, all keys migrated to the new scope, old scope deleted. UI button "Rename" that does the atomic operation.

Recommend (1) — less churn, preserves audit continuity. But small migration work on audit_log if it's stringly typed today.

Low priority; nobody's asked.

title: Wikilink rewriting on page rename
description: Rename works but doesn't scan/rewrite [[old-path]] wikilinks. Silent link breakage = data integrity issue. Spec requires atomic rewrite of all referencing pages.
close_reason: Implemented rename/move wikilink rewriting and atomic repo updates in PATCH page handling.

title: Explore page says 'public projects' instead of 'public wikis'
description: The explore page header shows '11 public projects' but should say '11 public wikis'. WikiHub calls them wikis, not projects.
close_reason: Changed 'public projects' to 'public wiki/wikis' with proper pluralization in people.html

title: TOC anchor scroll offset: sticky nav covers heading target
description: when clicking a TOC link, the page scrolls to the heading but the sticky top nav (56px) covers it. need scroll-padding-top or scroll-margin-top on heading elements so the anchor target appears below the nav bar.

title: Connection system: cross-referencing patterns that prevent noise
description:
## Context

Cross-references are the whole point of a wiki over flat notes. But random/spurious connections make it worse than no connections. This issue defines a connection system that maximizes signal and minimizes noise.

Tested on a 115-page wiki — the patterns below emerged from what worked and what didn't.

## The problem

Agents generating wiki pages tend to either:
- Add zero connections (each page is an island)
- Add random connections based on keyword overlap ("both mention 'learning'" → spurious link)

Both are bad. We need inline wikilinks woven naturally into prose — like Wikipedia, not like a "see also" appendix.

## How connections work: inline wikilinks in prose

Links are woven into the text naturally as the page is written:

> "Harrison worked at [[orbit-internship|Orbit]] where [[mark-khrapko|Mark Khrapko]] mentored him on [[hiring-and-teams|hiring philosophy]] and he developed the [[cnn-spectrogram-analysis|CNN spectrogram classifier]]"

No dedicated "connections" section. If a relationship matters, it belongs in the prose. If it's not worth mentioning in prose, it's not a real connection. Backlinks (auto-generated by renderer) handle the reverse direction.

### Rules for inline linking

- **Link on first mention only** — don't link the same page multiple times in one section
- **Use display text** — `[[slug|Natural Text]]` so prose reads naturally
- **Don't over-link** — link when it adds navigational value, not every noun
- **Don't self-link** — a page never links to itself
- **Conservative matching** — better to miss a link than create a wrong one. "Mark" alone is ambiguous; "Mark Khrapko" is not.

Agents creating wiki pages should include inline links AS THEY WRITE, not as a separate pass. The schema/CLAUDE.md should specify this behavior.

## Cross-section connection taxonomy

Agents should specifically look for these high-value connection types:
- **person ↔ topic**: conversation pages link to topics discussed with that person
- **course ↔ topic**: academic material links to personal exploration of same concepts
- **topic ↔ topic**: thematic overlap between different knowledge areas
- **experience ↔ reflection**: what happened links to what was learned from it

Cross-section connections are almost always more valuable than within-section.

## Quality rules (anti-spam)

**MUST:**
- Pass the test: "would a reader learn something by following this link?"
- Reference specific shared content, not just shared keywords

**SHOULD:**
- Every page should have at least 2 outbound inline links (prevents orphans)
- Inline links are uncapped but governed by "first mention, don't over-link"

**MUST NOT:**
- Link based solely on keyword overlap without semantic relationship
- Create a dedicated "connections" or "see also" section — if it's not in the prose, it's not a connection
- Link every page in the same category to each other

## Lint-based link discovery

Automated agent pass for existing wikis that lack inline links:
1. Build alias map: page slug → matching text patterns (full name, course code, key phrase)
2. Scan prose for mentions of other pages
3. Replace first occurrence with `[[slug|matched text]]`
4. Conservative: skip ambiguous matches
5. Flag pages with 0-1 outbound links for review

## Renderer support

- **Inline links**: render `[[slug|Display Text]]` as clickable links showing only display text
- **Backlinks panel**: auto-generated list of pages that link TO this page — this replaces any need for a "connections" section
- Optional: mini knowledge graph showing 1-hop connections from current page

title: Landing page zip upload lost after signup redirect
description: User uploads a zip on the landing page, gets told it'll create after signin. They click signin, get redirected to signin (not signup), sign up instead, and after account creation the zip upload is gone — the wiki is never created. The upload state needs to survive the auth redirect flow.
close_reason: Files now stashed in IndexedDB before signup redirect. /new page restores them on load — slug, title, and file list auto-populated.

title: Anonymous upload: drop files without signup → ephemeral account + claim flow
description:
Today dropping files as an anonymous user stashes them in IndexedDB and forces signup at /auth/login?next=/new, then replays on the new-wiki form. That's friction: the user came to try the product, not fill a form.

Proposed flow:
1. Anonymous user drops .md/folder/.zip on the hero drop zone.
2. Backend creates an ephemeral account ('anon-<8hex>', no password) and an API key.
3. Creates a wiki under that account (default public or unlisted — TBD).
4. Processes the uploaded files using the existing _process_uploads path.
5. Responds JSON with {wiki_url, username, api_key, claim_hint}.
6. Frontend shows a prominent one-time banner: 'Your wiki is live at X. Save your API key wh_... to keep editing it, or claim this account with an email.' Then navigates to the wiki.

Open questions:
- Default visibility for anon wikis — public or unlisted?
- Rate limiting per IP (see wikihub-ub2.1)
- 'Claim this account by email' is a follow-up, out of scope for v1.

Minimal v1: new endpoint POST /new-anonymous (CSRF-exempt), landing JS uses it when not logged in, result banner shown in-page before nav.

title: Add people browsing and folder creation UX
description: Design and implement a way to browse users on the system, improve person/profile discoverability relative to wiki browsing, review sidebar universality, and add a simple UI affordance for creating folders. Update the spec and ship the first pass in product.
acceptance_criteria: Explore or adjacent navigation exposes people browsing; user profile clearly distinguishes personal wiki and other wikis; folder creation flow exists in web UI; spec is updated; e2e coverage added.
notes: Implement people browsing on Explore, strengthen profile as person page, add first-pass folder creation UI, update spec, and add e2e coverage.
close_reason: Implemented people browsing on /explore and /people, strengthened /@username as the canonical person page with personal wiki first, added folder creation UI via index.md scaffolding, updated the spec, and added e2e coverage.

title: Folder reader new-page flow is inconsistent with folder-scoped creation
description: In browser smoke testing, folder creation drops into notes/index edit and the obvious reader-side New page affordance is root-scoped rather than folder-scoped. Also /notes 404s while /notes/index is the canonical reader route. Tighten the folder UX so creating a folder and then creating a page inside it works through the obvious clicks without needing to know route details.

title: .well-known/ai-plugin.json and .well-known/mcp.json return 404
description: Both /.well-known/ai-plugin.json and /.well-known/mcp.json return 404 HTML pages. The /mcp endpoint exists and works (returns JSON tool list), so .well-known/mcp.json should at minimum redirect there or provide equivalent metadata.

title: Update @wikihub/wikihub system wiki to document the CLI
description:
The @wikihub/wikihub system wiki's 'Agent Integration' page still says 13 MCP tools and doesn't mention the CLI. Also 'Getting Started' should add the curl | sh one-liner.

Pages to update:
- /@wikihub/wikihub/Agent_Integration — add CLI section; fix 13→16 MCP tools while at it
- /@wikihub/wikihub/Getting_Started — add the curl | sh install line
- New page: /@wikihub/wikihub/CLI — full CLI reference (adapt from https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/jacobcole/tools/wikihub-cli)

Blocked on: @wikihub system-user API key. Currently known credentials only cover @jacobcole. Options:
1. Mint a new API key for the wikihub user via server-side admin script
2. Push directly via SSH to /opt/wikihub-app/repos/wikihub/wikihub.git
3. Add the CLI section via git clone/push using a harqian account token (jacobcole doesn't have write on @wikihub)

Dogfood page already live at /@jacobcole/jacobcole/tools/wikihub-cli — reuse the content.

title: Loose vertical rhythm on rendered wiki pages (line-height, p/h2 margins)
description:
Page URL: https://jacobcole.wikihub.md/picortex/docs/mockups/

The spacing on this page looks ugly. Need to investigate:
- What CSS rules control spacing on mockup/docs pages
- Whether images/mockups have incorrect margins or padding
- Whether list/heading spacing is off
- Compare with other wiki pages that look fine

Use Chrome DevTools MCP to inspect the rendered page non-invasively.
notes:
## Correction — original diagnosis was wrong
The initial investigation targeted the folder-index page at /picortex/docs/mockups/ (empty space below single card). The actual complaint is about vertical spacing **within rendered content pages** (paragraphs, table rows, list items, headings too far apart).

## Real diagnosis (measured on live page, template `main.content-wrap`)

| Element | Value | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| content-wrap line-height | 1.6 (25.6px/16px) | ok |
| p line-height | **1.75** (26.25px/15px) | too loose — use 1.5–1.6 |
| p margin-bottom | 18px | slightly loose |
| h2 margin-top | **40px** | very loose, creates big section gaps |
| h2 margin-bottom | 16px + 8px padding-bottom | | 
| li margin-bottom | 4px | ok |

Consecutive-paragraph gaps observed: 18, 100, 153, 203, 378 — the big ones are headings between stacking their 40px top margin plus their own height.

In tables (visible in the user's screenshot), `td` inherits the 1.75 line-height, so 2-line cells balloon to ~48-50px row height.

## Suggested fixes (cheapest first)
1. `.content-wrap { line-height: 1.55 }` (or target `p, li, td`) — biggest single win
2. `p { margin-bottom: 12px }` (from 18)
3. `h2 { margin-top: 28px }` (from 40); drop padding-bottom to 4px
4. `td { line-height: 1.45 }` + tighten td padding

## Where to look
Search the wikihub template/CSS for: `.content-wrap`, paragraph styles, table styles. The folder-index layout issue (my original diagnosis) is a separate, real bug but lower priority — could be split into its own ticket if desired.

title: Bulk + individual collaborator add (CLI + UI)
description:
Add teammates to a wiki by username, email, or in bulk. Covers:

**CLI**
- `wikihub-cli share add <wiki> <user|email> [<user|email>...] --role=read|edit` — accept multiple identifiers in one call
- `wikihub-cli share ls <wiki>` — list current grants
- `wikihub-cli share rm <wiki> <user|email> [...]` — bulk revoke

**Web UI**
- Collaborators section on wiki settings page (app/templates/wiki_settings.html)
- Multi-token input with autocomplete on username (and maybe display name)
- Paste-friendly: accept comma/newline-separated list of emails/usernames
- Inline role picker (read/edit) per grant
- Shows current grants with revoke button

**API**
- `POST /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/share/bulk` accepting `{grants: [{pattern, username|email, role}, ...]}`
- `GET /api/v1/users/search?q=<prefix>` for autocomplete (public usernames only)
- Existing single-grant endpoint stays for backward compat

**Open questions**
- Invite-by-email for users who don't have accounts yet? (Deferred — out of scope for v1; reject unknown emails with clear error.)
- Wiki-level grant vs path-pattern grant? (UI defaults to wiki-level pattern `**`; power users can still use CLI for patterns.)

Closes part of wikihub-fd4 (per-person sharing settings page).

title: MCP connector (standalone Python) + Claude-session-auto-wiki hook
description:
Build a standalone Python MCP server at ~/code/wikihub/mcp-server/ that mirrors the noos/mcp-server/ pattern. Replaces the hand-rolled Flask /mcp endpoint (app/routes/agent_surfaces.py:539-570) with a proper @modelcontextprotocol/sdk-compatible server.

Full design blueprint: docs/MCP_CONNECTOR_BLUEPRINT.md (written 2026-04-22 by a research subagent that mapped the codebase).

## Headline value props

1. Auto-build an LLM wiki from Claude chat history (analogous to noos portable-context-ingest skill, adapted to WikiHub page model)
2. Live SessionEnd hook — after each Claude session, a two-stage evaluator (YES/NO cheap filter → synthesis) decides whether to write a new wiki page

## Tool surface (10 tools)

Read: wikihub_search, wikihub_get_page, wikihub_list_pages
Write: wikihub_create_page, wikihub_update_page, wikihub_append_section, wikihub_delete_page
Meta: wikihub_list_wikis, wikihub_register_agent
Unique: wikihub_curate (wraps /agent/chat SSE → returns final text)

## Stages

- MVP: 9 tools + Starlette HTTP + systemd unit on Lightsail 54.145.123.7 + Cloudflare DNS mcp.wikihub.md
- v1.1: ~/.claude/hooks/wikihub-session-end.sh + SessionEnd hook entry
- v2: wikihub_curate SSE wrapper + portable-context-ingest skill split (facts→noos, narratives→wikihub) + post-write noos_relate cross-link

## Risks

- Cloudflare requires User-Agent: curl/8.0 (default in api.py layer)
- wikihub_curate nests SSE-in-SSE; test that path early
- Per-user Anthropic key required for /agent/chat (or server env)

## Sibling tickets

- noos-m5pt — duplicate in noos beads (close one once the work lives in the canonical repo; this one in wikihub beads is the canonical home)
- noos-q2e2 — portable-context-ingest skill (reusable synthesis logic)
- noos-uh2u — Phase 2 full chat export (similar ingestion mechanics)

title: Recurring sidebar indentation regression on nested items with visibility icons
description: The left-hand sidebar indentation is regressing again on nested content. In the reported case, child pages under a folder do not read as clearly nested, especially when visibility icons are present on the child rows. This needs a deeper audit of the layout invariants instead of another one-off pixel tweak. Scope: folder toggle width, child padding, icon alignment, active-row background, rows with trailing visibility icons, and any divergence between server-rendered and async sidebar markup. Treat this as a recurring regression in the same family as wikihub-58c and wikihub-58bd.

title: Import curated ListHub content to WikiHub
description: Import select ListHub lists as wikis on WikiHub. Good candidates: Body Masters, Autodidacts, Mental State Hacks, Startup Ideas, Bay Area Activities, Supplements. Put on @jacobcole account. Feature best ones in @wikihub community index.
close_reason: Imported 7 ListHub items to @jacobcole/curated-lists. Created Featured Wikis page in @wikihub community wiki.

title: WikiHub PKM client app (desktop + mobile)
description:
## Vision

Build a dedicated client app for WikiHub that turns it into a full personal knowledge management (PKM) system — like Obsidian but backed by WikiHub's hosted git repos.

## Platforms

- Desktop: Electron app (or native Swift for Mac)
- Mobile: iOS / Android
- Web: could be the first step (online editor/reader)

## Key Questions to Decide

1. **Build from scratch vs. extend existing?**
   - Build from scratch — most control, cleanest WikiHub integration, but most work
   - Fork/extend an open-source markdown editor (e.g., Marktext, Nota, Obsidian-like)
   - Integrate with Notestream (IdeaFlow's existing app)
   - Wrap the WikiHub web UI in Electron as a starting point

2. **Offline-first architecture?**
   - Local git clone of wiki repos + sync on connect
   - Conflict resolution strategy (last-write-wins? merge?)
   - How much works without internet?

3. **What differentiates this from Obsidian?**
   - WikiHub-native: publish, fork, star, discover — social PKM
   - Agent-native: MCP server means your AI assistant can maintain your wiki
   - Karpathy pattern built in: raw/, wiki/, schema structure
   - No plugin ecosystem needed — opinionated defaults

4. **MVP scope**
   - Online web editor might be the fastest first step
   - WikiHub already renders markdown — adding an edit mode could be v0.1
   - Desktop app (Electron) as v0.2
   - Mobile as v0.3

## Why

Using WikiHub as a PKM system requires a better editing experience than the current web form. A dedicated client would make it viable for daily use — quick capture, wikilink navigation, offline editing, keyboard shortcuts, graph view, etc.

title: Curate: MIT Entrepreneurship resources
description:
Build a curated resource list for MIT entrepreneurship. Candidates to include:

- MIT Sandbox Innovation Fund
- Martin Trust Center / delta v accelerator
- MIT $100K Entrepreneurship Competition
- MIT Media Lab spinout resources
- Engine (tough-tech fund)
- trust center NEW VENTURES FINDER
- TLO / licensing office
- Classes: 15.390 New Enterprises, Bill Aulet's Disciplined Entrepreneurship
- Alumni networks, VMS mentorship

Format: whatever format is chosen by the architecture-decision ticket. For now assume markdown at @curate/mit-entrepreneurship on wikihub.md.

Minimum viable list: 20 high-signal entries with 1-sentence description + link each.
notes: Blocked by the architecture-decision ticket — don't start writing until we know where it lives.

title: Wiki index pages rendered like Quartz — folder overview with page previews
description:
When you visit a wiki root or folder, the index page could show previews/excerpts of child pages — similar to how Quartz renders folder pages with cards showing page titles, excerpts, and metadata.

Currently wiki index pages are either a rendered index.md or a plain file listing. A Quartz-style index would automatically generate a richer overview with:
- Page title + first paragraph excerpt
- Last updated date
- Tags if present
- Maybe a card layout

This could be opt-in via frontmatter (layout: quartz-index) or the default when no index.md exists.

title: Build jacob-tejas-strategy llmwiki from Gemini meeting notes
description:
Build a Karpathy-style llmwiki from 4 Jacob/Tejas Strategy meetings (Mar 26–Apr 15, 2026) already pulled from Gemini-notes Gmail + Google Drive transcripts. Raw source files on disk at `scratch/jacob-tejas-strategy/raw/` (4 summaries + 4 full transcripts).

## Two proposed schemas

### Schema A — meetings-first (initial proposal)

```
raw/
  summaries/YYYY-MM-DD.md   ← Gemini email summaries (verbatim)
  transcripts/YYYY-MM-DD.md ← full Google Doc transcripts (verbatim)
topics/
  wikihub-vs-graph.md
  linear-adoption.md
  openclaw-roadmap.md
  payroll-issues.md
  action-items.md
index.md
```

Premise: one page per meeting (summary + transcript) as the raw layer; synthesized topic pages cross-link back with wikilinks; action-items aggregated.

### Schema B — threads-first (Codex revision, preferred)

```
index.md
wiki/
  now.md
  monthly/2026-04.md
  threads/
    file-vs-graph.md
    wikihub-to-nuos-transition.md
    velocity-vs-quality.md
    prioritization-and-founder-bandwidth.md
    linear-adoption-and-planning.md
    openclaw-onboarding-and-group-chat.md
    financial-pressure-and-payroll.md
log/
  meetings/YYYY-MM-DD.md   ← *delta* pages (cleaned, what changed this week)
  timeline.md
schema/
  decisions.md
  action-items.md
  entities.md
sources/
  summaries/   ← untouched imports
  transcripts/
```

Key refinements vs A:
- Durable unit is the **recurring thread**, not the meeting or the product.
- `sources/` (untouched artifacts) is separate from `log/` (cleaned chronological deltas).
- Meeting pages record only the delta on each thread. Full arguments live on canonical thread pages.
- Aggressive synthesis, conservative quoting. Verbatim only for load-bearing phrases ("wrong abstraction", "virality coefficient", "saying yes to everything").
- Load-bearing wikilinks only: meeting→threads, thread→products/people/decisions. Cap at 3–6 per meeting page.

## First pages to create (Schema B)

1. `index.md` — landing, active threads, latest monthly, latest decisions
2. `wiki/monthly/2026-04.md` — 2-minute founder skim
3. `wiki/threads/file-vs-graph.md` — core architectural tension
4. `wiki/threads/wikihub-to-nuos-transition.md` — 24+36hr plan, whether it holds
5. `wiki/threads/velocity-vs-quality.md` — Apr 8 tension
6. `wiki/threads/prioritization-and-founder-bandwidth.md` — the "saying yes to everything" pattern
7. `wiki/threads/linear-adoption-and-planning.md` — process change
8. `wiki/threads/openclaw-onboarding-and-group-chat.md` — Telegram flows
9. `wiki/threads/financial-pressure-and-payroll.md` — Mar 26 payroll discussion
10. `schema/decisions.md` + `schema/action-items.md`

## Target

Publish as `wikihub.md/@jacobcole/jacob-tejas-strategy` once scaffolded locally.

## Open question

Whether to have Codex generate first-pass scaffolds with starter frontmatter + section templates for the 8 core pages, or hand-build.

title: Make folder-first organization the default path in UX and docs
description:
## Motivation

WikiHub supports nested paths (`folder/page.md`) but doesn't surface folder-first organization as the default anywhere. Result: agents and users over-create wikis when a folder would do.

Paired with Noos philosophical reference: will link once created.

## Action items

- [ ] AGENTS.md: add a "when to create a new wiki vs. folder vs. page" heuristic. Default: page. Reach for folder when a concept accretes multiple pages. Reach for a new wiki only when the content has its own lifecycle (forkable, separate ACL, distinct identity).
- [ ] `/llms.txt` and `/AGENTS.md` route: echo the heuristic so connected agents internalize it on first contact.
- [ ] Landing page / create-wiki UX: copy nudge — "If you're adding a section to existing content, consider creating a page or folder first."
- [ ] Create-page UX: autocomplete existing folder paths; show a small tree of where you are.
- [ ] Verify page tree sidebar renders cleanly with 2–3 levels of nesting.

## Related

- wikihub-aihm — the original over-creation observation
- Concrete mistake: /@jacobcole/asks (pending migration decision)

title: Web new-page route allows anonymous creation in private wikis
description:
Workflow audit finding from 2026-04-22.

`new_page()` on the web route lacks the owner/collaborator write gate present in `edit_page()`. Local test-client checks showed anonymous GET and POST to `/@owner/privatewiki/new` succeeded, allowing creation of a private page.

Likely fix:
- apply the same `can_write` / ACL gate used by `edit_page()` before rendering the form or saving content
- add an e2e regression that proves anonymous users cannot create pages in private wikis via the web flow

Evidence:
- app/routes/wiki.py around edit_page vs new_page
- workflow audit report /tmp/wikihub-workflow-audit.md
notes: Claimed from workflow audit follow-through on 2026-04-22. Implementing route guard + regression coverage.

title: Consistent footer across homepage and app pages
Profile says 1 wiki but lists none
description: After signup, the profile page shows '1 wiki' (the auto-provisioned personal wiki) but the wiki list below is empty — contradictory. Either show the personal wiki in the list or say 0 wikis.
close_reason: Fixed: hide empty wiki list for non-owners, removed 'project wikis' wording. Profile no longer shows contradictory count vs empty list.

title: Brainstorm: Cmd+K create with Karpathy LLM wiki standard — where do new pages go?
Review and triage spec/code audit findings (2026-04-10)
description: Full spec/code audit conducted 2026-04-10 by parallel Claude + Codex review. Found 44 issues across 4 severity tiers. Spec contradictions (category 1) already fixed in-session. Remaining: 4 critical code bugs, 10 high-priority gaps, ~20 medium missing features, ~10 low nits. Full findings in docs/audit-2026-04-10.md. This ticket: review the audit, create individual tickets for items worth tracking separately, and prioritize the critical 4 for immediate fix.
notes: Implementing the 2026-04-10 audit list end-to-end, starting with critical items and verifying with tests after each logical batch.
close_reason: Implemented the 2026-04-10 audit list and updated the checklist in docs/audit-2026-04-10.md.

title: Epic: Auth & account completeness (Google-Drive readiness)
description:
# WikiHub auth & account design — "Google Drive readiness"

Status: draft, sending to Codex for review.

## Current state (verified from code, 2026-04-21)

User model fields: `id, username, display_name, email (unique, nullable), password_hash (nullable), google_id (unique, nullable), llm_api_key_encrypted, created_at`. **No `email_verified_at`.**

Auth surfaces that exist:
- `/auth/signup` — username + password (+ optional unverified email)
- `/auth/login` — username/password OR `?api_key=`
- `/auth/magic/<token>` — single-use link, 15-min TTL, requires caller to already hold an API key or password to mint
- `/auth/google` + `/auth/google/callback` — Google OAuth
- `POST /api/v1/accounts` — admin/API account creation

Gaps:
1. No forgot-password / password-reset flow
2. No email verification (email field is unique + nullable + unverified)
3. No "change password" UI in /settings
4. No "link Google" / "unlink Google" in /settings
5. No delete-account flow
6. No account merge for duplicate sign-ups

## Latent security issue: Google auto-link by unverified email

Current `google_callback()`:
```python
user = User.query.filter_by(google_id=google_id).first()
if not user and email:
    user = User.query.filter_by(email=email).first()
    if user:
        user.google_id = google_id  # <-- links without verification
```

**Takeover path**: Alice signs up with password, claims email=`victim@gmail.com` (unverified — no confirmation ever sent). Later Victim hits "Sign in with Google", their real Google email is `victim@gmail.com`. Server finds Alice's User by email, binds `google_id=<Victim>` to Alice's account. Victim now logs in and lands in *Alice's* account. Alice retains her password + API key, so she sees whatever Victim creates after. This is exploitable as long as email goes unverified.

**Fix**: never auto-link by email unless the email on the matching account is verified. If match exists but isn't verified, force the Google user down the new-account path (or prompt them to explicitly claim the email via verification).

## Proposed design

### A. Email verification (foundation for everything else)

Schema:
- Add `email_verified_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE NULL`
- Change email uniqueness: partial unique index `WHERE email_verified_at IS NOT NULL`. Keep email column otherwise. This lets two unrelated users both claim `foo@bar.com` as unverified (first to verify wins), instead of first-to-type.
- New table `EmailVerificationToken(id, user_id, new_email, token_hash, expires_at, used_at)`. We store `new_email` on the token, not on User, so the "current" email doesn't change until verification. TTL 24h.

Flows:
- Signup with email → create User with `email` set and `email_verified_at=null`. Mint verification token, email the link. User can log in immediately; UI shows "Verify your email" banner.
- Settings → "Change email" → mint verification token on new address; verification swaps email + sets verified timestamp.
- Google OAuth → Google-provided email is treated as verified iff Google's token `email_verified=true` (Google provides this). Set `email_verified_at=now()` when we create the account from Google.

### B. Password reset

Schema: `PasswordResetToken(id, user_id, token_hash, expires_at, used_at)`. TTL 30 min. Single use.

Flow:
- `POST /auth/forgot` with `{email}`. Always respond 204 (don't leak existence).
  - If a verified email matches a user with `password_hash` set, mint token + send email with reset link.
  - If matched user has no password (Google-only), email them saying "your account uses Google Sign-In; click here to sign in with Google" instead.
  - If no match, do nothing. Still respond 204.
- `GET /auth/reset/<token>` → form, new password + confirm.
